name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that aggregates featured product categories, featured machines, upcoming events, and financing highlights, and provides quick navigation to key tools such as products, quotes, projects, support, distributor locator, events, and newsletter signup.

core_features: Display featured product categories using getHomeFeaturedCategories and provide direct navigation to the products listing filtered by the selected category.
Display a curated list of featured machines using getHomeFeaturedProducts and provide direct navigation to individual product detail pages.
Display a highlight of upcoming events using getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight, including a primary event with deeper navigation and access to a full events list.
Display a financing teaser using getHomeFinancingHighlight with links to the dedicated financing page.
Provide quick-access navigation shortcuts to the quote list, project list, support, distributor locator, and newsletter signup from the homepage.
user_workflows:
Explore product categories from Home:
1. User lands on Home (index.html) without required URL parameters.
2. Page calls getHomeFeaturedCategories and renders category items in the category navigation component.
3. User clicks a category item.
4. Browser navigates to products.html with URL parameter categoryId set to the clicked category's code (e.g., products.html?categoryId={code}).
5. Products page reads categoryId and filters products accordingly.

Explore featured machines from Home:
1. User lands on Home; page calls getHomeFeaturedProducts and renders each machine as a clickable card.
2. User reviews machine information provided by the interface (name, category, descriptions, status, pricing if available, badges).
3. User clicks a featured machine card.
4. Browser navigates to product.html with URL parameter id set to the clicked machine's product_id (product.html?id={product_id}).
5. Product page uses the id parameter to load full product details and allow quote/project actions there.

Discover upcoming events and trade shows from Home:
1. User scrolls to the events highlight section; page has already called getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight and rendered the primary and secondary events.
2. User clicks the primary event card.
3. Browser navigates to event_detail.html with URL parameter eventId set to the primary_event.event_id (event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}).
4. Alternatively, user clicks the "view all events" action in the events highlight component.
5. Browser navigates to events.html with URL parameter status set to upcoming (events.html?status=upcoming).

Learn about financing from Home:
1. User scrolls to the financing highlight section; page has already called getHomeFinancingHighlight and rendered the headline, subheadline, highlight text, and any featured plans.
2. User optionally interacts with the plans (e.g., selecting a plan card) for more details within the section, based on the data provided.
3. User clicks a financing call-to-action such as a generic "learn more" or "estimate payments" action.
4. Browser navigates to financing.html without parameters (financing.html). Downstream financing tools then handle plan selection and calculations.

Access operational tools (quotes, projects, support, distributor locator, newsletter) from Home:
1. User uses the header shortcut strip to click the quote list shortcut.
2. Browser navigates to quotes.html without parameters (quotes.html).
3. User uses the same strip to click the project list shortcut.
4. Browser navigates to projects.html (projects.html).
5. User scrolls to the support and distributor ribbon and clicks the support link block.
6. Browser navigates to support.html (support.html).
7. User clicks the "Where to buy" navigation element in the same ribbon.
8. Browser navigates to distributors.html (distributors.html).
9. User scrolls to the footer newsletter call-to-action and clicks it.
10. Browser navigates to newsletter.html (newsletter.html).
interactions: Category selection: When the user clicks on a category tile or shortcut in the category navigation component, an onCategoryClick handler reads the selected category's code from the getHomeFeaturedCategories result and navigates to products.html?categoryId={code}.

Products shortcut: When the user clicks a general products shortcut (if present) in the category navigation or header, an onProductsShortcutClick handler navigates to products.html with categoryId set to a value determined server-side (for example a special all-products category code), using only values provided by getHomeFeaturedCategories.

Featured product click: When the user clicks a featured product card, an onFeaturedProductClick handler reads product_id from the getHomeFeaturedProducts result and navigates to product.html?id={product_id}.

Event primary card click: When the user clicks on the primary event card in the events highlight, an onPrimaryEventClick handler reads primary_event.event_id from getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight and navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}.
View all events click: When the user clicks the "view all events" action in the events highlight, an onViewAllEventsClick handler navigates to events.html?status=upcoming.
Financing call-to-action: When the user clicks a financing CTA inside the financing highlight component, an onFinancingCtaClick handler navigates to financing.html.
Quote list shortcut: When the user clicks the quote list shortcut in the header strip, an onQuoteShortcutClick handler navigates to quotes.html.
Project list / plan my arcade shortcut: When the user clicks the project-related shortcut in the header strip, an onProjectShortcutClick handler navigates to projects.html.
Support quick link: When the user clicks the support block in the support and distributor ribbon, an onSupportClick handler navigates to support.html.
Distributor locator quick link: When the user clicks the distributor / where-to-buy block in the support and distributor ribbon, an onDistributorClick handler navigates to distributors.html.
Newsletter signup CTA: When the user clicks the newsletter signup CTA in the footer, an onNewsletterClick handler navigates to newsletter.html.

Section focusing via URL parameter: On page load, a general onPageLoad handler reads optional URL parameters such as focus and highlightProductId. If focus is present, it scrolls to the corresponding component (e.g., products, events, financing) without additional API calls. If highlightProductId is present, the featured products grid component uses it to emphasize the matching product card if it exists in the getHomeFeaturedProducts result.

Event highlighting via URL parameter: On page load, the events highlight component reads an optional eventId URL parameter. If this matches either the primary event or a secondary event returned by getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight, the component visually emphasizes that event and may scroll it into view, without changing navigation behavior or making additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require any parameters for its core data loading; all data is retrieved by calling getHomeFeaturedCategories, getHomeFeaturedProducts, getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight, and getHomeFinancingHighlight without input parameters. Optional URL parameters are used strictly for client-side state and navigation behavior, not for modifying backend queries. Example URL parameters and effects: 1) focus: When present (e.g., index.html?focus=events), the page's onPageLoad logic scrolls to the matching section component (such as the events highlight, financing highlight, or featured products section) and may set an internal view state flag that marks that section as initially expanded or emphasized. No API calls change based on this parameter. 2) highlightProductId: When present (e.g., index.html?highlightProductId={product_id}), the featured products grid compares the given value against the product_id values in the getHomeFeaturedProducts result. If a match is found, it sets a transient UI state to emphasize that product card and optionally scrolls it into view. If no match is found, no special emphasis is applied. 3) eventId: When present (e.g., index.html?eventId={event_id}), the events highlight component compares this value against primary_event.event_id and each secondary_events[i].event_id from getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight. If a match is found, that event is emphasized or scrolled into view. These URL-based states are not persisted beyond the current page load and do not rely on localStorage; they are recomputed purely from the current URL and the interface responses.

components.functionality: Provides immediate access from the homepage to the quote list and project list pages via shortcut links located near the top of the page.
Loads featured product categories via getHomeFeaturedCategories and renders them as clickable items that navigate to the products listing filtered by the selected category.

Retrieves a curated list of featured products using getHomeFeaturedProducts and displays them as interactive cards, each leading to its product detail page. Applies optional highlighting based on the highlightProductId URL parameter.

Uses getHomeUpcomingEventsHighlight to display a primary upcoming event with detailed summary information and a list of additional upcoming events. Provides navigation to the primary event detail page and to the full events listing filtered to upcoming events. Optionally emphasizes a specific event based on the eventId URL parameter.

Calls getHomeFinancingHighlight to display financing teaser content and any featured financing plans. Provides a generic call-to-action that navigates users to the full financing page for detailed calculators and inquiry forms.
Provides quick access blocks for support resources and the distributor locator from the homepage, without requiring any data interfaces.
Displays a persistent call-to-action at the bottom of the page that routes the user to the dedicated newsletter signup page.

On initial page load, parses URL parameters such as focus, highlightProductId, and eventId, and passes the relevant state flags or identifiers to child components (featured products grid and events highlight) to control scrolling and emphasis. Does not make any API calls and does not store state in localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom sequence of full-width sections, mirroring the mockup: global dark double header (info bar + main nav, assumed global) → a shallow light strip acting as a shortcut/navigation bar → a wide white main canvas with a centered hero-like category/navigation section → a three-column featured products row in the same pattern as the blog cards → vertically stacked event and financing highlight sections → a bold, full-width support/distributor ribbon → a quieter newsletter CTA just above the footer. A single floating circular control sits at the bottom-right, detached from the main content stream.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom sequence of full-width sections, mirroring the mockup: global dark double header (info bar + main nav, assumed global) → a shallow light strip acting as a shortcut/navigation bar → a wide white main canvas with a centered hero-like category/navigation section → a three-column featured products row in the same pattern as the blog cards → vertically stacked event and financing highlight sections → a bold, full-width support/distributor ribbon → a quieter newsletter CTA just above the footer. A single floating circular control sits at the bottom-right, detached from the main content stream.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and global nav occupy the top 10–15% of the viewport; the shortcut strip is relatively shallow. The hero/category section takes roughly the upper half of the remaining first viewport height, giving the page title, intro, and category navigation plenty of breathing room. The three-column featured products grid consumes most of the next viewport slice, with generous top and bottom padding. Events and financing highlights each occupy compact, clearly separated bands. The support/distributor ribbon and newsletter CTA share the lower part of the page, with ample vertical spacing. Vertical gaps between major sections follow the 48–64px rhythm, and all content is constrained to a centered 1140–1200px max-width grid within full-width background bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, highly scannable layout: few elements per section, large vertical padding, clear grouping. The hero/category block focuses on a single title, one intro paragraph, and a limited set of prominent category links. The featured products grid mirrors the blog layout with three cards and plenty of whitespace. Events and financing sections each highlight just one or a very small number of items with concise text. Navigation ribbons and CTAs use large, tappable targets with internal padding around 16–24px, avoiding cramped clusters.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention flows from the logo and main nav in the dark header down into the light shortcut strip (quick tools), then to the centered hero headline and intro, and directly into the horizontal category navigation. From there, the eye descends to the three bright featured product cards laid out left-to-right, then continues downward to the events highlight, financing highlight, and finally the support/distributor ribbon and newsletter CTA near the footer. A neon-lime floating state controller at the bottom-right serves as a persistent, secondary focal point that the eye naturally discovers after scanning the main content.

layout.overall_description: Below the global dark double header, a slim, light-colored shortcut strip stretches full width, holding compact links to key tools and echoing the breadcrumb band from the mockup. Beneath it, the background shifts to a clean white canvas where the main content lives inside a centered 1140–1200px container aligned to an invisible 12-column grid. At the top of this canvas, a spacious hero section presents the homepage title and a short paragraph, both center-aligned and surrounded by generous vertical whitespace; just below, a horizontal set of category tiles or pills allows quick navigation into major product families. Further down, a three-column row of featured product cards repeats the mockup’s blog layout: equal-width cards with small thumbnails and neon-lime product names, separated by wide gutters and ample breathing room above and below. As users scroll, a compact events highlight band and a slightly more emphasized financing section appear in sequence, each occupying its own visually distinct but still airy horizontal slice. Near the bottom, a darker, more saturated support-and-distributor ribbon cuts across the full width, drawing attention to two prominent help-related CTAs, followed by a softer newsletter signup area that sits on a light background with a centered, padded card. Throughout, a circular neon-lime floating control hovers above the content at the bottom-right corner of the viewport, maintaining a subtle but persistent invitation to interact with advanced homepage tools or state controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-shortcut-strip sits immediately below the global dark header and primary navigation, acting as the light, shallow band in the mockup’s breadcrumb position. It spans the full browser width with a very light gray or off-white background that clearly contrasts with the dark header above and the white main canvas below. Within this band, content is constrained to a centered 1140–1200px max-width container aligned to the 12-column grid. The strip’s height is modest—roughly 56–72px total—with vertical padding of about 12–16px, keeping it visually slim but comfortably touchable. Inside, a horizontal row of shortcut items (Products, Quotes, Projects, Support, Distributor Locator, Events, Newsletter) runs left-to-right, using small icons plus labels or pill-shaped buttons. On desktop, these shortcuts are evenly spaced or grouped with clear gaps between clusters; on tablet and mobile, they can wrap to a second row or become a horizontally scrollable strip while maintaining at least 16px left/right page padding. The strip has a clear 48–64px vertical gap below it before the main hero/category section begins, preserving the airy separation seen between breadcrumb and main content in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section that scrolls with the page; not fixed or sticky, but visually anchored between the global header and the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-category-navigation occupies the first major block of the white main canvas and functions like a hero plus primary navigation hub. It sits directly beneath the header-shortcut-strip, separated by a generous 48–64px white vertical gap that visually resets the page. The section spans the full width, but all content is centered within the 1140–1200px container following a 12-column grid. At the top of the section, a large, center-aligned page title (e.g., 'Amusement Machines & Solutions') stretches across roughly 6–8 grid columns centered on the page, with ample white space on either side. There is a 16–24px gap below the title, followed by a single, center-aligned introductory paragraph limited to about 60–70% of the container width to keep line lengths comfortable. Below the intro, after another 32–40px of vertical spacing, the category navigation itself appears: a single row of 4–6 category tiles or pill buttons aligned horizontally across the grid. Each tile occupies about 2–3 columns on desktop, with wide gutters (16–24px) between them, and uses internal padding around 16–24px to feel substantial yet airy. Category labels and icons lean on neon-lime or cyan accent lines, borders, or text to echo the playful tech character from the header. On tablet, categories wrap into two rows while still visually grouped; on mobile, they stack in a single column centered with full-width or near-full-width pills, maintaining at least 16px horizontal padding to the viewport edge. The entire hero/category section is cushioned with about 64px padding above its top-most title text and 64px below the bottom row of category tiles before the featured products grid begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block section inside the main content column; scrolls normally and defines the primary initial focal area of the homepage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-featured-products-grid is placed directly below the home-category-navigation section, separated by a clear 48–64px vertical gap of white space that visually marks it as the next content block. It uses the same full-width white background as the main canvas, with content centered in the 1140–1200px grid container. At the top of this section, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., 'Featured Machines') sits near the left edge of the inner container, spanning roughly 4–6 grid columns, with a short supporting line of text just beneath it or to the right within the same width. This heading block has 24–32px of vertical space beneath it. Below, a single row of three equally sized product cards mirrors the mockup’s three blog items: each card takes up about one third of the inner container width (4 grid columns each) with generous gutters (24–32px) between cards. Each card includes a small-to-medium image thumbnail at the top aligned with the card’s width, followed by 16–24px of internal vertical padding, then a neon-lime product title text line (one or two lines max), optionally followed by a brief, muted description line. The cards rest on a white or slightly elevated surface with subtle shadows or thin borders, but they are spaced so that there is significant whitespace above the row, between the cards, and below the row (48–64px padding at the bottom of the section). On smaller screens, the grid collapses first to two columns (tablet) and then to one column (mobile), but the vertical spacing remains generous to maintain the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard grid-based content section; scrolls normally, occupying a prominent mid-page band similar in visual weight to the mockup’s blog row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-events-highlight section appears below the featured products grid, separated from it by approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace. It occupies a distinct band that may use a very light tinted background (e.g., a subtle cool gray or ultra-light cyan) to gently differentiate it from the primary white canvas without feeling heavy. Content remains within the centered 1140–1200px container. At the top-left of this band, a concise heading such as 'Upcoming Events' anchors the section, spanning about 4–5 grid columns. Below or beside the heading, a brief one- or two-line summary explains the events focus, maintaining short line lengths. To the right, occupying the remaining width, one or two event highlight cards are laid out horizontally on desktop: each card covers about 4–5 grid columns, with ample horizontal gutters (24px) and internal padding (16–24px). The cards contain a date badge, event name (possibly accented with lime/cyan text or underline), and a short detail line, all arranged with clear vertical spacing between elements so they feel light and easy to scan. The overall section height is moderate—roughly 30–40% of the viewport height on desktop—so it feels like a concise summary rather than a full list. On smaller screens, event cards stack vertically underneath the heading with consistent 24–32px gaps. Generous top and bottom padding (around 40–56px each) keep the section airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard horizontal band with subtle background differentiation; scrolls normally with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-financing-highlight section sits directly below the events highlight, again separated by approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace to maintain clear sectional hierarchy. This band can lean slightly more into the neon-lime/cyan tech character by using a soft, pale gradient or a very light tinted background that hints at these colors while keeping text legible and the overall look corporate. Content is centered in the 1140–1200px grid. The layout is horizontally divided: on the left, spanning about 6–7 grid columns, a strong heading like 'Flexible Financing for Every Project' appears with a short explanatory paragraph beneath it; on the right, spanning 5–6 grid columns, sits a single, more prominent financing call-to-action block—such as a card showing a key financing offer or link to a calculator. This right-hand card includes generous internal padding (24–32px), a clean border or subtle elevation, and a primary CTA button with neon accent styling. Vertical padding at the section level (top and bottom) is around 48–64px, making the band feel substantial but not overwhelming. On tablet and mobile, the right-hand card stacks below the left-hand copy, centered and full-width, with a 32–40px gap between them. Despite its informational density, spacing between text lines and elements remains loose to preserve the low-density, premium feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard content band with slightly more visual emphasis through background tint; scrolls with the page like other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-and-distributor-ribbon serves as a bold, action-oriented band near the bottom of the page, positioned below the financing highlight with about 48–64px of vertical spacing. It runs full width and uses a darker or more saturated background—often a deep navy or charcoal with neon-lime and cyan accent lines—visually echoing the dark header and creating a strong call-to-action zone. Within this band, the 1140–1200px content container is centered. The layout is a simple left-right split: on the left half (approximately 6 grid columns), a concise heading such as 'Need Support or a Local Distributor?' and a short supporting line are set in high-contrast text; on the right half, two large, horizontally aligned CTA buttons or tiles ('Support Center' and 'Distributor Locator') sit side by side, each taking about 3 grid columns with a clear gutter between them. These CTAs are visually substantial, with 16–24px internal padding, pill or rounded-rect shapes, and strong neon-lime/cyan accents. The ribbon’s overall height is about 96–128px, with enough vertical padding to feel comfortable but still compact compared to hero sections. On smaller screens, the CTAs stack below the heading in a single column, each full-width with vertical spacing of 16–24px. This section visually precedes the softer newsletter CTA and acts as a clear transitional band into the page’s footer region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width, visually emphatic ribbon that scrolls normally; not sticky, but designed to stand out due to color contrast and centralized CTAs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-newsletter-cta sits in the final content band before the site’s global footer, directly below the support-and-distributor-ribbon with a 48–64px vertical gap to reset the intensity of the previous dark band. The background returns to a light gray or white tone, creating a calm zone. Centered within the 1140–1200px container is a single, prominent newsletter sign-up card, typically a white rounded rectangle or softly elevated panel that spans about 60–70% of the container width on desktop and is horizontally centered. This card has generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and is arranged either as a vertical stack (heading → short description → email input + button) or as a two-column layout where the left side holds the heading and description and the right side holds the email field and submit button. The heading is center- or left-aligned, and the form controls are sized for comfortable interaction, with clear 16–24px gaps between fields and buttons. There is ample whitespace both above and below the card (around 40–56px) within the section, ensuring it feels like a soft, inviting endpoint of the page rather than a crowded footer add-on. On mobile, the card becomes nearly full-width with 16–20px margin to the viewport edges and maintains the same vertical spacing between its internal elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard pre-footer call-to-action that scrolls with the page; visually centered and self-contained within a lighter background band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-page-state-controller is manifested as a circular, floating action button that overlays the main content at the bottom-right of the viewport, closely following the floating chat button pattern from the mockup. It does not occupy space in the normal layout flow; instead, it hovers above all sections with a consistent offset of about 24–32px from the right and bottom edges of the viewport. The button is small but visually strong, with a diameter around 56–64px, a solid neon-lime fill or ring, a contrasting icon (such as a control, filter, or quick-tools icon) in the center, and a soft shadow to separate it from the content below. It remains visible as the user scrolls the page, subtly drawing the eye as a persistent secondary action without obstructing key content. On mobile devices, it may sit slightly higher (e.g., 72–88px above the bottom edge) to avoid overlapping system UI or bottom navigation, but it always anchors to the right. Any label or expanded panel triggered by this button would open above or to the left of it, but in its default state it stays compact. The surrounding content maintains normal margins and is not pushed aside by this floating element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position floating control anchored to the bottom-right corner of the viewport, overlaying all scrolling content with a consistent offset; always visible while the user is on the homepage.

name: Product Listing / Category
title: Products
description: Category and search results page that lists amusement machines, with filtering, sorting, and options to add machines to a quote list or project list.

core_features: Load and display category information based on the categoryId URL parameter using getCategoryDetails.
Load available filter options (players, power/voltage, width, price, sort) for the current category using getProductFilterOptions and expose them as interactive controls.
Load a paginated, filtered, and sorted list of products for the current category using getCategoryProducts, reflecting all URL parameters (categoryId, q, filters, sort, page, pageSize).
Allow users to refine product results by adjusting filters (players, power/voltage, width range, price range) and sorting, updating the URL and reloading results using getCategoryProducts.
Allow users to search within the current category (or across all products) using a search input bound to the q URL parameter and propagated as a filter into getCategoryProducts.
Allow users to open an individual product detail page by clicking on a product card, navigating to product.html with id and categoryId URL parameters.
Allow users to add products from the listing to a quote list using addProductToQuoteList, showing feedback and enabling navigation to quotes.html.
Allow users to add products from the listing to a project/wish list using addProductToProject, showing feedback and enabling navigation to projects.html.
Provide pagination controls so users can navigate through multiple pages of products, updating the URL page parameter and refreshing data via getCategoryProducts.
user_workflows:
Browse a category:
1) User navigates to products.html with a categoryId URL parameter.
2) The page reads categoryId, calls getCategoryDetails to load the category header, getProductFilterOptions to prepare filters, and getCategoryProducts with default filters and sort for page 1.
3) The product grid displays the products returned by getCategoryProducts.
4) User can click any product image or name to navigate to product.html with id and categoryId parameters.

Search within products:
1) User types a query into the search input and submits.
2) The page updates the q parameter in the URL (and resets page to 1), then calls getCategoryProducts with filters that include the search term derived from q.
3) Product grid refreshes with the new results list.
4) User can modify filters or sort while the search term remains in the URL and continues to affect results.

Filter and sort products:
1) User adjusts filters (e.g., players, power/voltage, width range, price range) or changes sort order using filter and sort controls.
2) Each change updates the corresponding filter and sort URL parameters and resets page to 1.
3) The page controller calls getCategoryProducts with categoryCode from categoryId and filters derived from URL parameters.
4) Product grid updates with the filtered and sorted list; the filter sidebar reflects current selections based on URL state.

Paginate through results:
1) User clicks a pagination control to move to another page (next, previous, or a specific page number).
2) The page updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving categoryId, q, filters, sort, and pageSize.
3) The page controller calls getCategoryProducts with the new page value.
4) Product grid refreshes to show products for the selected page.

Add a product to a quote list from the listing:
1) User locates a product card that is available for quote (is_available_for_quote from getCategoryProducts item).
2) User enters a quantity (or uses a default) and clicks an 'Add to quote list' action on the product card.
3) The product card triggers addProductToQuoteList with productId and quantity.
4) On success, the response (including quote_list_id, total_items, total_estimated_value, and currency) updates the quote status component, which becomes visible or refreshed.
5) User can click a 'View quote list' action to navigate to quotes.html.

Add a product to a project/wish list from the listing:
1) User locates a product card that is available for project (is_available_for_project from getCategoryProducts item).
2) User enters a quantity and optional notes, then triggers an 'Add to project' action.
3) The product card triggers addProductToProject with productId, quantity, and notes.
4) On success, the response (including project_id, total_machines, total_estimated_width_cm) updates the project status component.
5) User can click a 'View project list' action to navigate to projects.html.
interactions: Selecting a category via navigation to products.html with a categoryId URL parameter, which triggers initial loading via getCategoryDetails, getProductFilterOptions, and getCategoryProducts.
Typing into the search input and submitting, which updates the q URL parameter and triggers a refresh of the product list via getCategoryProducts.
Adjusting filters (players, power/voltage, width range, price range) in the filter sidebar, which updates filter-related URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers getCategoryProducts.
Changing the sort option in the sort control, which updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers getCategoryProducts.
Changing the page size (if exposed) by editing a page size control, which updates the pageSize URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers getCategoryProducts.
Clicking on a product card, image, or name, which opens product.html with id set to the product_id of the clicked item and categoryId set to the currently active categoryCode.
Clicking 'Add to quote list' on a product card, which triggers addProductToQuoteList with productId and quantity and updates on-page quote status based on the response.
Clicking 'Add to project' on a product card, which triggers addProductToProject with productId, quantity, and notes and updates on-page project status based on the response.
Clicking 'View quote list' in the quote status component, which navigates to quotes.html.
Clicking 'View project list' in the project status component, which navigates to projects.html.
Using pagination controls (next/previous or specific page number buttons), which update the page URL parameter and trigger getCategoryProducts.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses. The primary URL parameters are: categoryId (required), q (optional search term), and additional filter/sort/pagination parameters defined for this page. On initial load or any URL change, the page reads the following parameters: categoryId (mapped directly to categoryCode for API calls), q (mapped into filters.searchQuery for getCategoryProducts), players, powerVoltage, widthMin, widthMax, priceMin, priceMax, sort, page, and pageSize (all optional). The page then performs these steps: (1) Call getCategoryDetails with categoryCode equal to categoryId to get the category header information. (2) Call getProductFilterOptions with categoryCode equal to categoryId to get dynamic ranges and option lists for filters; these responses define the allowable values and ranges for players, power_voltages, width_cm, price, and sort_options. (3) Construct a filters object for getCategoryProducts by combining URL parameters and the filter options: players from players, power_voltage from powerVoltage, width range from widthMin and widthMax, price range from priceMin and priceMax, and searchQuery from q. (4) Determine sort from the sort URL parameter; if missing, the page may fall back to a default sort defined by getProductFilterOptions.sort_options or by a site-level configuration not hardcoded in this page. (5) Determine page from the page URL parameter (defaulting to the first page if absent) and pageSize from the pageSize parameter (falling back to a configured default, not hardcoded in this page). (6) Call getCategoryProducts with categoryCode, the constructed filters object, sort, page, and pageSize. Whenever the user interacts with filters, sort, search, pagination, or page size controls, the page updates the relevant URL parameters (using navigation that preserves other parameters) and re-runs the same load sequence. No persistent state is stored outside the URL; short-lived UI state such as the most recent quote or project response is held only in memory for the current page session and not written to localStorage or other persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent reloads when URL parameters change. Reads categoryId, q, and filter/sort/pagination parameters from the URL; calls getCategoryDetails, getProductFilterOptions, and getCategoryProducts; and distributes responses to child components as props or state.

Displays current category information (name, description) based on getCategoryDetails and reflects the active categoryId from the URL. Provides contextual text and can be used as an anchor for the product list.

Provides an input field to search within the current category or across all products, binding to the q URL parameter. On submit, updates q in the URL (and resets page to 1) and triggers the controller to call getCategoryProducts with a filters object including the search query.

Displays dynamic filters for the current category using data from getProductFilterOptions (players, power_voltages, width_cm, price, sort_options). It reads current filter values from URL parameters and shows them as selected. When the user changes a filter, the component updates the corresponding URL parameters (players, powerVoltage, widthMin, widthMax, priceMin, priceMax) and resets page to 1, causing the controller to call getCategoryProducts with updated filters.

Displays a sort dropdown populated from getProductFilterOptions.sort_options and a control for page size based on the current pageSize URL parameter. When the user changes sort or page size, it updates the sort and/or pageSize URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a reload of products via getCategoryProducts.

Displays the list of products returned by getCategoryProducts as a grid. Each product card shows fields such as name, model_code, short_description, players, price, width_cm, power_voltage, status, and thumbnail_image_url. It passes product availability flags (is_available_for_quote, is_available_for_project) and identifiers to the product-card-actions component.

Embedded within each product card to handle user actions. It shows available actions based on is_available_for_quote and is_available_for_project. For quote actions, it collects quantity and calls addProductToQuoteList with the product_id and quantity. For project actions, it collects quantity and optional notes and calls addProductToProject. It also handles click navigation to product.html using the product_id and current categoryId for detail view.

Uses the page, page_size, and total_count values from getCategoryProducts to render pagination controls (e.g., next, previous, and specific page selection). When the user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter while preserving categoryId, q, filters, sort, and pageSize, then triggers getCategoryProducts via the controller.

Shows transient feedback after addProductToQuoteList is called (e.g., number of items in the quote list and total_estimated_value), using the response from addProductToQuoteList. Provides a 'View quote list' action that navigates to quotes.html. It is visible only after at least one successful quote addition in the current page session.

Shows transient feedback after addProductToProject is called (e.g., total_machines and total_estimated_width_cm), using the response from addProductToProject. Provides a 'View project list' action that navigates to projects.html. It is visible only after at least one successful project addition in the current page session.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global double-height dark header and breadcrumb (outside this scope) → a centered category header block (title + short intro) → a wide, centered search/refine bar → a main content band with a two-column layout (left filter sidebar, right product listing column) → within the right column, a sort-and-page-size bar at the top, then a spacious three-column product grid, followed by centered pagination. Two compact floating status bars for quote and project lists sit at the bottom-right of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s floating chat button behavior.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global double-height dark header and breadcrumb (outside this scope) → a centered category header block (title + short intro) → a wide, centered search/refine bar → a main content band with a two-column layout (left filter sidebar, right product listing column) → within the right column, a sort-and-page-size bar at the top, then a spacious three-column product grid, followed by centered pagination. Two compact floating status bars for quote and project lists sit at the bottom-right of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s floating chat button behavior.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical spacing mirrors the mockup: generous 48–64px gaps between major sections (category header, search bar, main content area, pagination), and 24–32px internal padding within components. Horizontally, content is constrained to a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px). Within the main listing area, the filter sidebar occupies roughly 3/12 of the container width on the left, and the product listing column occupies the remaining 9/12. Products are arranged in a three-column grid similar to the three blog items from the mockup, with wide gutters and ample white space around each card.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and airy: large vertical padding around the category header and search bar, generous spacing between filter groups, and product cards with substantial white space around images, titles, and action buttons. Only three product cards are displayed per row on desktop to echo the mockup’s light three-item row, with clear separation between rows and minimal visual clutter in the controls. The status bars are compact but not dense, showing only essential information and a single key action each.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the dark global header and breadcrumb (external), then moves to the centered category title and intro as a strong focal point. From there, it flows down to the wide search/refine bar, then into the main content band where the left filter column provides structural stability while the right-side sort bar and product grid attract most attention. Within the grid, the eye moves left-to-right across the three product cards per row, drawn by product images and bright accent colors on action buttons. Finally, the fixed quote and project status bars in the bottom-right act as persistent, high-contrast endpoints for ongoing quote/project actions, mirroring the floating button emphasis from the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Inside a full-width light background that sits below the site’s dark double header and breadcrumb, the product listing page is organized within a centered, max-width content container. At the top of this container, a spacious category header block presents the category name and a short intro paragraph in the center, surrounded by generous white space so it reads like a calm, hero-style title. A wide search/refine bar sits directly beneath, spanning most of the container width and clearly separated with comfortable vertical gaps, echoing the mockup’s clean, single-purpose rows. Below this, the main content band splits into two columns: a slim filter sidebar on the left and a broader listing column on the right. The listing column starts with a simple sort-and-page-size bar, then opens into a low-density, three-column product grid where each card has a tidy image, descriptive text, and prominent neon-accented action buttons. Pagination controls sit centered under the grid, with ample breathing room above and below. While the main column scrolls, two compact, elevated status bars for the quote list and project list float in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, layered above the content and softly glowing with the neon accent, providing an always-available call-to-action that mirrors the floating button behavior seen in the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and sits logically at the very top of the page’s structure, preceding all visible components in the DOM. It does not occupy any visual space, does not introduce margins or padding, and has no direct presence in the 12-column grid. Its role is purely functional: initializing data, filter states, and URL parameters before the category header and other visible components render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visible spatial behavior; it neither floats nor sticks and does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the global breadcrumb strip, the category header occupies the full width of the centered content container, with the surrounding page background remaining light and uncluttered. Vertically, it is given generous breathing room: approximately 48–64px of whitespace above (down from the breadcrumb) and around 32–48px below (before the search/refine bar). The main category title is center-aligned within this block, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container width, with a slightly smaller intro paragraph directly underneath, also center-aligned. The block feels like a subtle, compact hero section: no borders, just ample padding and clean typography, with perhaps a faint cyan or lime accent underline beneath the title to tie back to the dark-header accents.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-refine bar sits immediately below the category header, separated by roughly 32px of vertical whitespace. It is horizontally centered within the max-width container and spans about 70–80% of that width on desktop, creating a prominent, yet not overwhelming, horizontal band. Its internal layout is a single row with a wide search input occupying the majority of the bar (around 70% of the bar width), flanked by a primary action button (e.g., ‘Search’ or ‘Apply’) styled with a neon-lime or cyan accent. The bar uses a subtle, light background with slightly rounded corners and 16–24px of internal padding, floating visually against the white page. There is an additional 32–40px of spacing below this bar, clearly separating it from the two-column main content band that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the flow; it scrolls away with the content. On smaller screens it can expand to nearly full width with the input and controls stacking vertically if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar appears as the left column of the main content band, starting directly beneath the search-refine bar. It aligns to the left edge of the centered content container and occupies roughly 3 of the 12 grid columns (about 25% of the container width) on desktop. The sidebar extends vertically alongside the product grid, beginning at the same top baseline as the sort-and-page-size bar in the right column. It is styled as a light, card-like panel with 16–24px internal padding, subtle dividers between filter groups, and around 24–32px vertical spacing between each group to maintain the low-density feel. Horizontally, there is a generous gutter (approximately 32px) between the sidebar and the product listing column to keep the layout feeling open. On smaller breakpoints, the sidebar collapses above the grid as a full-width, collapsible panel to preserve clarity and breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the filter sidebar behaves as a sticky column: its top edge remains offset about 16–24px below the bottom of the global header/breadcrumb as the user scrolls, so filters remain visible while the product grid scrolls. On mobile, it becomes non-sticky and stacks above the listing for simplicity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-page-size bar anchors the top of the right-hand product listing column, aligned horizontally with the top of the filter-sidebar. It spans the full width of the right column (approximately 9 of 12 grid columns) and sits flush to the left gutter between sidebar and main content. This bar is visually light: a single horizontal row about 48px tall, with 12–16px of vertical padding and no heavy borders. Typical controls—such as a ‘Sort by’ dropdown and a page-size selector—are aligned to the right edge, while an optional results count or view-toggle can be aligned to the left, leaving generous whitespace in the center. There is about 24–32px of vertical spacing between the bottom of this bar and the first row of product cards, keeping the area uncluttered and echoing the mockup’s spacious separation between textual blocks and visual items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls away with the content. It does not stick to the top, allowing the product grid to become the dominant visual element as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid occupies the main portion of the right-hand column, directly beneath the sort-and-page-size bar, and spans the full width of that column (approximately 9 of 12 grid columns). Mirroring the mockup’s three blog items, the grid is organized into three equal-width product cards per row on desktop, each card taking roughly one third of the column width minus wide horizontal gutters of about 24–32px between cards. There is also generous vertical spacing (32–40px) between rows. The grid begins around 24–32px below the sort bar and extends downward, with an additional 48–64px of white space below the last row before the pagination controls. Each card includes an image area on top, followed by product title and brief meta info, and then the product-card-actions region at the bottom, all arranged with ample internal padding (16–24px) and clear vertical separation. The cards sit on a clean white background with subtle shadow or border to maintain the airy, modern corporate look. On tablets, the grid collapses to two columns, and on mobile to one column, while preserving the generous spacing around each card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the right column. It expands vertically as more products are loaded, pushing pagination and page bottom downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each product card, the product-card-actions area occupies a dedicated horizontal strip at the bottom, visually separated from the descriptive content above by either extra spacing or a subtle divider line. It spans the full internal width of the card, with 12–16px of vertical padding. The primary actions—such as ‘Add to quote’ and ‘Add to project’—are arranged in a horizontal row, typically two buttons side by side, each consuming about 45–50% of the card’s inner width with a small gap between them. The more important action uses a solid neon-lime or cyan background to echo the header accent, while the secondary action uses an outline or ghost style. The actions area is visually balanced with ample breathing room around the buttons so the card maintains a low-density feel. To keep card heights consistent regardless of title length, this action strip is anchored to the bottom edge of the card interior, with the content above flexing as needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside each product card; it does not float independently. It behaves as a fixed bottom region of the card, maintaining its position relative to card edges even as card content varies.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly below the product grid within the right-hand column, centered horizontally relative to that column. There is a generous block of whitespace (approximately 48–64px) separating the bottom of the last row of product cards from the top of the pagination, and another 48–64px of whitespace below the pagination before any subsequent content or page footer. The control elements (page numbers, previous/next arrows) are arranged in a single, compact horizontal row, with the active page highlighted using a subtle accent—such as a neon-lime underline or pill-shaped background—consistent with the site’s accent colors. The pagination unit itself is visually light, without heavy borders, so it reads as a clean, minimal control bar punctuating the end of the grid rather than a dense block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s flow; it moves downward as more content is added above and scrolls out of view with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-status-bar appears as a compact, floating panel in the bottom-right quadrant of the viewport, visually inspired by the mockup’s floating chat button. It does not sit within the main content container’s flow; instead, it hovers above it with a small offset of about 24–32px from both the right and bottom edges of the screen. The bar is a pill- or rounded-rectangle shape roughly 260–320px wide and 48–56px tall, with a dark background and neon-lime accent border or glow to make it stand out against the light content area. Inside, an icon on the left, a short status line (e.g., ‘3 items in quote’) in the center, and a primary action label or button (e.g., ‘View quote’) on the right are arranged in a single row with comfortable padding (8–12px). The overall appearance is minimal and unobtrusive, yet visually strong, acting as a persistent call-to-action endpoint. On mobile, this may condense into a circular or small pill button with an icon and badge, aligned to the same bottom-right position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s bottom-right corner, independent of scroll. It remains visible as the user scrolls through products, lightly overlapping the main content area without obscuring critical information. On narrower screens, it can partially overlap but keeps a small margin from the edge to maintain touch comfort.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-status-bar mirrors the quote-status-bar in style and behavior, forming a small vertical stack of floating controls in the bottom-right corner. It is positioned just above the quote-status-bar with a vertical gap of about 8–12px between them, maintaining the same width (around 260–320px) and rounded shape. The background remains dark but may use a complementary cyan accent instead of lime to help distinguish it from the quote bar. Internally, it displays a concise status message (e.g., ‘2 machines in project’) and a single key action (e.g., ‘Open project’), arranged in a horizontal row with compact padding. The stacked arrangement preserves the mockup’s concept of a strong, always-available action element in the bottom-right, while still keeping the overall footprint modest and the layout visually balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s bottom-right area, stacked directly above the quote-status-bar. It remains visible while scrolling, maintaining its offset from the viewport edges. On smaller screens where vertical space is limited, it can collapse into a toggleable tray or icon that expands on tap, still anchored to the bottom-right.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays full details of a single amusement machine, including specifications, media, manuals, and actions to add the product to a quote list or project.

core_features: Load and display complete product data using getProductDetails(productId) based on the id URL parameter.
Show technical specifications such as players, dimensions, weight, power, and status from the product data.
Display product media (primary image and gallery images) using thumbnail_image_url and gallery_image_urls.
Provide breadcrumb navigation back to the product category listing using categoryId URL parameter when available, or the product’s category_code.
Allow users to add the product with a chosen quantity to the current quote list using addProductToQuoteList(productId, quantity).
Allow users to add the product with a chosen quantity and optional notes to the current project using addProductToProject(productId, quantity, notes).
Display support resources and manuals for the product using getProductSupportResources(productId) and related_manuals from getProductDetails.

Provide navigation to related pages: category listing, quote list, project list, downloads (manuals), service request, and financing estimation with appropriate URL parameters derived from the loaded product.
user_workflows:
View product details from a listing:
1. User clicks a product on products.html, opening product.html?id={Product.id}&categoryId={ProductCategory.code}.
2. On page load, the controller reads id and categoryId from the URL.
3. The page calls getProductDetails(productId = id) and waits for the response.
4. Once loaded, header, media gallery, specs table, and actions (quote/project/support) are populated with data from the response.
5. Breadcrumbs are built using categoryId if present; otherwise they use the returned category_code and category_name.
6. In parallel or after details load, the page calls getProductSupportResources(productId = id) and shows manuals in the support panel.

Add a product to the quote list:
1. User views product.html?id={Product.id} with details already loaded.
2. In the quote action area, the user sets a quantity value.
3. User clicks the "Add to quote" button.
4. The page invokes addProductToQuoteList(productId = current product_id, quantity = selected quantity).
5. On success, the component displays feedback using the returned message and updates any on-page quote summary (e.g., total_items and total_estimated_value if shown).
6. The user can click a "View quote list" navigation action, which opens quotes.html (no extra parameters, as quote list context is handled outside this page).

Add a product to a project/wish list:
1. User views product.html?id={Product.id} with details already loaded.
2. In the project action area, the user sets a quantity and may optionally enter notes for how this machine will be used in their venue.
3. User clicks the "Add to project" button.
4. The page invokes addProductToProject(productId = current product_id, quantity = selected quantity, notes = entered notes).
5. On success, the component displays feedback using the returned message and may show updated aggregate info such as total_machines and total_estimated_width_cm.
6. The user can click a "View project list" navigation action, which opens projects.html.

Access manuals and support resources:
1. User is on product.html?id={Product.id}.
2. Support panel shows a list of manuals from getProductSupportResources(productId) and related_manuals from getProductDetails.
3. User clicks on a specific manual row to open the file_url (handled by the downloads section or direct link on that page, not by this page’s own APIs).
4. If the user wants to see more manuals, they click a "View all manuals" link.
5. The page navigates to downloads.html with parameters built from current product details: query = product model_code or name (as configured) and documentType = installation_manual.

Request service for the product:
1. User is on product.html?id={Product.id} with product details loaded.
2. In the support section, user clicks "Request service for this model".
3. The page constructs a URL to service_request.html including productCategoryCode = current product’s category_code and productId = current product_id.
4. User is redirected to service_request.html with those parameters pre-filling the target form.

Estimate financing based on product price:
1. User is on product.html with product details loaded, including price and price_currency (if available).
2. User clicks "Estimate monthly payments".
3. The page navigates to financing.html with price set to the product’s price (from getProductDetails).
4. The financing page uses this price parameter to initialize its own calculator (outside the scope of this page).

Navigate back to listing or category:
1. User arrives at product.html from products.html, index.html, downloads.html, or events.html.
2. Breadcrumb component shows links such as Home and category.
3. If categoryId is present in the current URL, the "Back to category" link points to products.html?categoryId={categoryId}.
4. If categoryId is not present, the component falls back to using the product’s category_code from getProductDetails and builds products.html?categoryId={category_code}.
5. When the user clicks this link, they return to the category listing with the appropriate filter.

Navigate to quote and project overviews:
1. After adding the product to a quote or project, success messages are shown.
2. User clicks "View quote list" to go to quotes.html, or "View project list" to go to projects.html.
3. The destination pages load the relevant quote or project context using their own mechanisms (not defined on this page).
interactions: On page load: Read id and categoryId from URL. If id is missing, the controller should not call the interfaces and instead show an error state; if id is present, call getProductDetails(productId = id), then call getProductSupportResources(productId = id).
On URL parameter change (e.g., navigation within a single-page app context): Re-run getProductDetails and getProductSupportResources with the new id.
On image thumbnail click in the gallery: Update the currently displayed main image to the selected gallery_image_urls entry.
On "Add to quote" quantity input change: Update an internal quantity state for the quote action; validate that it is a positive number before allowing submission.

On "Add to quote" button click: Call addProductToQuoteList(productId = current product_id, quantity = current quantity state); handle success and error responses by updating feedback messages and optional on-page summary.
On "Add to project" quantity input or notes input change: Update internal quantity and notes state for project action; ensure quantity is valid before enabling submit.

On "Add to project" button click: Call addProductToProject(productId = current product_id, quantity = current quantity state, notes = current notes state); handle response with feedback and optional totals display.
On "View quote list" click: Navigate to quotes.html (no additional parameters required).
On "View project list" click: Navigate to projects.html (no additional parameters required).

On "View all manuals" click: Navigate to downloads.html with query and documentType parameters based on loaded product details (e.g., query set to model_code or name, documentType set to installation_manual).
On "Request service for this model" click: Navigate to service_request.html with productCategoryCode and productId taken from the loaded product.
On "Estimate monthly payments" click: Navigate to financing.html with price set to the product’s price from getProductDetails.
On breadcrumb category link click: Navigate back to products.html?categoryId={derived category code}, prioritizing categoryId from URL, otherwise using product.category_code.
On support manual item click: Open the manual’s file URL or direct download (the exact behavior depends on how the manual links are implemented outside of this page’s APIs).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. The id URL parameter is mandatory for loading data; the page reads id from product.html?id={Product.id}. If id is present, getProductDetails(productId = id) is called to obtain all product information, including category_code, model_code, and related_manuals. getProductSupportResources(productId = id) is also called to obtain detailed manual metadata. The optional categoryId URL parameter is used to construct breadcrumbs and the 'Back to category' link when available. If categoryId is absent, the page derives the categoryId value from the returned product.category_code. Within the page, local UI state such as selected media image, selected tab (if any), quantity, notes, and success/error messages is maintained in memory only for the duration of the visit and is not persisted with localStorage or similar mechanisms. All cross-page navigation uses URL parameters only, for example: products.html?categoryId={category code}, downloads.html?query={product model or name}&documentType=installation_manual, service_request.html?productCategoryCode={product.category_code}&productId={product_id}, financing.html?price={product.price}. No static or hardcoded product data is used; all product attributes and support resources are derived dynamically from getProductDetails and getProductSupportResources responses using the current id URL parameter.

components.functionality: Central controller for the product detail page. Reads URL parameters, triggers data loading via the assigned interfaces, coordinates loading states, error handling, and passes product and support data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read id and categoryId from the URL. If id exists, call getProductDetails(productId = id).

onProductDetailsSuccess: Store the product details in page state and trigger getProductSupportResources(productId = product_id from response). Notify dependent components (header, gallery, specs, actions, support).
onProductDetailsError: Set an error state to inform components that product data could not be loaded; suppress calls to other interfaces.
onSupportResourcesSuccess: Attach the manual list to page state and pass data to the support-resources-panel.
onSupportResourcesError: Set a support-specific error state but keep product details available.
onUrlParamChange: When id or categoryId changes (e.g., via internal navigation without full reload), repeat the load sequence with the new id.
onQuantityChange: Update shared quantity state (if a central shared quantity is used or propagated to specific action components).
onNotesChange: Update shared notes state used by the project action component.

components.functionality: Displays navigational breadcrumbs including a link back to the product category listing and possibly the site root. Uses categoryId from the URL when available, falling back to the product’s own category_code and category_name from getProductDetails.
event_handlers: onRender: Determine effectiveCategoryCode = categoryId from URL if defined, otherwise getProductDetails.category_code.
onRender: Build 'Back to category' link pointing to products.html?categoryId={effectiveCategoryCode}.
onClickHomeBreadcrumb: Navigate to the site home page (index.html) without altering other state.

components.functionality: Shows core product identity fields at the top of the detail page, including name, model code, short description, status, and a minimal set of primary specs (e.g., players or category name).
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Populate header fields from the getProductDetails response.
onStatusChange: If product.status is not 'active', adjust display to indicate availability status (behavior defined functionally, not visually).

components.functionality: Displays the primary product image and a selectable gallery of additional images. Allows users to switch the main image by clicking thumbnails.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Initialize the currently displayed main image using thumbnail_image_url or the first entry in gallery_image_urls if defined.
onThumbnailClick: Update the main displayed image to the clicked gallery_image_urls item.
onImageLoadError: Fallback to another available image from gallery_image_urls or a non-static placeholder behavior defined by the implementation (without hardcoded image data).

components.functionality: Displays a structured list or table of technical specifications for the product, such as players, dimensions, weight, power requirements, price, and other numeric/enum fields derived from getProductDetails.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Render the specs table by mapping each non-null field from getProductDetails to a row or definition entry.
onFieldValueChange: If the product details are updated while on the page (e.g., via a refresh action), re-render the specs accordingly.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select a quantity and add the current product to their quote list. Shows success or error feedback using the addProductToQuoteList response and may show a minimal summary of quote totals.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Enable or disable the quote form based on getProductDetails.is_available_for_quote.
onQuantityInputChange: Update local quoteQuantity state and validate that it is a positive number; disable the submit button if invalid.
onSubmitAddToQuote: If is_available_for_quote is true and quantity is valid, call addProductToQuoteList(productId = getProductDetails.product_id, quantity = quoteQuantity).
onAddToQuoteSuccess: Display the response.message, and if desired, show returned totals (total_items, total_estimated_value, currency).
onAddToQuoteError: Display an error message and allow the user to retry.
onClickViewQuoteList: Navigate to quotes.html after a successful addition or at any time the user chooses.

components.functionality: Lets the user add the current product to a project or wish list with a chosen quantity and optional notes. Uses addProductToProject and displays aggregate project information from the response.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Enable or disable the project form based on getProductDetails.is_available_for_project.
onProjectQuantityInputChange: Update local projectQuantity state and validate it.
onSubmitAddToProject: If is_available_for_project is true and quantity is valid, call addProductToProject(productId = getProductDetails.product_id, quantity = projectQuantity, notes = projectNotes).
onAddToProjectSuccess: Display response.message and, if needed, show total_machines and total_estimated_width_cm from the response to give context about the project size.
onAddToProjectError: Display an error message and allow retry.
onClickViewProjectList: Navigate to projects.html after successful addition or when the user chooses.

components.functionality: Provides a functional link to the financing page for estimating monthly payments based on the loaded product’s price.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Determine if a price is available; if not, the link can be disabled or accompanied by an informative message.
onClickEstimateMonthlyPayments: Navigate to financing.html with price set to getProductDetails.price (e.g., financing.html?price={price}).

components.functionality: Displays a list of manuals and technical documents for the product using data from getProductSupportResources and related_manuals from getProductDetails. Provides navigation to downloads.html for viewing all manuals related to the product.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Use related_manuals as a quick summary list of key manuals, if present.
onSupportResourcesLoaded: Merge or present the detailed manual list from getProductSupportResources, including title, document_type, language, version, and published_at.
onManualItemClick: Open the selected manual’s file (through its associated URL, which is handled in the downloads or file service context, not via additional APIs on this page).
onClickViewAllManuals: Navigate to downloads.html with query set to getProductDetails.model_code or name (depending on implementation) and documentType set to installation_manual.

components.functionality: Provides a functional link to request service for the displayed product, pre-populating the service request form with the product’s identifier and category via URL parameters.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsLoaded: Build a URL for service_request.html including productCategoryCode = getProductDetails.category_code and productId = getProductDetails.product_id.
onClickRequestService: Navigate to the constructed service_request.html URL.

components.functionality: Manages navigation back to category or other listing contexts based on URL parameters and product data. Ensures that the user can easily return to where they came from or to a default category view.
event_handlers: onProductDetailsOrUrlLoaded: Determine effectiveCategoryCode from URL categoryId if present, otherwise from getProductDetails.category_code.

onClickBackToAllProducts: Optionally navigate to products.html?categoryId=all_products or another implementation-defined category code derived at runtime (no hardcoded text labels beyond functional parameters).

components.functionality: Centralized handler for displaying validation, success, and error messages for quote actions, project actions, and data loading.
event_handlers: onAddToQuoteSuccess: Show a success message using response.message.
onAddToQuoteError: Show an error message indicating that adding to the quote list failed.
onAddToProjectSuccess: Show a success message using response.message.
onAddToProjectError: Show an error message indicating that adding to the project failed.
onProductDetailsError: Show an error message indicating that the product could not be loaded.
onSupportResourcesError: Show an error or informational message indicating manuals could not be loaded.
onValidationError: Display messages when quantity or other inputs are invalid and block submission until resolved.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, stacked structure as the mockup: global dark double header (outside this spec) → a shallow full-width light breadcrumb strip → a wide white main content canvas with a centered max-width container. Inside the container: page-level feedback messages (when present) sit at the very top, followed by a prominent product header summary block. Below that, a single key information row uses the mockup’s three-column pattern: media gallery, specs, and action stack (quote/project/financing). Under this row, secondary content (support resources, category/listing navigation) is stacked in clearly separated horizontal bands. A lime-accent floating service button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport as the persistent action endpoint.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, stacked structure as the mockup: global dark double header (outside this spec) → a shallow full-width light breadcrumb strip → a wide white main content canvas with a centered max-width container. Inside the container: page-level feedback messages (when present) sit at the very top, followed by a prominent product header summary block. Below that, a single key information row uses the mockup’s three-column pattern: media gallery, specs, and action stack (quote/project/financing). Under this row, secondary content (support resources, category/listing navigation) is stacked in clearly separated horizontal bands. A lime-accent floating service button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport as the persistent action endpoint.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Respect the mockup’s proportions: the dark double header occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport; the breadcrumb strip is a shallow band. The main content area uses generous vertical spacing: 48–64px between major blocks (breadcrumb → header summary → main three-column row → support/resources → bottom navigation). Inside each card or panel, use 16–24px padding. The three main columns together span about 80–90% of the centered container width, each roughly one-third of that width with comfortable gutters between them. The floating service action occupies a small circular area in the bottom-right corner, offset 24–32px from the edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, airy layout. Typography has ample vertical breathing room with 16–24px separation between headings, body text, and CTAs. Panels contain only the essentials in each block: the header summary is concise, the media gallery is visually dominant but not cluttered, specs are scannable, and action panels focus on a small number of clear buttons or links. Support resources and navigational links are grouped but not crowded, using whitespace to emphasize hierarchy and scannability rather than packing in many items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye vertically from top to bottom, mirroring the mockup: logo and navigation (global) → breadcrumb strip → centered product title and key summary → left-to-right sweep across the three-column information row (media → specs → quote/project/financing actions). Accent color (neon-lime, cyan) is used sparingly but strategically: product name highlights, primary CTAs, and the floating service button. The persistent floating service button in the bottom-right acts as a visual endpoint and safety net action, echoing the mockup’s floating chat button.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page sits beneath a dark, double-height corporate header and a slim, light breadcrumb strip, opening into a broad white canvas with a centered content column around 1140–1200px wide. At the top of this column, any feedback messages appear first, followed by a spacious product header summary block that gives the product name and a concise description room to breathe. Below this, a single, balanced three-column row spans most of the container: on the left, a tall media gallery showcases the machine; in the middle, a clean specifications panel lists key data; on the right, a vertical stack of compact cards presents the add-to-quote and add-to-project actions, plus a financing link, with neon-lime CTAs echoing the accent colors in the header. Generous vertical gaps separate this primary row from a quieter support/resources area below, where manuals and support links are grouped, and from a final, understated navigation band that helps users jump back to categories or related listings. All of this scrolls beneath a small, circular, lime-accent service button that floats fixed at the bottom-right of the viewport, subtly hovering over the content as a persistent, high-visibility action point, mirroring the mockup’s floating chat affordance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-detail-controller defines the overall page canvas under the global dark double header. It stretches full-width across the browser, setting a light background for the breadcrumb strip directly below the header and a white background for the main content area beneath. Within this controller, a centered content container (approximately 1140–1200px wide) is anchored to the middle of the viewport horizontally, using a 12-column grid.
