3. User scrolls or uses in-page navigation to move focus to the technical support section.
4. The user reads the technical_support_section description and uses the displayed contact_email and/or contact_phone from the interface data.
5. If the user needs a generic contact form, they click the technical support link that triggers navigation to contact.html.
Workflow: Changing focus between support pathways within the page
1. User arrives on support.html and the page loads data from getSupportOverview.
2. The page determines an initial active section based on the focus URL parameter or a default if none is present.
3. User interacts with a section selector (for example, clicking a tab or internal link representing manuals, technical support, or service).
4. The page updates its internal activeSection state, scrolls to the selected section, and updates the URL using pushState or replaceState to set the focus parameter accordingly, without reloading or recalling getSupportOverview.
5. The user continues interacting with the newly focused section (e.g., clicking its primary action).
interactions: On page load, the page reads the focus URL parameter (if present) and calls getSupportOverview. Once the data is received, it renders all sections and then scrolls to and activates the section associated with the focus value (if valid).
Users can click the manuals primary action in the manuals section, which triggers a navigation to downloads.html.
Users can click the primary service request CTA (either global or inside the service section), which triggers a navigation to service_request.html.
Users can click a technical support link or button inside the technical support section to navigate to contact.html.

Users can interact with any internal section selector control (e.g., clicking a link representing one of the support pathways) to change which section is considered active, causing the page to scroll to that section and update the focus URL parameter.

If getSupportOverview fails or returns incomplete data, the page displays a non-specific error state for the affected sections (without any hardcoded fallback text for titles or descriptions) and keeps navigation actions disabled or hidden for any section that lacks required data.

If the user manually changes the focus parameter in the URL and reloads the page, the page reinterprets the parameter on load, setting the active section and scroll position accordingly based solely on the new URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page maintains its primary state using the focus URL parameter and in-memory state derived from it, without using localStorage. When support.html is loaded, the implementation reads window.location.search for a focus parameter. Valid values are mapped to specific sections: for example, a value corresponding to manuals maps to the manuals_section data, and a value corresponding to service maps to the service_request_section data. If the focus parameter is missing or does not match any known mapping, the page selects a default section (for example, the manuals section or the first available section returned by getSupportOverview) as active without modifying the URL. After getSupportOverview returns, the page stores the response object (containing manuals_section, technical_support_section, and service_request_section) in memory and uses it to render the three support sections. No further calls to getSupportOverview are made when switching between sections. When a user changes focus via in-page controls, the activeSection state is updated, the page scrolls to the corresponding section, and the browser URL is updated via history.pushState or history.replaceState to reflect the new focus parameter (e.g., support.html?focus=<section-key>) without reloading the page. If the URL changes externally (e.g., browser back/forward navigation changes the focus value), the page listens for popstate events, re-derives the active section from the updated focus parameter, and adjusts the UI and scroll position accordingly, still using the already-loaded getSupportOverview data.

components.functionality: Initializes the Support Overview page by reading the focus URL parameter, calling getSupportOverview once to retrieve section content, and initializing in-memory state for manuals, technical support, and service sections as well as the currently active section.

Derives and manages which support section (manuals, technical support, or service) is considered active based on the focus URL parameter and user interactions. It maps focus parameter values to section identifiers, updates the internal activeSection state, triggers scrolling to the chosen section, and synchronizes the URL focus parameter when the user switches sections.

Renders the manuals and downloads support pathway using the manuals_section data from getSupportOverview. It displays the section title and description as provided by the interface and exposes a primary action element that navigates the user to downloads.html when activated.

Renders the technical support pathway using the technical_support_section data from getSupportOverview. It displays the section title, description, and direct contact details (email and phone) from the interface and provides a navigation action that sends the user to contact.html for general technical support inquiries.

Renders the service request and maintenance pathway using the service_request_section data returned by getSupportOverview. It displays the section title and description and exposes a primary action (such as a button) that navigates users to service_request.html to submit detailed service requests.

Provides a persistent, prominent call-to-action on the Support Overview page that navigates directly to service_request.html, independent of the detailed service section content, ensuring that users can always initiate a service request quickly.

Monitors the result of the getSupportOverview call and determines whether individual sections can be rendered. If an error occurs or required fields are missing for a section, it flags that section as unavailable and updates the UI to prevent navigation actions that rely on missing data, without introducing any hardcoded fallback titles or messages.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: global double dark header (info bar + primary nav) at the very top → a shallow, full-width light breadcrumb strip → the Support Overview main content in a centered, max-width white canvas. Inside this canvas, the flow is: page-init block with title and intro (centered) → an optional, subtle support-section-router control just below the intro → a single row of three equally weighted support-section cards (manuals, technical support, service request) laid out as a three-column grid within the 12-column system → the white background then continues below with generous empty space, while a floating global-service-cta button sits above the bottom-right edge of the canvas as a persistent action. The support-error-state-handler, when active, appears directly under the breadcrumb and above the page-init block, spanning the inner content width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: global double dark header (info bar + primary nav) at the very top → a shallow, full-width light breadcrumb strip → the Support Overview main content in a centered, max-width white canvas. Inside this canvas, the flow is: page-init block with title and intro (centered) → an optional, subtle support-section-router control just below the intro → a single row of three equally weighted support-section cards (manuals, technical support, service request) laid out as a three-column grid within the 12-column system → the white background then continues below with generous empty space, while a floating global-service-cta button sits above the bottom-right edge of the canvas as a persistent action. The support-error-state-handler, when active, appears directly under the breadcrumb and above the page-init block, spanning the inner content width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The double header still occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport, with the breadcrumb as a shallow strip beneath it. The main Support Overview canvas uses large vertical spacing: ~48–64px between breadcrumb and page-init, ~32px between title and intro, ~32px between intro and router, and ~48–64px above the three-column section row. Each of the three section-block cards occupies approximately one third of the inner content width (4 columns each in a 12-column grid) with wide horizontal gutters (~24–32px). The floating global-service-cta occupies a small but visually strong circular footprint fixed near the bottom-right, not competing for vertical flow. The error handler consumes a shallow but full-width band of the content container with ~16–24px internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low and airy as in the mockup. The title and intro have generous breathing room above and below. Each section-block card is visually light, with a small illustrative thumbnail/icon and a concise title and short description, plus a primary action, surrounded by ample whitespace and consistent 16–24px internal padding. The router control is compact, with pill-like buttons and comfortable horizontal spacing, avoiding clutter. The error handler appears as a single band of text with clear padding and margin, not overwhelming the layout. Overall, the page favors scannability and clear, distinct touch targets rather than packing many items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves vertically from the logo/navigation in the dark header down to the breadcrumb, then into the main white canvas. Within the canvas, the bold, center-aligned Support title is the primary anchor, followed by the intro paragraph directly below. Next, the eye drops slightly to the horizontally centered router control (if used), and then naturally to the three side-by-side support sections whose headings and accent colors guide scanning left-to-right across Manuals → Technical Support → Service Request. Finally, the floating neon-lime global-service-cta in the bottom-right forms a persistent endpoint for high-intent actions. When present, the error handler temporarily takes precedence just below the breadcrumb, catching attention before the title while still fitting into the vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: From top to bottom, the Support Overview page sits within the site’s established frame: a full-width dark double header for info and primary navigation, followed immediately by a shallow, full-width light breadcrumb strip. Below this, a wide white content area spans the center of the viewport, constrained to a max width of about 1140–1200px and aligned to a 12-column grid. After a generous vertical gap from the breadcrumb, a large, center-aligned Support Overview title appears with ample white space around it, followed by a single short intro paragraph that remains relatively narrow and centered within the canvas for comfortable reading. Just beneath this, a slim row of pill-style router options may appear, centered horizontally, lightly outlined and spaced to feel unobtrusive. Further down, a single row of three equally sized support cards occupies the main part of the viewport, each card representing Manuals & Downloads, Technical Support, and Service & Maintenance requests. These three blocks sit side by side, each taking roughly one third of the content width with spacious gutters between them and consistent internal padding, small imagery at the top and lime-accented titles drawing the eye from left to right. The white background continues below these cards, maintaining open space and visual calm. Floating above this canvas, fixed near the bottom-right corner of the viewport, a circular neon-lime action button for contacting global service subtly overlaps the white area, mirroring the chat-style FAB in the mockup. When an error occurs, a full-width, lightly styled alert strip appears just under the breadcrumb and above the hero title, spanning the same centered width as the content, clearly visible without disrupting the airy rhythm of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component occupies the top portion of the main white content canvas below the breadcrumb. It is contained within the centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) and starts after a generous vertical margin of about 56px from the bottom of the breadcrumb strip. Within this space, the primary page title (e.g., “Support Overview”) is center-aligned horizontally, spanning roughly 60–70% of the content width for readability, with whitespace on both sides. The title sits with about 16–24px of breathing room above and at least 24–32px below. Directly beneath the title, an intro paragraph is also center-aligned but constrained to a slightly narrower text column (about 50–60% of the container width) so it forms a clean block of text, not stretching too wide. There is a vertical gap of about 32px between the paragraph’s bottom edge and the next component (the support-section-router). The entire page-init area uses low density: no crowded elements, just a bold heading and a short descriptive paragraph anchored in the middle of a tall band of white space, mirroring the blog page’s title and intro treatment from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main content flow; scrolls naturally with the page and is vertically separated from other sections by large top and bottom margins consistent with the 48–64px section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-section-router is positioned immediately below the intro paragraph inside the same centered content container, preserving the vertical flow. It sits in a relatively shallow horizontal band, with a gap of roughly 32px above (from the page-init intro) and about 32–40px below (down to the three-column section row). Visually, it appears as a row of 2–4 pill-like filter or tab buttons (e.g., “All,” “Manuals,” “Technical Support,” “Service & Maintenance”) aligned horizontally and centered in the container, spanning approximately 50–60% of the container width. Each pill is around 80–140px wide, with 12–16px horizontal padding inside, rounded corners, and subtle borders or background fills using the neon-lime or cyan accent for the active state. The router does not occupy significant vertical height—its total height including padding is similar to a compact button bar—but it respects generous lateral and vertical spacing so the area feels light. On smaller screens, the pills can wrap to a second line or become a horizontally scrollable row while still appearing as one cohesive horizontal control below the title/intro and above the section cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control; it remains in the normal document flow between page-init and the section-block row and scrolls away as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The manuals-support-section is the left-most card in a single row of three support sections that mirrors the three-column blog layout from the mockup. This row starts roughly 48–64px below the router (or below the page-init intro if the router is hidden) within the same centered max-width container. On desktop widths, the manuals card spans about one third of the container (4 of 12 grid columns), aligned flush to the left inner edge of the content grid. There are generous horizontal gutters (about 24–32px) separating it from the middle and right cards. The card itself has a white or very light surface with a subtle border or shadow and internal padding of 16–24px on all sides, keeping content airy. At the top of the card, a small, wide thumbnail or icon (e.g., a document stack) spans roughly 100% of the card width with a fixed height, echoing the blog thumbnail treatment but scaled for a functional card. Below the thumbnail, a lime-accented section title such as “Manuals & Downloads” sits left-aligned, with one or two short lines of description underneath in softer text color. A primary inline button or link (e.g., “View manuals”) anchors the bottom-left of the card. Vertical spacing within the card uses 8–16px increments, leaving generous breathing room above the title and between text elements. On tablets and mobile, this card shifts into a stacked layout: on medium screens it may become the first in a two-column grid, and on narrow screens it spans 100% width with 24–32px vertical gaps between cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the left third of the three-column support row; participates in responsive reflow (from three columns to two and then one) but otherwise scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The technical-support-section occupies the central position in the three-column support row. It is horizontally centered in the same max-width container and aligned so that it takes up approximately one third of the width (4 of 12 columns), with equal horizontal gutters between the left (manuals) and right (service request) cards. Its vertical start aligns with the top edges of the adjacent cards, creating a clean single row. Styling mirrors the manuals card: a light card surface with a subtle border or shadow, 16–24px internal padding, and a small, full-width thumbnail or icon at the top (such as a headset or circuit graphic). Beneath this, a lime-accented heading (e.g., “Technical Support”) appears, followed by a compact description in 1–3 short lines, and a primary action (e.g., “Contact support” or “Open ticket”) aligned to the left near the bottom edge. Internal spacing remains consistent with the other cards so that all three cards appear visually balanced and equal in height, even if text lengths differ slightly. On smaller viewports, this card drops below the manuals card in a vertical stack, keeping 24–32px vertical gaps and maintaining the same internal padding and hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card centered within the three-column row; reflows responsively into second position in a stacked or two-column layout while scrolling with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-request-section card sits on the right side of the three-column support row, aligned to the rightmost columns of the content grid and matching the top alignment of the other two cards. It also spans about one third of the container width (4 of 12 columns). The card’s surface and padding mirror the others: a light background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px internal padding. A small, wide thumbnail or icon at the top could represent a wrench or van, spanning the card width with a modest height. Immediately below, a lime-colored heading such as “Service & Maintenance” or “On-site Service” sits left-aligned, functioning as the main eye-catching element. A brief outtake explains key actions (e.g., “Request on-site repair or schedule preventive maintenance”), followed by a primary button or link (e.g., “Request service”) anchored toward the bottom of the card. The internal spacing ensures the text and button don’t feel crowded, and the three cards together present as a unified set of options. On smaller screens, this becomes the third card in the vertical stack, again with 24–32px vertical spacing separating it from the technical-support-section above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the right third of the three-column row; participates in responsive reflow, dropping to the third position in a vertical sequence on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-service-cta is a floating, circular action button that sits detached from the main content blocks, echoing the floating chat button behavior in the mockup. It is fixed to the viewport’s bottom-right corner, overlapping the white content canvas rather than the dark header or footer. The button’s center is positioned roughly 24–32px from the right edge and 24–32px from the bottom edge of the viewport. Its diameter is about 56–64px, making it large enough to be tappable while still visually compact. The button uses a neon-lime fill with a subtle shadow or glow to stand out against both light and dark areas, and it may feature a simple icon (e.g., a headset, wrench, or life buoy) centered within, possibly with a cyan accent. It does not align to the grid of the main content, but remains visually consistent by using the same accent colors and rounded shape language. As the user scrolls, the button remains pinned in this bottom-right location, always visible, slightly overlapping the main content but clear of key text and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position floating action button; anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of the main scroll, always visible unless programmatically hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-error-state-handler appears only when needed and occupies a shallow yet full-width band at the top of the main content area, directly under the breadcrumb strip and just above the page-init component. It is constrained to the same centered max-width container as the rest of the content, spanning 100% of that width. Vertically, it uses 12–16px of padding on all sides, giving enough room for a short error title and optional explanatory line without feeling cramped. The background color is a light warning or error shade (e.g., pale red or amber) with a clear contrast to the white canvas, and the text is left-aligned within the band, possibly accompanied by a small icon on the left. There is a vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of this error band and the top of the page-init title, so the components remain visually distinct. When multiple errors or messages are displayed, they stack within this band or in separate bands with small vertical separations. The handler pushes the rest of the content downward but does not overlay it, preserving the clean, linear flow while making the error immediately noticeable upon entering the page or refreshing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient inline banner that appears in the normal document flow below the breadcrumb; it scrolls with the page and pushes main content down when visible, without fixed or sticky behavior.

name: Manuals & Downloads
title: Manuals & Downloads
description: Searchable library of product manuals and technical documents where users can search, filter, view details, and download files for amusement machines.

core_features: Populate available document filters and sort options using getManualFilterOptions.
Allow users to search manuals by free-text query (e.g., model name) via searchManuals.
Filter manuals by document type and date criteria (year range or predefined date ranges) via searchManuals.
Sort search results using available sort options from getManualFilterOptions and pass the selected sort value to searchManuals.
Paginate search results using the page and pageSize parameters of searchManuals.
Open a manual detail view by manual ID and load full metadata (including file URL and related product) using getManualDetails.
Trigger a file download for a selected manual using initiateManualDownload and the returned file_url.
Navigate from a manual detail view to a related product page (product.html?id=...) when product information is available.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct visit and basic search
1. User navigates to downloads.html without URL parameters.
2. Page-state component reads URL (no query/documentType/productId) and initializes empty search criteria.
3. Page calls getManualFilterOptions to retrieve document_types, years, date_ranges, and sort_options.
4. Search form and filters are populated from getManualFilterOptions and URL state (currently default/empty).
5. Page triggers an initial call to searchManuals with current criteria (e.g., empty query, no documentType, no dateRange, default sort, page 1) using values derived from URL parameters or defaults.
6. Results list displays items returned by searchManuals.
7. User enters text in the search field and submits the form.
8. On submit, the search query is written to the URL parameter query, page is reset to 1 in the URL, and searchManuals is called with the new query and current filters/sort.
9. Results list refreshes with the updated items from searchManuals.

Workflow 2: Arriving from Support or Product pages with pre-filters
1. User clicks a link on support.html or product.html to downloads.html with some of: query, documentType, productId as URL parameters.
2. On page load, the state controller reads the incoming URL parameters:
   - query pre-populates the search input and is passed to searchManuals.
   - documentType pre-selects a document type filter in the filter panel once getManualFilterOptions returns, and its value is passed to searchManuals.
   - productId is stored in page state but not sent to any interface; it is used only for contextual display or analytics and does not alter interface contracts.
3. Page calls getManualFilterOptions, then searchManuals with the query and documentType from the URL plus default date and sort values.
4. User may refine filters (type/date) or adjust sort; each change updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers searchManuals again.
5. The user can bookmark or share the URL; anyone opening it will see the same pre-filtered list, because state is reconstructed from URL parameters.

Workflow 3: Refining results with filters, sort, and pagination
1. After an initial result set is loaded, the user selects one or more filter criteria (a document type, a predefined date range or a start/end year range) using controls populated from getManualFilterOptions.
2. On each filter change, the filter component updates the relevant URL parameters (documentType, dateRange, startYear, endYear), resets page to 1, and triggers searchManuals with current query + filters + sort.
3. User changes the sort option (e.g., relevance vs. newest) in the sort control.
4. Sort control updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and calls searchManuals with the updated sort value.
5. For pagination:
   - User clicks a next/previous page control or a specific page number.
   - Pagination component updates the page parameter (and optionally pageSize) in the URL.
   - searchManuals is called with the new page and pageSize along with current query, filters, and sort.
6. Result list updates based on the response from searchManuals, using its page, page_size, total_count, and items properties.

Workflow 4: Viewing manual details and downloading
1. User clicks on a specific manual entry in the results list.
2. Results list component emits a selection event with the manual_id.
3. Detail panel component:
   - Updates the URL with manualId parameter representing the selected manual.
   - Calls getManualDetails(manualId) to load full details including file_url, product_id, and related_product_name.
4. Manual detail panel displays metadata from getManualDetails (title, product_model_name, document_type, language, version, published_at, description, file_size_mb, etc.) and the document status is_active.
5. User clicks the "Download PDF" button inside the detail panel.
6. Detail panel calls initiateManualDownload(manualId).
7. If initiateManualDownload returns success=true and a file_url, the component initiates browser download or opens the file URL in a new tab, depending on implementation.
8. If initiateManualDownload returns success=false, the component displays the message from the response.
9. If getManualDetails includes a product_id and related_product_name, a "View product page" link is shown.
10. On clicking the product link, the browser navigates to product.html?id={product_id} without calling any additional interfaces.

Workflow 5: Deep linking to a specific manual detail
1. A user receives a shared link to downloads.html that includes manualId (and optionally query/filters) in the URL.
2. On page load, state controller reads manualId from URL along with other parameters.
3. State controller performs the usual initialization: calls getManualFilterOptions, then searchManuals using query/filters in URL.
4. In parallel or after results load, the detail panel component recognizes manualId in the URL and calls getManualDetails(manualId).
5. Manual detail panel opens populated with data from getManualDetails without requiring the user to click in the results list.
6. User can download the manual via initiateManualDownload(manualId) or close the detail panel.
7. Closing the detail panel removes manualId from the URL while leaving other search parameters intact, so the search context persists.

Workflow 6: Resetting and changing search context
1. User wants to clear all filters and start a new search.
2. User clicks a "Clear filters" or equivalent control in the filter panel.
3. Filter panel clears documentType, dateRange, startYear, and endYear from URL parameters and resets page to 1; query may remain unchanged or be cleared based on the chosen behavior.
4. searchManuals is called with updated parameters to reflect the cleared filters.
5. If the user also clears the search query (e.g., via a clear button in the search input), the query parameter is removed from the URL, page is reset to 1, and searchManuals is called again.
6. Any open manual detail (manualId) is closed and removed from the URL to keep the search context consistent.
interactions:
Search input interactions
- Typing into the search field updates internal component state but does not call searchManuals until the user submits the form.
- On form submit or pressing Enter:
  * URL parameter query is set to the entered value (or removed if empty).
  * URL parameter page is set to 1.
  * State controller calls searchManuals with query from URL and current filter/sort parameters.
  * Results list re-renders with items from searchManuals.items.

Document type filter interactions
- Document type dropdown or multi-select is populated exclusively from getManualFilterOptions.document_types.
- When user selects or deselects a document type:
  * URL parameter documentType is updated to the selected value (or removed if no document type is selected).
  * URL parameter page is set to 1.
  * searchManuals is called with updated documentType and other criteria from URL.
  * Results list updates based on returned items.

Date range and year filter interactions
- Date controls are populated exclusively from getManualFilterOptions.years and getManualFilterOptions.date_ranges.
- If a predefined date range is selected:
  * URL parameter dateRange is set to the selected date range value.
  * URL parameters startYear and endYear are cleared/ignored for the call.
  * page is reset to 1.
  * searchManuals is invoked with dateRange and other criteria.
- If a specific year range is set (startYear/endYear inputs):
  * URL parameters startYear and endYear are set/updated accordingly.
  * URL parameter dateRange is cleared.
  * page is reset to 1.
  * searchManuals is called with startYear and endYear and other criteria.
- All date-related values used for search come from URL-derived state, not locally hardcoded.

Sort control interactions
- Sort options are populated only from getManualFilterOptions.sort_options.
- On selecting a sort option:
  * URL parameter sort is updated to the selected sort_options.value.
  * URL parameter page is reset to 1.
  * searchManuals is called with the new sort value and current query/filters.
  * Results list re-renders according to the new sort order.

Pagination interactions
- Pagination controls use searchManuals.total_count, page, and page_size to calculate available pages.
- On clicking a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page index):
  * URL parameter page is updated to the selected page number.
  * Optionally, if pageSize is adjustable, URL parameter pageSize is updated when user changes the page size.
  * searchManuals is called with page and pageSize from URL plus current query, filters, and sort.
  * Results list updates with items for the new page.

Result item selection and detail view interactions
- Clicking on a result item row or explicit "View details" control:
  * Emits an event containing the selected manual_id.
  * Updates URL parameter manualId with that manual_id.
  * Triggers getManualDetails(manualId) to fetch detailed metadata.
  * Opens the detail panel populated with getManualDetails response.
- Closing the detail panel:
  * Removes manualId from the URL without altering query, filters, sort, or page.
  * Does not affect the result list or searchManuals state.

Download interactions
- From a result row download control:
  * Clicking a row-level download button calls initiateManualDownload(manualId) for that item.
  * On success, the component uses the returned file_url to initiate a download or open the PDF.
  * On failure, the component displays the returned message.
- From detail panel download button:
  * Behavior is the same as above, using manualId from the open detail panel.
- Download controls do not rely on static file paths; they always use the file_url returned by initiateManualDownload.

Navigation to product page interactions
- Within the detail panel, if getManualDetails returns a product_id:
  * A "View product page" link is rendered.
  * On click, the browser navigates to product.html?id={product_id} using standard URL navigation.
  * No additional interface calls are made as part of this navigation.

URL synchronization interactions
- Any change to query, documentType, dateRange, startYear, endYear, sort, page, pageSize, or manualId triggers a URL update.
- State controller listens to URL changes (including browser back/forward navigation) and re-runs searchManuals and/or getManualDetails as necessary to keep the UI consistent with URL parameters.
- This ensures that reloading the page or using browser navigation preserves search context and open detail state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the single source of truth for page state. On load, the state controller reads the following recognized parameters from the URL: query, documentType, productId, dateRange, startYear, endYear, sort, page, pageSize, manualId. Behavior:
- Initialization:
  * query: Optional string. If present, pre-fills the search input and is passed directly to searchManuals.
  * documentType: Optional string. If present and present in getManualFilterOptions.document_types, it is selected in the document type filter and passed to searchManuals as the documentType parameter.
  * productId: Optional string. Stored in state for contextual use (e.g., indicating that the page was opened from a specific product), but not sent to any interface because searchManuals and other assigned interfaces do not accept productId. It does not change interface behavior.
  * dateRange: Optional string. If present and matching a value from getManualFilterOptions.date_ranges, it is used as the dateRange parameter for searchManuals, and startYear/endYear are ignored.
  * startYear/endYear: Optional numbers. If present and valid, they are passed as startYear and endYear to searchManuals. If dateRange is also present, the component prioritizes dateRange and does not send startYear/endYear in the same call.
  * sort: Optional string. If present and matching one of getManualFilterOptions.sort_options.value, it is passed as the sort parameter to searchManuals. If absent, the default sort is the first option returned by getManualFilterOptions.
  * page: Optional number. If present and > 0, it is passed to searchManuals as page; otherwise page defaults to 1.
  * pageSize: Optional number. If present and > 0, it is passed to searchManuals as pageSize; otherwise pageSize defaults to a value chosen at runtime (e.g., a configuration setting) rather than a hard-coded constant in this design.
  * manualId: Optional string. If present, the page loads the corresponding manual detail by calling getManualDetails(manualId) and opens the detail view.
- Ongoing updates:
  * Any user action that changes search criteria, filters, sort, or pagination updates the corresponding URL parameters and then calls searchManuals using only values derived from the current URL state.
  * Any action that opens or closes the manual detail panel adds or removes manualId from the URL, and getManualDetails is called only when manualId is present.
  * The page never uses localStorage or other persistent client storage for state; all shareable state is encoded in URL parameters.
- Interface calls:
  * getManualFilterOptions is called once on initial load (and potentially on hard reload) to populate dynamic options for document_types, years, date_ranges, and sort_options.
  * searchManuals is called whenever any of query, documentType, dateRange, startYear, endYear, sort, page, or pageSize changes, using the current URL-derived values.
  * getManualDetails is called whenever manualId becomes present in the URL or changes.
  * initiateManualDownload is called only in response to explicit user actions (clicks) with a manualId taken from a result item or the detail panel, not automatically based on URL.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL parameters, coordinates calls to getManualFilterOptions and searchManuals, and orchestrates when manual details should be loaded via getManualDetails based on URL state.
Provides a text input for searching manuals by model name or keyword. Initializes from the query URL parameter and, on submission, updates the URL and triggers a new search via the state controller.

Manages filter options for document type and date criteria. It uses dynamic options from getManualFilterOptions and current selections derived from URL parameters (documentType, dateRange, startYear, endYear). When the user changes filters, it updates URL parameters and triggers search via the state controller.
Displays available sort options for manuals using the sort_options from getManualFilterOptions. Updates the sort URL parameter when the user selects a different option and requests a new result set.

Displays a list of manuals returned by searchManuals, including basic information such as title, product_model_name, product_name, document_type, language, version, published_at, description, file_size_mb, and is_active. Allows user to select a manual to view details or trigger a direct download action for a specific manual.

Controls navigation between pages of search results. Uses searchManuals.page, searchManuals.page_size, and searchManuals.total_count to render pagination state, and updates the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters when the user changes page.

Shows full details for a selected manual based on manualId (from URL or a clicked result). Calls getManualDetails(manualId) to fetch details, displays metadata, and exposes actions to download the PDF via initiateManualDownload and navigate to the related product page when product_id is available.
Reusable button component rendered in each result row or within the detail panel that triggers initiateManualDownload for a given manualId and handles the returned file_url or error message.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s stacked, full-width section flow: global dark double header → light breadcrumb strip → a generous white content area. Within the white area, center a page title and intro, then place a prominent, centered search form. Below that, split the main content into a two-column layout inside the 12-column grid: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider results area on the right, with sort control at the top of the results. Results list and pagination run vertically down the page. A detail panel appears as a right-side overlay when an item is selected.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s stacked, full-width section flow: global dark double header → light breadcrumb strip → a generous white content area. Within the white area, center a page title and intro, then place a prominent, centered search form. Below that, split the main content into a two-column layout inside the 12-column grid: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider results area on the right, with sort control at the top of the results. Results list and pagination run vertically down the page. A detail panel appears as a right-side overlay when an item is selected.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header and breadcrumb as in the mockup (top ~10–15% of viewport). In the main content, allocate generous vertical padding around the title/intro (about 64px above and 48px below). The search form sits with clear air above and below (24–32px). Below that, the main grid uses roughly 3/12 width for the filter panel and 9/12 for the results area. Each results item has ample vertical spacing (24–32px) between cards, and pagination has a roomy top margin (~32–48px). The detail panel, when open, consumes about 4–5/12 of the page width from the right, overlaying part of the results while preserving the header and breadcrumb.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow the low-density, high-breathing-room style: large vertical padding around key controls, no tightly packed elements. Filters are grouped with clear separation, the search form is not crowded by other controls, and each result card has distinct whitespace around its thumbnail, text, and download button. Pagination controls are centered with ample surrounding whitespace, keeping the overall page light and scannable rather than a cramped file directory.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye runs top-to-bottom from logo and nav in the dark header, to the breadcrumb, to the centered 'Manuals & Downloads' title and intro. From there, the user naturally moves down to the prominent search bar. Below the search, the layout divides: the eye first registers the left filter sidebar as a secondary control area, then is drawn to the right-side results list, scanning vertically through items. Inside each result, the bright lime download button acts like the mockup’s lime blog titles and chat FAB, pulling attention to the primary action. When a manual is opened, the detail panel on the right becomes the new focal area while still visually tied to the list on the left.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing dark double header and the light breadcrumb strip, the main content sits in a centered, max-width white canvas. A large, center-aligned page title 'Manuals & Downloads' with a short intro paragraph is given generous vertical breathing room, echoing the mockup’s spacious blog heading. Immediately beneath, a wide, centered search form spans most of the content width, clearly the primary control. Below this, the layout resolves into a two-column grid: on the left, a slim, vertically stacked filter panel; on the right, a wider column holding a top-right sort control and a clean vertical stack of result cards. Each result card uses a simple, airy composition—small thumbnail, concise manual info, and a neon-lime download button aligned to the right—mirroring the mockup’s bright blog titles as focal accents. Pagination is centered beneath the list with plenty of whitespace, and when a manual is selected, a tall detail panel slides in from the right, overlaying part of the results area while keeping the header and list context visible. The overall effect is a modern, minimal corporate library page that feels as clean and scannable as the original blog layout, with a clear vertical reading path and considered, low-density spacing throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state manager is a non-visual layer that conceptually spans the entire main content area beneath the breadcrumb. It aligns with the centered max-width content container and extends from just below the title/intro block to the bottom of the results/pagination zone. While it has no visible chrome, it governs which of the main visible regions are active: the search state, applied filters, current page of results, and whether the right-hand manual-detail-panel is open. Think of it as an invisible frame enveloping the search form, filter sidebar, sort control, results list, detail panel, and pagination, ensuring they stay synchronized as a cohesive experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; logically fixed to the main content container, spanning full width within the content column and full height of the scrollable area, orchestrating state without affecting visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is positioned directly below the page title and intro, centered within the max-width content area. It spans roughly 70–80% of the available content width (about 8–9 of 12 grid columns) and sits on a single line where possible. There is generous white space above (approximately 32px from the intro) and below (24–32px before the filter/results grid), echoing the airy hero region from the mockup. The search input itself is tall and prominent, with 16–24px internal padding, and may include a subtle cyan or lime accent on the focus ring or search icon to reflect the playful tech character from the header. On large screens, it feels like a strong horizontal bar anchoring the top of the functional area; on small screens, it scales to near-full-width while retaining clear margins from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, centered in the content column. On scroll, it moves off-screen naturally (not sticky), preserving the simple top-to-bottom reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel sits below the search form, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container, occupying roughly 3 of the 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the inner width). It forms a vertical sidebar starting about 24–32px below the search form. The panel itself is a light card or bordered block with 16–24px internal padding and clearly separated filter groups stacked from top to bottom, each with ample vertical spacing so it never feels cluttered. The top of the filter panel aligns horizontally with the top of the results area to its right, establishing a clean row. On desktop, the filter sidebar can remain in view with a mild sticky behavior: as the user scrolls the results list on the right, the filter panel stays anchored near the top of the viewport until its content is fully scrolled through. This creates a stable, secondary column of controls that contrasts with the scrolling results column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Left-aligned column within the 12-column grid, with a sticky-top behavior on larger screens (sticking just below the header/breadcrumb area). On smaller screens, it can collapse into a top-expanding panel above the results, but always with generous top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned at the top of the results area, in the right-hand column that sits beside the filter panel. Horizontally, it is right-aligned within the results column, occupying a compact horizontal strip that spans roughly 3–4 of the 9 grid columns in that area (about 30–40% of the results width). Vertically, it sits on the same baseline as the top of the filter panel, with 16–24px of breathing room above it from the search form. The control might appear as a simple dropdown or segmented toggle with a light border and subtle accent on the active state, keeping it visually light so it doesn’t compete with the search bar or results themselves. Below the sort control, there is a subtle gap (16–24px) before the first result card, delineating control from content while maintaining the clean, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results column, aligned to the top-right of that area. It scrolls off with the results list, reinforcing the top-down scan pattern when users browse many pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main right-hand column, adjacent to the filter panel. It spans approximately 9 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–75% of the inner width) and begins directly beneath the sort control with a small vertical gap (16–24px). Each result is rendered as a full-width card within this column, maintaining a single-column stack for clarity: a small image or file-type icon is placed towards the left, with the manual’s title and key metadata (product name, version, date, file size) to the right of it, and the result-download-button aligned to the far right on the same row or just below the title. Cards have generous internal padding (16–24px) and are separated from each other by 24–32px of vertical space, mirroring the mockup’s airy blog tiles. The titles or key labels can use a lime or cyan accent, echoing the bright blog titles from the mockup, aiding quick scanning. The list itself extends downward, potentially beyond the first viewport, keeping a consistent left alignment and a clear visual column. On very narrow screens, each card becomes a simple stacked block: title at top, meta beneath, and the download button full-width or right-aligned at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the right-hand column. It expands vertically as results grow, pushing pagination downward. When the manual-detail-panel is open, the visible width of each card may reduce slightly, but the list remains in place as the left-hand context column beneath the overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly below the results list, centered within the same right-hand column. There is a noticeable vertical gap (approximately 32–48px) between the last result card and the pagination row, creating a clear end to the list. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal cluster—previous/next arrows, page numbers, or a load-more button—occupying roughly 40–50% of the results column width and centered so that there is equal whitespace on both sides. The controls are visually light, using subtle borders and hover states, with a single accent color (lime or cyan) for the active or hovered page. Below the pagination, additional vertical whitespace (32–48px) ensures the bottom of the page feels open rather than cramped, echoing the mockup’s abundant negative space beneath the initial blog row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, located at the bottom of the results column. It scrolls naturally with the content and serves as a visual terminus for the page’s main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The manual detail panel appears as a tall, right-side overlay that activates when a result is selected. It docks to the right edge of the viewport, extending from just below the header/breadcrumb area down to near the bottom of the screen, with a slight margin at the bottom to preserve the airy feel. In width, it occupies roughly 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (about 35–40% of the full content width), overlapping the right portion of the results column while leaving the left side—including the filter panel and a narrow strip of the results list—visible in the background for context. Inside, content is arranged in a clean, vertical layout: document title at the top, key metadata and status below, followed by a main action area (possibly featuring the primary download button again) and detailed description or preview. Internal padding is generous (24px all around), and sections within the panel have comfortable spacing (16–24px) to maintain the low-density style. The panel surface is a slightly elevated white or very light tone with a subtle shadow, clearly distinguished from the underlying page. A close icon sits at the top-right, aligned with the panel’s internal grid. On smaller screens, this panel can transition to a full-width, full-height overlay sliding up from the bottom, still with ample internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays the right side of the main content as a slide-in panel fixed to the viewport, staying in place as the underlying page scrolls are locked or dimmed. On desktop, it maintains a fixed position relative to the viewport edges; on mobile, it behaves as a full-screen modal drawer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each results card, the download button is the key focal action. It is positioned towards the right edge of the card, typically aligned horizontally with the manual title and key metadata, or slightly below the title in a secondary row, but always anchored to the card’s right side. The button has a compact yet noticeable size, with comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) and generous vertical padding (8–12px), and uses the bright neon-lime accent color extracted from the mockup, making it pop against the otherwise neutral card background. This mirrors the role of the lime blog titles and the floating chat button in the mockup as high-contrast action points. There is at least 16px of space between the button and any adjacent text or card edge to avoid crowding. On hover, a subtle glow or border accent in lime or cyan reinforces its interactivity. On smaller screens, the button may span nearly the full width of the card at the bottom, still separated by generous top and bottom margins to keep the card feeling open.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each result card, right-aligned on desktop. It moves with the card as the user scrolls the list. On narrow screens, it shifts into a full-width or near-full-width block at the bottom of the card, but always remains the primary action within that local context.

name: Service Request
title: Service Request
description: Page to submit a technical service or on-site maintenance request for amusement machines, with dynamic service types, product selection, venue details, preferred appointment time, issue description, and contact information.

core_features: Load available service types and product categories dynamically using getServiceFormOptions when the page initializes.
Allow the user to select a service type from a dropdown populated from service_types returned by getServiceFormOptions.
Allow the user to select a product category from a dropdown populated from product_categories returned by getServiceFormOptions.
Load available models for the selected product category using getServiceModelsForCategory and populate a dependent model dropdown.
Use incoming URL parameters productCategoryCode and productId (when present) to preselect the product category and model in the form.
Collect venue details for the machine installation, including venue name and address fields.
Collect a preferred appointment date and time using date and time pickers that together provide a single preferredDateTime value.
Collect a detailed problem/issue description in a multiline input.
Collect contact details (name, phone, email) along with a required consent-to-terms checkbox.
Validate required form fields before submission and display inline error states without using any additional interfaces.
Submit the completed service request to the backend using submitServiceRequest and handle the success or failure response.
After a successful submission, display a confirmation state including the returned message and optionally the service_request_id and status.
Provide a navigation action from the confirmation state back to support.html with focus=service via a link.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Open blank service request form:
1. User navigates to service_request.html via footer navigation or a direct link.
2. On page load, the page calls getServiceFormOptions with no parameters.
3. The response populates internal state for service_types and product_categories.
4. The service type dropdown and product category dropdown are populated accordingly, while the model dropdown remains disabled or empty until a category is chosen.
5. User sees an empty form ready to be filled out.

Workflow 2 – Preselected category/model from another page:
1. User clicks a 'Request Service' action on support.html or product.html.
2. The browser navigates to service_request.html, optionally including URL parameters productCategoryCode and/or productId.
3. On page load, the page parses productCategoryCode and productId from the URL.
4. The page calls getServiceFormOptions and stores service_types and product_categories.
5. If productCategoryCode is present and matches one of the returned product_categories, the product category dropdown is preselected with that code.
6. Once the category selection is set (either by URL or default user choice), the page calls getServiceModelsForCategory(categoryCode).
7. When models are returned, the model dropdown is populated. If productId is present, the page searches the returned models for a matching product_id, and if found, preselects that model.
8. If productId is present but productCategoryCode is missing, the page can iterate over available categories from getServiceFormOptions, calling getServiceModelsForCategory for each until it finds a model with matching product_id and then preselect both category and model.
9. User reviews the preselected values, completes the remaining form fields, and proceeds to submit.

Workflow 3 – Selecting category and model manually:
1. User chooses a service type from the service type dropdown.
2. User selects a product category from the category dropdown.
3. On category change, the page calls getServiceModelsForCategory with the selected categoryCode.
4. When the model list is returned, the model dropdown becomes active and is populated with model options.
5. User selects the specific product model from this list.
6. User continues to fill in venue, appointment, issue, contact, and consent fields.

Workflow 4 – Completing and submitting the form:
1. After all required inputs are filled, the user clicks the submit button on the service request form.
2. The form component performs client-side validation (e.g., required fields, email format, consent checked). If validation fails, it sets error state and displays inline messages; submission is blocked.
3. If validation passes, the form assembles parameters: serviceType, productCategoryCode, productId (if a model was selected), venueName, venueAddressLine1, venueAddressLine2, city, stateRegion, postalCode, country, issueDescription, preferredDateTime (combined from date and time fields), contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail, consentToTerms.
4. The page calls submitServiceRequest with these parameters and sets a submitting state (e.g., disables the submit button and may show a 'Submitting' state indicator).
5. When the response returns, if success is true, the page stores service_request_id, status, submitted_at, and message from the response and transitions to a confirmation state while hiding or locking the form.
6. If success is false or the call fails, the page displays an error message in a status banner component while leaving the form data intact so the user can retry.

Workflow 5 – Post-submission confirmation and navigation back to support:
1. After a successful submitServiceRequest call, the confirmation component displays the returned message and optionally the service_request_id and status.
2. The user can choose to either close the page, or click a 'Back to Support' link.
3. On clicking the 'Back to Support' link, the browser navigates to support.html with the URL parameter focus=service, ensuring the support page opens its service-related section.
4. The service_request.html page does not attempt to re-submit the request during this navigation and retains its submitted state if the user returns via the browser back button.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters productCategoryCode and productId, then call getServiceFormOptions to initialize the form options.

When getServiceFormOptions resolves, populate the service type dropdown and product category dropdown. If a productCategoryCode from the URL matches, preselect that category and immediately trigger a call to getServiceModelsForCategory for that category.
When the user changes the service type dropdown, update internal state for selectedServiceType; this may influence validation but does not invoke any interface calls.

When the user changes the product category dropdown, update internal state for selectedCategoryCode and reset any previously selected model, then call getServiceModelsForCategory with that categoryCode to refresh the model dropdown.

When getServiceModelsForCategory returns, populate the model dropdown with the returned models; if a productId URL parameter is present, attempt to match it to one of the product_id values and set the selected model accordingly.
When the user selects a model in the model dropdown, update internal state for selectedProductId and selectedProductModelName.
When the user types into venue, address, issue description, or contact fields, update internal state for each field and clear any field-specific validation errors as the input becomes valid.
When the user selects a date and time in the appointment picker inputs, update internal state and combine them into a datetime value that will be passed as preferredDateTime to submitServiceRequest.
When the user toggles the consent-to-terms checkbox, update internal state for consentToTerms and enforce that it must be true for a successful submission.

When the user clicks the submit button, run client-side validation; if any errors are found, prevent the call to submitServiceRequest and display errors; otherwise, call submitServiceRequest with the current state values.

When submitServiceRequest resolves successfully, update internal state to reflect a submitted status, store the returned metadata, disable or hide the form fields, and show the confirmation component.
If submitServiceRequest returns an error or success is false, display an error message in a status banner, keep the form enabled, and allow the user to edit and retry submission.
When the user clicks the 'Back to Support' link in the confirmation state, perform a navigation to support.html?focus=service using standard browser navigation (no additional API calls).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the script reads the following from the query string: productCategoryCode and productId. These values are stored in initial state variables (initialCategoryFromUrl, initialProductIdFromUrl). The page then calls getServiceFormOptions to retrieve service_types and product_categories; once resolved, it populates dropdown options and validates whether initialCategoryFromUrl matches any product_categories.code. If it matches, selectedCategoryCode is set to that value; if not, selectedCategoryCode remains unset until the user chooses. Whenever selectedCategoryCode is set (either from URL preselection or manual user selection), the page calls getServiceModelsForCategory(selectedCategoryCode) and populates a models list. On the first such call, if initialProductIdFromUrl is present, the page searches the returned models for a matching product_id. If found, selectedProductId and selectedProductModelName are set to that model; if not found and productCategoryCode was not provided, the page may optionally attempt to search across other categories by iterating product_categories and calling getServiceModelsForCategory for each until a match is found. If no match is found at all, the model remains unselected and the user must choose manually. The internal form state holds all other field values (venue, address parts, date, time, issue description, contact details, consentToTerms, validationErrors, isSubmitting, submissionResult). preferredDateTime is derived when the form is submitted by combining the selected date and time values into a single datetime object or string. After submitServiceRequest returns, submissionResult stores success, service_request_id, status, submitted_at, and message, and formSubmitted is set to true. Rendering logic switches from showing the editable form to showing a confirmation panel once formSubmitted is true and submissionResult.success is true. No data is persisted using localStorage or similar mechanisms; each page load re-derives its state solely from the current URL parameters and fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Handles initial page setup: reads URL parameters, calls getServiceFormOptions, and initializes top-level form state including preselection attempts for category and model.
Displays a dropdown of available service types for the request, populated dynamically from getServiceFormOptions.service_types, and stores the currently selected service type value.

Displays a dropdown of product categories for which service can be requested, populated from getServiceFormOptions.product_categories, and manages the currently selected category including any preselection from productCategoryCode URL parameter.

Shows a dependent dropdown of product models for the selected category by calling getServiceModelsForCategory when the category changes; it also supports preselecting a model based on the productId URL parameter when a matching product_id is found.
Collects the venue details where the machine is installed, including venue name and address fields, and maintains these values in the local form state before submission.
Provides separate date and time inputs for the preferred service appointment; combines them into a single preferredDateTime value used when calling submitServiceRequest.
Collects a detailed description of the technical issue or service request, storing it as issue_description in the form state.
Captures the contact person's name, phone, and email for the service request and keeps them synchronized with the form state.
Represents the user's consent to terms; must be checked for a valid submission and its boolean value is passed as consentToTerms in submitServiceRequest.
Performs client-side validation for all required fields before calling submitServiceRequest, tracks validation errors per field, and communicates validity state to other components.
Wraps the entire service request input workflow, orchestrates validation, and on successful validation calls submitServiceRequest with the aggregated form state.

Executes the submitServiceRequest interface using the current form state, handles loading state, and stores the returned success flag, service_request_id, status, submitted_at, and message in the page state.
Displays transient messages about validation errors, API submission errors, or submission in-progress state based on form and API status.
After a successful submission, shows the confirmation message returned from submitServiceRequest along with the service_request_id and status, while preventing further edits to the submitted form.
Provides a navigation control in the confirmation state that sends the user back to the support page with focus set to the service section by navigating to support.html?focus=service.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width dark double header (global info bar + primary navigation) at the very top, followed by a shallow full-width light breadcrumb strip. Below that, the main Service Request content sits on a white background within a centered, max-width container (~1140–1200px). Inside this container, the sequence is: a subtle 'Back to Support' text link top-left, then a centered page title ('Service request') with a short intro paragraph, then (when needed) a full-width status banner, followed by the main form card. The top row of the form uses a three-column layout (service type, product category, product model) that echoes the mockup’s three-column blog row. Remaining form sections are stacked vertically with clear separation. After submission, the form area is replaced by a confirmation panel in the same central position.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width dark double header (global info bar + primary navigation) at the very top, followed by a shallow full-width light breadcrumb strip. Below that, the main Service Request content sits on a white background within a centered, max-width container (~1140–1200px). Inside this container, the sequence is: a subtle 'Back to Support' text link top-left, then a centered page title ('Service request') with a short intro paragraph, then (when needed) a full-width status banner, followed by the main form card. The top row of the form uses a three-column layout (service type, product category, product model) that echoes the mockup’s three-column blog row. Remaining form sections are stacked vertically with clear separation. After submission, the form area is replaced by a confirmation panel in the same central position.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The double header collectively occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height, with the breadcrumb strip being shallow (around 40–56px high). The main white content area has generous vertical padding: about 64px from breadcrumb to the title block, 24–32px between title, intro, status banner, and form. Inside the form, each major section (selectors row, venue details, appointment, issue description, contact details, consent + submit) is separated by 24–32px. The central form/confirmation card uses roughly 70–80% of the inner container width on desktop (max ~800–900px), leaving wide margins on left and right for breathing room, and shrinks to nearly full width with 16–24px side padding on mobile.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a deliberately low content density. Text blocks (title, intro, labels) have ample whitespace above and below. Form fields are grouped into logical sections rather than stacked tightly. The three top selectors have comfortable horizontal gutters between them, and vertical spacing around each section follows the 24–32px rhythm so no area feels crowded. Error messages and banners are kept compact but with enough padding to remain legible and not feel cramped against neighboring elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye begins at the dark header and navigation, moves down to the breadcrumb, then to the centered 'Service request' title and supporting intro text. From there it drops to any status banner, then to the top row of three selectors, which pulls the eye left-to-right across the form. The flow then progresses stepwise down through venue details, appointment, issue description, and contact details, finally landing on the bold, accent-colored submit action at the bottom-right of the form. On success, the flow leads from the header/breadcrumb to the confirmation panel title and message, then to the 'Back to Support' link as the next action.

layout.overall_description: On a dark, full-width background, the global info bar and main navigation form a double-height header that anchors the top of the page, with neon-lime and cyan accents giving a subtle tech/amusement character. Immediately below, a shallow, full-width light breadcrumb strip sits on a pale background, clearly separating navigation context from the main content. Under this strip, the page opens into a wide, white canvas where the Service Request experience is centered inside a fixed-width container. At the top of this white area, a small 'Back to Support' text link sits left-aligned, followed by a generously spaced, center-aligned 'Service request' title and a concise introductory paragraph explaining the purpose of the form. Beneath this, an optional status banner stretches the full width of the content column, and below that, a clean form card is centered, occupying about two-thirds to three-quarters of the available width. The form begins with a prominent three-column row of dropdowns—service type, product category, and product model—mirroring the three-blog-card row from the mockup. The rest of the form flows vertically in clearly separated blocks: venue details, appointment date and time, a large issue description area, and contact details. At the bottom, a left-aligned consent checkbox and a right-aligned, high-contrast submit button form a clear action bar. On successful submission, the form area is replaced by a spacious confirmation panel in the same centered position, maintaining the low-density, airy feel and guiding the user naturally to a concluding 'Back to Support' action. Throughout, generous margins, consistent spacing, and restrained use of accent colors create a modern corporate aesthetic tuned to a tech/amusement brand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This invisible initializer underpins the entire page layout. It stretches across the full viewport width and height, instantiating the global double-height dark header (info bar stacked above primary navigation), the full-width light breadcrumb strip directly below it, and the center-aligned max-width content container that sits on the white main background. Within this container, it establishes the vertical spacing rhythm: about 64px from the breadcrumb strip to the top of the main content (where the back link and page title appear), 24–32px between the title, intro paragraph, status banner, and form, and 48–64px of padding at the bottom of the main content area before the page ends. It also configures the responsive 12-column grid used to size and align the three top dropdowns and subsequent form sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural component; defines the static layout shell and responsive grid but does not render UI elements itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main service request form appears as a centered block on the white background, directly below the intro text (and any status banner), with approximately 32px of vertical gap above and below it. On desktop, it spans roughly 8 of the 12 grid columns, equating to about 70–80% of the content container width, and is horizontally centered with wide left and right margins. The form can be rendered as a subtle card with a faint border or shadow, using 24–32px internal padding on all sides to preserve the airy look. Inside, fields are arranged in vertical sections: a three-column row at the top, then full-width groups for venue details, appointment, issue description, and contact details, followed by the consent checkbox and submit button aligned along the bottom. On tablet and mobile, the form expands to almost the full container width, maintaining 16–24px side padding and stacking the previously three-column row into a vertical sequence while keeping the same section spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container within the main scrollable page; its height grows with content and validation messages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service type dropdown is the first interactive control within the form and sits in the left third of the top row of fields. On desktop, it occupies approximately 1/3 of the form’s width, aligned to the left margin of the form with a consistent right gutter (about 24px) separating it from the next column. Its label appears directly above the input, with around 8–12px of space between label and field, and about 16–24px of space above the label relative to the top of the form padding, giving it prominence. This arrangement echoes a card-like block similar in proportion to one of the blog items from the mockup but simplified to a labeled field. On smaller screens, the service type selector becomes the first full-width field, stacking above the product category and model selectors with 16–24px of vertical spacing between each.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form field; participates in a responsive 3-column row that collapses into a vertical stack on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product category dropdown sits in the center of the top row, horizontally aligned with the service type and model selectors. On desktop, it occupies the middle third of the form width, centered between left and right gutters so that all three selectors visually read as a continuous row of equal-weight blocks, echoing the mockup’s three-column blog layout. It shares the same label spacing (8–12px above the field) and vertical alignment as the other two selectors, with 24–32px of space below the row before the next section begins. On tablet and mobile, this selector appears directly under the service type selector as a full-width field, with generous vertical spacing (around 16–24px) separating it from the field above and below to maintain clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form field; part of a responsive 3-column row that reflows into a vertical stack on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product model dropdown completes the top row of selectors, positioned in the rightmost third of the form on desktop. Its left edge aligns with the right column of the 12-column grid, leaving a consistent left gutter between it and the product category selector, while its right edge lines up with the form’s right padding. The label and field spacing mirrors the first two selectors, creating a visually uniform three-block row reminiscent of the mockup’s three blog cards. A generous gap (24–32px) separates this row from the following venue details section. On narrower screens, the model selector drops below the category selector as a full-width field, with the same vertical spacing, preserving the linear top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form field; final element in a responsive 3-column row that collapses into a stacked layout on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The venue details form section sits directly beneath the top selector row, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the form’s inner area, aligning with the left and right padding of the form card. A section heading such as 'Venue details' appears at the top-left of this block, with 8–16px of space below before any internal fields (e.g., venue name, address, city) begin. Internally, the section may use a two-column arrangement for some fields, but externally it reads as a single, cohesive block occupying the full width. There is 24–32px of clear space below this section before the appointment date/time picker, maintaining the low-density, clearly segmented structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the form, expanding vertically based on the number of venue-related fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment date and time picker appears as its own block directly beneath the venue details section, with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the form’s content area, aligned with the same left and right margins as other sections.
