- Any user interaction that changes filters, sorting, or pagination updates the URL (changing only relevant parameters while preserving others), then refreshes data via getEvents. Browser navigation (back/forward) thus restores previous states because they are encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Provides controls to filter events by status (e.g., upcoming, ongoing, past, cancelled). Updates the status URL parameter and refreshes the main events list using getEvents.
event_handlers: onStatusTabClick(statusValue) -> updates URL status=statusValue, resets URL.page=1, triggers getEvents with new status
onInit() -> reads URL.status; if undefined, sets effectiveStatus='upcoming' for the initial getEvents call

components.functionality: Allows users to filter events by event_type such as trade_show, conference, webinar, open_house, or other. Synchronizes selection with the eventType URL parameter and refreshes the events listing.
event_handlers: onEventTypeChange(selectedType) -> updates URL.eventType=selectedType (or removes it when cleared), resets URL.page=1, triggers getEvents with updated eventType
onInit() -> reads URL.eventType to set the currently selected option

components.functionality: Provides a toggle control to limit the list to events that offer on-site demos. When activated, applies hasOnSiteDemos=true in both the URL and getEvents call.
event_handlers: onToggleDemosOnly(isEnabled) -> if isEnabled, sets URL.hasOnSiteDemos='true'; else removes or clears this parameter; resets URL.page=1; calls getEvents with hasOnSiteDemos mapped from URL
onInit() -> interprets URL.hasOnSiteDemos to set initial toggle state

components.functionality: Allows users to change the sort order of events (for example, by start date). Updates the sort URL parameter and refreshes the list.
event_handlers: onSortChange(sortValue) -> updates URL.sort=sortValue, resets URL.page=1, triggers getEvents with new sort option
onInit() -> reads URL.sort; if missing, applies a default sort for the first getEvents call

components.functionality: Identifies and displays a single current or next upcoming trade_show event with on-site demos, encouraging users to view details and book demos on the detail page. Uses getEvents data to select an appropriate event.
data_binding: Event (via getEvents for featured selection: event_type, status, start_date, city, country, has_on_site_demos, booth_number, event_id, title, description_excerpt)
event_handlers: onInitFeatured() -> selects featured event using either the main getEvents response or, if necessary, a focused getEvents call (e.g., status='upcoming', eventType='trade_show', hasOnSiteDemos=true, limited pageSize) to find the earliest suitable trade_show with on-site demos

components.functionality: Renders the main list of events returned by getEvents according to current filters, sort, and pagination. Delegates per-item rendering to the event-list-item component.
data_binding: Event (via getEvents.items[]: event_id, title, event_type, status, start_date, end_date, city, country, has_on_site_demos, booth_number, description_excerpt)
event_handlers: onDataLoad(filterState) -> calls getEvents({ status, eventType, hasOnSiteDemos, sort, page, pageSize }) and binds response to the list
onRetryLoad() -> re-invokes getEvents with the last used parameters if a previous call failed

components.functionality: Displays a single event record in the list, including title, date range, location, status, demo availability, and navigation to the event detail page. Highlights items where has_on_site_demos=true.
Provides pagination for the events list using data from getEvents (page, page_size, total_count). Updates the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters when users navigate pages.
event_handlers: onPageChange(targetPage) -> updates URL.page=targetPage, calls getEvents with updated page while keeping current status, eventType, hasOnSiteDemos, sort, pageSize
onPageSizeChange(targetPageSize) -> updates URL.pageSize=targetPageSize, sets URL.page=1, calls getEvents with updated pageSize

components.functionality: Coordinates the status-filter-tabs, event-type-filter, demos-filter-toggle, and sort-selector components into a single toolbar that manages the filter state and URL synchronization.
event_handlers: onInit() -> reads URL parameters and initializes all child filter components with the effective state
onFilterChange(updatedFilterState) -> merges updatedFilterState into URL, resets page=1 when relevant filters change, and triggers getEvents through events-list

components.functionality: Displays a contextual message when getEvents returns zero items for the current filter set and suggests adjusting filters.
event_handlers: onAdjustFiltersClick() -> may clear or relax certain URL filters (e.g., remove hasOnSiteDemos or eventType), then triggers getEvents via the standard flow

components.functionality: Handles and indicates loading and error states for getEvents calls, ensuring consistent user feedback and providing a retry option.
data_binding: getEvents (loading state, error state as managed by implementation)
event_handlers: onStartRequest() -> shows loading indicator when getEvents is invoked
onRequestSuccess() -> hides loading indicator when getEvents resolves successfully
onRequestError(error) -> displays an error message and shows a 'Retry' control
onRetryClick() -> re-calls getEvents with the last known filter and pagination parameters

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: full-width dark double header and light breadcrumb strip remain consistent across the site. Inside a centered max-width content container, place a prominent, center-aligned page title ('Events & Trade Shows') and intro paragraph, followed by a horizontal filters toolbar. Immediately below the filters, feature a single, wide 'featured-trade-show' promo card. Beneath that, stack the events list as a clean vertical column of event cards, ending with centered pagination controls. Conditional states (loading, empty, error) appear in the main content area, replacing or overlaying the events list region while preserving the overall structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: full-width dark double header and light breadcrumb strip remain consistent across the site. Inside a centered max-width content container, place a prominent, center-aligned page title ('Events & Trade Shows') and intro paragraph, followed by a horizontal filters toolbar. Immediately below the filters, feature a single, wide 'featured-trade-show' promo card. Beneath that, stack the events list as a clean vertical column of event cards, ending with centered pagination controls. Conditional states (loading, empty, error) appear in the main content area, replacing or overlaying the events list region while preserving the overall structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Respect the generous vertical spacing from the mockup: 48–64px between major sections (title block to filters, filters to featured promo, featured promo to events list, events list to pagination). Within each card or bar, use 16–24px internal padding. The content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (max ~1140–1200px), centered on a white background. The featured event card occupies nearly the full width of the content container and about one third to two fifths of the initial viewport height. Event list items are shorter but still generously padded, with 24–32px spacing between cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, airy layout. Each control group (filters, list items, featured card) has clear breathing room around it, with minimal visual clutter and plenty of whitespace. Filters are grouped but not crammed: two loose rows rather than a packed single row. Each event card focuses on a small set of key details (date, title, status, location, demos highlight) with ample padding and whitespace between text lines. The page favors scannability and clarity over showing as many events as possible on one screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top to bottom in a smooth sequence: global header and navigation, then light breadcrumb strip, then large center-aligned title and intro. From there, attention moves to the filters toolbar (primary interaction area), then to the bold, horizontally prominent featured trade show card. The eye then flows down the vertical list of event cards and finally to the centered pagination controls. A floating lime-accented chat button (part of the global layout, not this component list) remains fixed at the bottom-right of the viewport as a persistent secondary focal point and action.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark double header and slim light breadcrumb strip, the Events Listing page opens onto a wide, white content canvas. A centered content container, about 70–80% of the viewport width, holds a large, center-aligned page title and a one- to two-line intro paragraph, both surrounded by generous vertical whitespace. Beneath this introduction, a full-width filters toolbar stretches across the container, reading visually as a subtle control band: status tabs align to the left, while sorting and additional filters sit to the right or in a second soft row beneath, all with plenty of padding and separation. Directly below, a single, wide featured trade show promo card anchors the top of the content area, spanning nearly the full container width and standing out with a bold image and lime accent details. Further down, the main events list appears as a vertical stack of evenly spaced event cards, each full-width within the container and cleanly separated by whitespace. Pagination controls rest centered at the bottom of the list, with ample space above and below so they feel light rather than cramped. Throughout, the layout sticks to a simple vertical rhythm, using whitespace to create hierarchy and clarity; the bright lime elements (titles, badges, and the global floating chat button at the bottom-right of the viewport) provide subtle tech/playful contrast against the otherwise restrained corporate aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-filter-tabs sit inside the filters-toolbar as the primary, left-aligned control group on the top row. They are positioned directly below the page intro block, with approximately 48–64px of vertical space separating them from the intro text above. The tab set starts aligned to the left edge of the content container and extends horizontally, occupying roughly 40–60% of the container width depending on the number of tabs (e.g., Upcoming, Ongoing, Past, All). Each tab is rendered as a pill or rectangular button with 8–16px internal horizontal padding and 8px vertical padding, separated by a consistent 8–12px gap. The overall height of the tab row is relatively shallow to stay consistent with the mockup’s light, unobtrusive controls, but the active tab uses the lime accent or an underline to stand out, making this group a clear visual anchor within the toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it participates in the normal document flow as part of the filters-toolbar and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-type-filter dropdown is placed within the filters-toolbar on a second, slightly offset row beneath the status-filter-tabs, maintaining a comfortable 8–16px vertical gap. It is aligned to the left edge of the content container so that it visually lines up with the tabs above, creating a strong left-hand column of controls. The dropdown occupies a fixed-width block of roughly 220–280px (about 20–25% of the container width on desktop), with 12–16px internal horizontal padding and a down-arrow icon on the right. This control is not tightly packed against other elements; it has at least 16–24px of horizontal space before any adjacent controls such as the demos-filter-toggle, preserving the airy feel. On narrower viewports, this filter stacks directly beneath the tabs and remains full-width or nearly full-width to preserve clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters-toolbar, stacking vertically with other filters on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The demos-filter-toggle appears in the filters-toolbar to the immediate right of the event-type-filter on the same second row. It is aligned so that its left edge is separated from the dropdown by a 16–24px gap, giving each control its own breathing room. The toggle itself is compact—occupying roughly 10–15% of the container width—with a pill-style switch and a short label such as 'On-site demos only'. Vertically, it is centered within the row so its switch aligns visually with the dropdown field. The entire second row (dropdown plus toggle) sits roughly 8–16px below the status tabs, keeping the toolbar’s total height modest while still clearly separating rows. On smaller screens, the toggle stacks beneath the event-type-filter with 8–12px vertical spacing, preserving a clear top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it remains inline with other controls inside the filters-toolbar and stacks naturally on smaller breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-selector is placed on the right side of the filters-toolbar, aligning horizontally with the status-filter-tabs on the top row. It is right-aligned to the content container, such that its right edge matches the container’s right margin, creating a balanced left-to-right tension between the tabs and the sort control. The control itself is a compact dropdown or segmented control occupying about 15–20% of the container width, with 12–16px internal horizontal padding and a label like 'Sort by' or a leading icon. A horizontal gap of at least 24–32px separates it from the nearest tab on its left, preserving the low-density feel. On smaller screens, the sort-selector drops down to a row beneath the tabs (and possibly above the event-type-filter), still right-aligned but spanning a larger percentage of the width (up to 50–100%) for readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it is part of the filters-toolbar and shifts from right-aligned on the first row to its own line as space becomes constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-trade-show card sits directly below the filters-toolbar, separated by a generous 48–64px vertical gap to clearly distinguish it as a highlighted content section. It spans nearly the full width of the centered content container (about 90–100% of the container width) and is horizontally centered within it. The card is a horizontally oriented block on desktop: on the left, an image or visual occupies roughly 40–45% of the card width, while the right 55–60% contains text details (event name, dates, location, key benefits, and a prominent CTA) laid out with ample line spacing. Internal padding on all sides is 24–32px to echo the spaciousness of the main content sections. The card has a slightly elevated visual weight compared to standard events: a subtle shadow or darker outline, plus lime accents on the title, badges, or primary CTA, make it the dominant content element in the upper half of the page. On smaller screens, the card reflows into a vertical stack with the image on top and text content beneath, while preserving the generous padding and whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it sits in the main content flow between the filters-toolbar and the events-list, expanding vertically as needed while maintaining its full-width card appearance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-list occupies the main body of the page beneath the featured-trade-show card. It begins after a clear vertical gap of approximately 48px to visually separate the 'hero' event from the general listing. The list itself is a single, centered column of event-list-item cards inside the content container, each card spanning nearly the full container width (about 90–100%), with consistent left and right alignment to the featured card and filters above. Between each event card, leave 24–32px of vertical spacing so that each event reads as an independent block. The list extends downward as needed, with the white background continuing beyond the fold; there are no sidebars, so the user’s attention stays focused on this vertical stack. On larger screens, the list remains single-column to reinforce clarity and keep event details easy to scan, mirroring the mockup’s preference for low-density, straightforward layouts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it grows vertically with the number of events and scrolls within the main page flow, with pagination-controls delimiting its bottom edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event-list-item is a full-width card inside the events-list column, aligned with the edges of the featured-trade-show card and filters above. The card has 20–24px padding on all sides and is separated from neighboring cards by a 24–32px vertical gap, ensuring a generous, airy presentation. On desktop, content within each card is arranged in three visual zones: a compact left-hand column (about 15–20% of the card width) showing the date and status badge; a central content area (roughly 60–65%) containing the event title (potentially with a lime-accented word or underline), venue/location, brief description, and basic metadata; and a right-hand column (about 15–20%) reserved for key highlights such as a 'On-site demos' badge and a subtle arrow or text link indicating the detailed event page. The typography uses ample line-height, and there is clear vertical spacing (8–16px) between the date, title, and metadata. On smaller screens, these zones collapse into a top-to-bottom stack: date/status on top, followed by title and details, with demos highlight and CTA at the bottom, all still enclosed in the same padded card. The overall effect is a clean, scannable event tile that feels substantial without being dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; each card is anchored within the events-list column and reflows responsively from a three-zone horizontal layout to a vertical stack on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed directly beneath the events-list, separated from the last event-list-item by a generous 32–48px vertical gap to prevent crowding. They are horizontally centered within the content container, occupying an auto width dictated by the number of buttons (e.g., Previous, page numbers, Next). Individual pagination buttons are compact, with 8–12px horizontal padding, and spaced by 4–8px, maintaining the general low-density aesthetic without appearing overly large. The entire pagination band has at least 16px of padding above and below the clickable elements (via spacing rather than a full-width bar) so that it feels like a light touch at the bottom of the page rather than a heavy footer. On small screens, buttons wrap gracefully to a second line if necessary while remaining centered. The user’s eye naturally lands here after scrolling through the events list, signaling the end of the current batch of events.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it anchors the bottom of the events-list region and scrolls away with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters-toolbar is a horizontally oriented control band positioned directly beneath the intro text area, with a generous 48–64px vertical gap separating it from the title/intro block. It spans the full width of the centered content container (about 70–80% of the viewport, max ~1140–1200px), with internal horizontal padding of 16–24px on the left and right and 12–16px vertical padding on the top and bottom. Visually, it can be indicated by a very subtle background tint or a bottom border to echo the light breadcrumb strip above while keeping the design minimal. Within this band, the first row contains the status-filter-tabs aligned to the left and the sort-selector aligned to the right, creating a balanced horizontal composition. A second row sits just beneath (8–16px of spacing) containing the event-type-filter dropdown and demos-filter-toggle aligned toward the left, forming a clustered but spacious group of secondary filters. On narrower screens, these rows collapse into a stacked vertical sequence, with each control occupying near full width. The toolbar is the main interaction cluster on the page and anchors the top of the events area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static by default; it scrolls with the content as part of the main flow. It may optionally become sticky just below the header on larger screens, but its baseline behavior follows the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears in the same vertical region normally occupied by the events-list when no events match the current filters. It is centered within the content container and placed directly below the featured-trade-show card (if present) or the filters-toolbar if the featured card is not shown, with a 48–64px gap above it. The message consists of a simple illustration or icon, a bold headline, a short explanatory paragraph, and possibly a clear CTA to reset filters or view all events. All of these are center-aligned, with generous vertical spacing (12–24px) between elements, and the entire block occupies roughly the central third of the viewport height on desktop, with ample white space above and below. This creates a calm, uncluttered moment that clearly communicates the absence of results without feeling like an error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and conditional; it replaces the events-list content region and scrolls within the main flow when shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loading-and-error-state component is positioned immediately below the filters-toolbar, occupying the same vertical space where the featured-trade-show and events-list would normally appear. When loading, skeleton placeholders or subtle shimmering blocks mimic the general shapes of the featured card and event-list-item cards, stacked vertically with the same spacing (24–32px between skeleton rows), maintaining the page’s overall rhythm. When an error occurs, a single, prominent inline notification appears centered within the content container, slightly below the filters (32–48px gap), showing an icon, a succinct error message, and a retry action. This error message is left-aligned or center-aligned within its card but does not introduce a heavy banner; it keeps a similar padding (20–24px) and width to other cards so it visually belongs to the same layer. In all states, the header, breadcrumb, title, intro, and filters remain visible and unchanged, while the content area below them is what switches between loading, error, list, or empty states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and conditional; it occupies the main content region beneath the filters-toolbar, replacing the featured-trade-show and events-list content while active and scrolling normally with the page.

name: Event Detail & Demo Registration
title: Event Details & Demo Registration
description: Page that shows full details for a selected event and lets users book a 15-minute on-site demo slot by choosing an available date/time and submitting their contact information.

core_features: Read the eventId from the URL parameters and load event details using getEventDetails(eventId).
Display structured event information including title, dates, status, location, address, booth number, and external event website when available.
Load available demo dates and time slots for the event using getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId).
Allow users to select a demo date from the available event dates and then select an available time slot; unavailable slots are shown as disabled and cannot be selected.
Expose a primary action (e.g., 'Book a demo') that scrolls/focuses the demo registration section and, optionally, initializes default date/time selections based on availability.
Provide a demo registration form that collects required registrant information (name, email) and optional information (company, area of interest, comments).
Pre-fill the registration form’s selected date and time slot fields based on the user’s current selection; enforce that both are valid before submission.
Submit the demo registration using submitDemoRegistration with the selected eventId, date, time slot, and contact details, then show success or error feedback.
Update the page URL parameters to reflect key interaction states (selected date/time, registration success) so that state persists across refreshes and can be deep-linked.
Provide navigation back to the events listing (events.html) with a status filter set via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View event details only
1. User navigates to event_detail.html with a URL parameter eventId.
2. On page load, the page reads eventId from the URL.
3. The page calls getEventDetails(eventId).
4. The page displays event information (title, date range, location, booth number, etc.) using the response.
5. If has_on_site_demos in the event details is false, the demo booking section is either hidden or shown as disabled with an explanatory message; otherwise, the booking section remains available.

Workflow: Book a demo from the event detail page
1. User lands on event_detail.html?eventId=... and the page loads event details via getEventDetails(eventId).
2. The page calls getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId) and displays the list of dates and associated time slots.
3. User clicks the primary action (e.g., 'Book a demo') or scrolls to the demo booking section.
4. User selects a date from the available dates. The page updates its internal state and URL parameters (e.g., demoDate) to reflect the selected date.
5. The page filters or highlights the corresponding time slots for the selected date and displays their availability.
6. User selects an available time slot. The page updates internal state and URL parameters (e.g., demoTimeSlot) to reflect the selected slot.
7. User fills in the registration form fields (contact name, company name, email, area of interest, comments).
8. On form submission, the page validates that required fields are filled and that the selected date and time slot are still available in the latest getEventAvailableDemoSlots data.
9. If validation passes, the page calls submitDemoRegistration with eventId, selectedDate, timeSlot, contactName, companyName, email, areaOfInterest, and comments.
10. If the API response indicates success, the page shows a confirmation panel using the returned message and demo_registration_id, updates URL parameters to reflect registration success (e.g., registrationStatus=submitted, registrationId=[demo_registration_id]), and may reset the form or keep the summary visible.
11. If the API response indicates failure, the page shows an error message and keeps the form and selections so the user can retry.

Workflow: Return to the event page via a confirmation or marketing link
1. User returns to event_detail.html with eventId in the URL and may also have demoDate, demoTimeSlot, or registrationStatus in the URL (e.g., from a confirmation email link).
2. On load, the page calls getEventDetails(eventId) and getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId).
3. If demoDate and demoTimeSlot are present in the URL, the page attempts to preselect that date and time slot among the available slots. If they are not currently available, the page displays a notice and asks the user to choose a different slot.
4. If registrationStatus in the URL indicates a past successful registration, the page displays the confirmation panel in a non-editable state summarizing the previously registered date/time and providing instructions or an option to book an additional demo (which clears registrationStatus and re-enables the form).

Workflow: Navigate back to events listing
1. User clicks the 'Back to events' navigation control on the page.
2. The page navigates the browser to events.html with URL parameters such as status=upcoming.
3. The target page uses the status parameter to filter its events listing accordingly.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: Page load (onInit).
- Behavior: Read eventId from the URL query string. If eventId is missing or empty, display an error state and hide demo booking features. If present, call getEventDetails(eventId). After event details are retrieved and if the event supports on-site demos and is not cancelled or past (based on status), call getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId).

Interaction: Event details load success/failure
- On success: Bind the returned event details to all components displaying title, dates, location, and booth. Conditionally render demo booking elements based on has_on_site_demos and status.
- On failure: Show an error panel indicating that event details could not be loaded and provide a link back to events.html.

Interaction: Book demo button click
- Trigger: User clicks the primary 'Book a demo' action button.
- Behavior: Scroll or focus the viewport to the demo booking section, optionally auto-selecting a default date and/or time slot based on the first available entry from getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId). Update URL parameters to reflect the chosen date/time if any default is applied.

Interaction: Date selection change
- Trigger: User selects or changes the demo date in the date selector component.
- Behavior: Update internal state for selectedDate, filter visible time slots to those associated with that date, clear any previously selected time slot that is incompatible with the new date, and update the URL parameter demoDate to the selected date value. Do not call any new back-end interfaces; rely on previously loaded getEventAvailableDemoSlots data.

Interaction: Time slot selection change
- Trigger: User selects a time slot within the currently selected date.
- Behavior: Set selectedTimeSlot in local state, ensure the chosen slot is marked as selected, and update URL parameter demoTimeSlot with the selected time slot value. Attempting to select a slot where is_available is false is ignored or displays a brief notice that the slot is unavailable.

Interaction: Form field edits
- Trigger: User types into or changes any form input field (contact name, company name, email, area of interest, comments).
- Behavior: Update internal form state. Clear any previous field-specific validation errors upon change. No API interaction occurs at this step.

Interaction: Form submission
- Trigger: User submits the demo registration form (e.g., clicking a 'Confirm demo' button).
- Behavior:
  1) Validate that eventId is present, selectedDate and selectedTimeSlot have valid values corresponding to available slots, and that required contact fields meet basic format rules.
  2) If validation fails, present inline error messages and prevent submission.
  3) If validation passes, call submitDemoRegistration with parameters built from current form and selection state.
  4) While waiting for the response, disable the submit button and indicate pending state.
  5) On success, display a confirmation panel using the returned message, demo_registration_id, and created_at, update URL parameters (e.g., registrationStatus=submitted, registrationId=[demo_registration_id], demoDate, demoTimeSlot), and optionally reset or lock the form.
  6) On failure, display an error message, keep the form data intact, and re-enable the submit button.

Interaction: Confirmation panel actions
- Trigger: User clicks an action within the confirmation panel, such as 'Book another demo'.
- Behavior: Clear or reset form-specific state while preserving event details and slot availability, optionally removing registrationStatus and registrationId URL parameters and keeping or clearing demoDate/demoTimeSlot according to the desired UX. Focus the booking section so the user can start a new registration.

Interaction: Back to events navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to events' link or button.
- Behavior: Navigate to events.html with a query parameter status=upcoming (and without calling any page-specific interfaces on this page).

Interaction: URL parameter changes (without full reload, if supported)
- Trigger: The page modifies the browser’s URL (e.g., via history API) when user selections change.
- Behavior: Sync currently selectedDate, selectedTimeSlot, and registrationStatus into the query string. On subsequent soft or full page loads, initialize page state from these parameters so user context is retained.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the URL query parameter eventId. The page requires eventId to call getEventDetails(eventId) and getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId). If eventId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state and does not attempt demo slot or registration operations.

Additional URL parameters are used to represent user interaction state:
- demoDate: Represents the currently selected demo date. When present on load, the page attempts to preselect this date in the demo slot picker. The value must correspond to one of the date values returned by getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId); otherwise, the page shows a notice and clears the invalid selection.
- demoTimeSlot: Represents the currently selected time slot for the selected date. When present on load, the page attempts to preselect this time slot among the returned slots for demoDate. If the slot does not exist or is not available, the page shows a notice and requires the user to choose another slot.
- registrationStatus: Indicates whether a registration was recently submitted or confirmed (e.g., 'submitted'). On load, if registrationStatus indicates a successful registration, the page shows the confirmation component in a highlighted state. If registrationStatus is absent, the confirmation component is hidden until a new successful submission occurs.
- registrationId: When present and registrationStatus indicates success, this is used to display the reference ID within the confirmation panel but is not used to call any additional API on this page (since only the three assigned interfaces may be used).

On any change in selectedDate or selectedTimeSlot made by the user, the page updates demoDate and demoTimeSlot in the URL using the browser history API, without a full page reload. After a successful registration, registrationStatus and registrationId are appended to the URL as well. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all recoverable state comes from the URL parameters combined with live data returned by getEventDetails and getEventAvailableDemoSlots.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading eventId from the URL, calling getEventDetails and getEventAvailableDemoSlots, and exposing loading, success, and error states plus the retrieved data to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read eventId from URL query parameters. If absent, set an 'invalidEventId' error state and skip API calls.
onInit: If eventId is present, call getEventDetails(eventId) and store the resulting event details.
onEventDetailsLoaded: If the event supports on-site demos and is not cancelled or past (based on status), call getEventAvailableDemoSlots(eventId) and store the resulting dates and time_slots.
onEventDetailsError: Set an error state that will be displayed by the error-state component.
onDemoSlotsError: Set an error or fallback state for the demo booking components (e.g., cannot load slots).

components.functionality: Displays high-level event information such as title, event type, status, date range, and a 'Book a demo' trigger when on-site demos are available.
event_handlers: onRender: Read title, event_type, status, start_date, and end_date from the Event data provided by event-data-loader and display them.
onRender: Conditionally show the primary action button for demo booking only if has_on_site_demos is true and status is not cancelled.
onClickBookDemo: Notify demo-registration-toggle to scroll/focus the booking section and potentially initialize default selections.

components.functionality: Shows detailed location information for the event, including venue name, address, city, country, postal code, booth number, and a link to the official event website when available.
event_handlers: onRender: Bind location_name, address_line1, address_line2, city, postal_code, country, and booth_number from Event to the corresponding text fields.
onRender: If event_website_url is present, show an external link; otherwise, hide the external link control.
onClickExternalLink: Open event_website_url in a new browser tab or window (handled by the browser).

components.functionality: Provides the main 'Book a demo' call to action that moves the user to the demo booking controls and optionally initializes default slot selections.
event_handlers: onClick: Scroll or move focus to demo-slot-picker and demo-registration-form components.

onClick: If no demoDate and demoTimeSlot are currently set in URL or internal state, determine an initial default available date and time slot from getEventAvailableDemoSlots response and notify demo-slot-picker to select them, then update URL parameters accordingly.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose a demo date and an available time slot based on the data returned by getEventAvailableDemoSlots.
event_handlers: onInit: Read demoDate and demoTimeSlot from the URL (if present) and attempt to preselect matching entries from the getEventAvailableDemoSlots response; if invalid, show a notice and clear the invalid selections.
onDateChange: When the user selects a date, update the selectedDate state, filter available time slots for that date, clear an incompatible selectedTimeSlot, and update the URL parameter demoDate.

onTimeSlotChange: When the user selects a time slot, verify that the corresponding slot has is_available=true; if so, set selectedTimeSlot, visually mark the selection, and update the URL parameter demoTimeSlot; if not, ignore the selection and optionally show a notice.

onSlotsDataUpdate: If new slot data is reloaded (e.g., due to page refresh), ensure existing demoDate/demoTimeSlot selections still match available slots; if a previously selected slot has become unavailable, clear the selection and notify the user.
onSelectionChange: Emit the current selectedDate and selectedTimeSlot to the demo-registration-form so that the form can use them on submission.

components.functionality: Collects user contact information and ties it to the selected event, date, and time slot to submit a demo registration.
event_handlers: onInit: Read demoDate and demoTimeSlot from the URL and subscribe to selection changes from demo-slot-picker to keep the form's selectedDate and selectedTimeSlot fields synchronized.
onFieldChange: Update internal state for contact_name, company_name, email, area_of_interest, and comments, clearing any validation errors for the changed field.

onValidate: Before submission, check that eventId exists, selectedDate and selectedTimeSlot are defined and correspond to an available slot in the getEventAvailableDemoSlots response, and that required fields (at minimum contact_name and email) are non-empty and correctly formatted.

onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitDemoRegistration with eventId, selectedDate, selectedTimeSlot, contactName, companyName, email, areaOfInterest, and comments, then disable the submit button and show a pending state until the response arrives.

onSubmitSuccess: Receive the API response, display the success message and demo_registration_id in the demo-registration-confirmation component, update URL parameters (e.g., registrationStatus and registrationId along with demoDate and demoTimeSlot), and optionally lock or reset the form depending on the desired behavior.
onSubmitError: Display an error message (generic or specific based on the returned message), re-enable the submit button, and retain the user's input for correction and resubmission.

components.functionality: Displays confirmation details after a successful demo registration, including the selected date/time and the registration reference ID.
event_handlers: onInit: Read registrationStatus and registrationId from the URL. If registrationStatus indicates a completed submission, show the confirmation panel; otherwise, hide it by default.

onSubmitSuccess: When notified by demo-registration-form, update the displayed information (selectedDate, timeSlot, contactName, demo_registration_id, created_at, and message) to reflect the most recent submission and make the confirmation panel visible.

onClickBookAnotherDemo: Hide or collapse the confirmation panel, clear registrationStatus and registrationId from the URL, optionally reset form fields in demo-registration-form, and keep the current demoDate/demoTimeSlot or clear them based on the desired UX.

components.functionality: Provides a direct way for users to return to the events listing filtered by upcoming events.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to events.html with the URL parameter status=upcoming.

components.functionality: Shows error messages when event details or demo slots cannot be loaded, or when eventId is missing or invalid.
event_handlers: onInit: If event-data-loader reports an 'invalidEventId' state, render a message indicating the event cannot be found and provide the navigation-back-to-events link.
onEventDetailsError: Show an error message indicating that event details could not be loaded, hide the demo booking components, and display navigation-back-to-events.
onDemoSlotsError: Show a message indicating that demo slots cannot be loaded while still displaying event details. Leave the demo-registration-form disabled or hidden in this scenario.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top-to-bottom structure: global dark double header (info bar + nav) at the very top, then the light breadcrumb strip full-width. Below that, the Event Detail page content sits in a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) on a white background. Inside this container, content flows vertically: a subtle 'Back to events' navigation link, then a prominent event header summary, then a location/details block. Beneath the event information, a clearly separated demo registration section begins with a toggle/section header, followed by the slot picker, then the registration form, with a confirmation state that occupies the same area as the form when active. Error and loading states overlay or replace this central column as needed. A global floating chat button remains fixed at the bottom-right, above the content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s stacked, top-to-bottom structure: global dark double header (info bar + nav) at the very top, then the light breadcrumb strip full-width. Below that, the Event Detail page content sits in a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) on a white background. Inside this container, content flows vertically: a subtle 'Back to events' navigation link, then a prominent event header summary, then a location/details block. Beneath the event information, a clearly separated demo registration section begins with a toggle/section header, followed by the slot picker, then the registration form, with a confirmation state that occupies the same area as the form when active. Error and loading states overlay or replace this central column as needed. A global floating chat button remains fixed at the bottom-right, above the content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main container is centered and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens, adhering to the 12-column grid with generous side gutters. The dark double header uses about the top 10–15% of the viewport height, with the breadcrumb as a shallow strip just below it. Within the white content area, major sections (event header, location block, registration section) are separated vertically by 48–64px. Each interactive block (location, slot picker, form, confirmation, error) behaves like a card with 16–24px internal padding. The demo registration section occupies roughly the middle third to half of the initial viewport height, leaving comfortable whitespace below for scrolling. The slot picker internally echoes the mockup’s three-column rhythm where possible by arranging date/time options in up to three columns on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains low visual density: generous whitespace above and below the event header summary, loose spacing between the location block and the registration section, and clear breathing room between the slot picker and the form. Fields in the registration form are arranged in a single column to keep the layout light. The slot picker uses small but well-separated slots with ample horizontal and vertical gaps, inspired by the airy three-blog-item row. No section feels crowded; each major block has its own ‘island’ of whitespace around it to support scannability and reduce cognitive load.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a clear vertical hierarchy: logo and main navigation in the dark header → breadcrumb strip → left-aligned 'Back to events' link → large, central event title and summary → supporting location details → prominent 'Book a demo' toggle as a cue to action → slot picker area where neon-lime accents draw attention to selectable slots → registration form where input fields guide the user downward → confirmation panel in the same area upon success. Parallel to this, the floating lime chat button at the bottom-right serves as a secondary focal endpoint and alternative action. When loading or error states appear, they dominate the central column to make the system status immediately obvious without disrupting the overall structure.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark, neon-accented double header and the pale breadcrumb bar, the Event Detail & Demo Registration page unfolds in a single, centered column on a clean white background. The content sits within a max-width container, visually anchored in the middle of the screen with wide left and right margins. At the top of this column, a subtle 'Back to events' link hugs the left edge, acting as a quiet breadcrumb companion. A large, spacious event header block follows, with the event title and key information presented prominently and comfortably, echoing the mockup’s 'Corporate blog' title treatment. Directly beneath, a lightly framed location block adds detail without crowding the page. After a generous vertical gap, a distinct demo registration section starts with a neon-tinged toggle/section header, clearly signaling the interactive booking area. Expanding this section reveals a slot picker that arranges available demo times in a clear, grid-like layout, visually reminiscent of the mockup’s three-column blog row. Below that, a roomy, single-column form invites the user to complete their details. Upon submission, a confirmation panel appears in place of the form, highlighted with subtle lime accents. Throughout, ample whitespace ensures each block feels separate yet connected, while a floating circular chat button remains fixed in the bottom-right corner of the viewport, hovering above the white canvas as a constant, bright secondary call to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as an overlay specifically over the main white content area beneath the breadcrumb. It spans the full width of the central content container (approximately 70–80% of the viewport on large screens) and extends from just below the breadcrumb strip down to cover the event header, location block, and registration section. The background of the overlay is a slightly translucent white or very light grey, allowing the underlying structure to be faintly visible while clearly indicating a loading state. At the center of this overlay, both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible content region, a compact spinner or loading indicator sits with generous padding around it (at least 48px on all sides), maintaining the low-density feel. On smaller screens, the overlay continues to cover the full width of the viewport below the header and breadcrumb, always centered. When active, this loader dims interaction with the underlying components but respects the fixed positioning of the global floating chat button, which remains visible above it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main content column as an absolute-positioned layer within the page body (below the header and breadcrumb), centered both horizontally and vertically, and disappears once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event header summary is the first substantive block inside the white content area. It is positioned just below the breadcrumb bar with a comfortable top margin of about 48–64px, following the spacious pattern from the mockup. The block itself is centered within the max-width container and spans roughly 60–70% of the container’s width, leaving visible white margins to the left and right. The main event title is large and visually dominant, akin to the 'Corporate blog' heading in the mockup, and is either center-aligned or left-aligned within this central block; in either case, the entire block remains centered relative to the page. Below the title, a short set of key details (date, time, event type) appears in a smaller, lighter style, with 16–24px vertical spacing separating lines, maintaining an airy feel. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of this summary block and the top of the next section (the event location block). On mobile, this block becomes nearly full-width with consistent internal padding, while preserving generous vertical spacing above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow, anchored near the top of the main content column with consistent vertical margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event location block sits immediately below the event header summary, separated by approximately 24–32px of whitespace. It appears as a lightly outlined or softly shadowed card spanning roughly 70–80% of the content container’s width, remaining centered with equal left and right margins, reinforcing the linear vertical flow. Inside the card, 16–24px padding on all sides creates a calm, easy-to-scan layout for address details, venue name, and possibly transport information. On wider screens, the internal content can imply a two-column structure (for example, address and practical details on the left, a small map thumbnail or icon on the right), but the overall block still reads as a single, unified section. There is a clear vertical separation of about 48px between the bottom of this block and the start of the demo-registration-toggle, ensuring the event information feels complete before the user encounters the booking interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component in the main column, maintaining its centered, card-like appearance and scaling to full width minus margins on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The demo registration toggle is the visual and functional gateway into the booking area. It sits below the event location block with a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px, marking the transition from informational content to interactive content. It spans the full width of the content container (matching the width of the event location block) and is centered horizontally. Visually, it behaves like a slim section header bar or pill-shaped card with 16–20px vertical padding, a subtle outline or faint background fill, and a neon-lime accent on the text or left-edge indicator line to match the playful corporate tech vibe. The text (e.g., 'Book a 15-minute on-site demo') is center-aligned within the bar on large screens, making it easy to spot. An optional chevron or plus/minus icon is aligned right within the bar to indicate its collapsible nature. When collapsed, only this bar is visible; when expanded, the slot picker and form appear directly underneath, pushing content downward. On smaller screens, it remains full-width, with the same breathing room around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the flow that acts as a collapsible section header; expanding or collapsing it reveals or hides the slot-picker and form directly below within the same vertical column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the registration toggle is expanded, the demo slot picker appears directly beneath it, with a small but clear gap of about 16–24px. The slot picker forms a visually distinct card, spanning roughly the same width as the toggle (70–80% of the content container, centered) and using 16–24px internal padding. Within this card, available dates and times are laid out in a structure that nods to the mockup’s three-column blog row: on larger screens, date groups or time slots may be arranged in up to three equal columns across the width, with generous horizontal gutters (16–24px) and ample vertical spacing (16–24px) between rows, giving a light, grid-like feel. Each individual slot appears as a small pill or tile with clear, lime-accented selection states, but overall the density remains low, avoiding overcrowding. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to one or two columns, but the spacing between options remains consistent with the spacious design language. There is a vertical gap of 32–40px between the bottom of the slot picker card and the top of the registration form card that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element contained within the expanded registration section; it grows vertically as needed to accommodate available slots, while respecting the overall centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The demo registration form sits directly below the slot picker, separated by about 32–40px of whitespace to clearly distinguish selecting a time from entering personal details. It spans the same centered width as the slot picker and location block, maintaining visual alignment. The form is presented as a light card with subtle border or shadow, and 24px internal padding on all sides, preserving the airy feel. Form fields are arranged in a single vertical column to keep the layout clean and scannable, each field stretching nearly the full width of the card with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. Headline or instructional text appears at the top of the form with a 16–24px gap before the first input. The primary submit button sits at the bottom right of the card’s content area on larger screens (or full-width on smaller screens), with at least 24px of whitespace beneath it, so the card doesn’t feel cramped at the bottom. The form as a whole occupies roughly one third to half of the initial viewport height, leaving comfortable white space below for continued scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component that appears only when the registration section is expanded and no confirmation is currently shown; it is replaced or visually overlaid by the confirmation panel upon successful submission.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel occupies the exact spatial footprint of the demo registration form once a submission is successful. It appears in the same position in the vertical flow, directly beneath the demo slot picker (or beneath the toggle if the slot picker is not needed after confirmation), maintaining the centered width and alignment with the other cards. The panel is styled like a feedback card with 24px internal padding, but with more prominent use of neon-lime or cyan accents—for example, a lime accent strip along the left edge or a soft lime-tinted background—to immediately signal success while preserving the minimalist look. The content is compact: a confirmation headline, a brief description of the booked slot, and any next steps, all arranged with 16–24px vertical spacing. The overall height of the panel is moderate, leaving generous whitespace above and below so the success state feels deliberate and uncluttered. The user can see that it cleanly replaces the form, reinforcing the completion of the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed block that replaces (or overlays) the registration form within the same area of the layout after a successful submission; it remains static within the document flow and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Back to events' navigation link sits at the very top of the main content column, just below the breadcrumb strip and above the event header summary. It is left-aligned with the content container’s left edge, giving it a subtle, utility-oriented presence akin to a secondary breadcrumb. The link is a simple inline text element, optionally with a left-facing arrow icon, and occupies minimal vertical space (about 24–32px of height including line spacing). There is approximately 24–32px of whitespace between the bottom of this link and the top of the event header summary, ensuring the main event title still feels like the primary focal point. On smaller screens, the link remains at the top of the column, spanning only as wide as its text, with plenty of blank space to its right to maintain the airy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element in the normal flow, always positioned directly under the breadcrumb and above the event header summary; it scrolls away with the content as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs (such as the event failing to load), the error state panel replaces the typical event content in the main column. It is centered horizontally within the content container and limited to about 50–60% of the container’s width on large screens, giving it a compact but clear presence with wide margins on either side. Vertically, it appears beneath the breadcrumb and 'Back to events' link area, with roughly 64px of top margin to echo the padding used for the event header summary. The panel itself resembles a feedback card with 24px internal padding and a subtle border or soft tinted background indicating an error (while still fitting the corporate aesthetic). Inside, an error icon, concise error message, and recovery actions (such as a 'Back to events' button) are arranged in a vertical stack with 16–24px spacing between elements. The page beneath remains largely empty white space, emphasizing the central error message without introducing clutter. On mobile, the card expands to nearly full width with consistent padding, still centered in the vertical space above the fold as much as possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed block that takes the place of normal event content in the central column; it sits in the standard document flow but becomes the main visible content when active, with other detail and registration components hidden or not rendered.

name: Financing & Calculator
title: Financing & Calculator
description: Page for viewing financing information, estimating monthly payments for amusement machines, comparing plan options, and submitting a short financing inquiry.

core_features: Load and display financing overview content, highlights, and FAQs using getFinancingOverview.
Display a financing calculator where users enter an equipment price and select a currency.
Fetch available financing plan options for the selected currency using getFinancingPlanOptions.
Calculate estimated monthly payments for all available plans based on the entered equipment price using calculateFinancingPayments.
Show a comparison view of all returned plans side by side, including term, interest rate, and estimated monthly payment.
Automatically highlight the plan with the lowest estimated monthly payment in the comparison view.
Allow the user to select a preferred plan directly from the comparison view.
Display and submit a short financing inquiry form (name, company, email, optional phone) for the selected plan using submitFinancingInquiry.
Prefill the calculator amount from the optional URL parameter price when the page is accessed from other pages.
Provide a financing FAQ section which includes a link to contact.html for contacting the financing team.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Estimating payments from a product or home page link
1. User clicks an 'Estimate payments' link on product.html or index.html which navigates to financing.html with a price URL parameter.
2. On financing.html load, the page reads the price parameter and pre-populates the equipment price field in the calculator.
3. The page determines or uses a default currency and calls getFinancingPlanOptions with that currency.
4. After the financing plans are loaded, the page calls calculateFinancingPayments with the pre-filled equipment price, selected currency, and the list of term months from the retrieved plans.
5. The comparison table displays all plans with their estimated monthly payments, and the lowest payment is highlighted.
6. The user can adjust the price or currency; the page updates the URL parameters and re-runs calculateFinancingPayments to show updated results.

Workflow 2: Exploring financing options from site navigation
1. User navigates to financing.html via the main navigation or footer link without a price parameter.
2. On load, the page calls getFinancingOverview to display general financing information and FAQs.
3. The calculator shows empty or default values; the user manually enters an equipment price.
4. The user optionally changes the currency; the page calls getFinancingPlanOptions for the selected currency.
5. The user triggers calculation (e.g., by clicking a 'Calculate' button); the page calls calculateFinancingPayments using the entered price, selected currency, and term months corresponding to the loaded plan options.
6. The comparison view updates with calculated monthly payments and highlights the lowest payment.

Workflow 3: Selecting a plan and submitting a financing inquiry
1. After reviewing the comparison table, the user clicks a 'Select plan' action on one of the plans.
2. The selected plan is stored in the page state and reflected in the URL via a selected_plan_id parameter (and optionally selected_term_months), so the chosen plan is preserved on refresh or sharing.
3. The inquiry form becomes visible or is pre-populated with the selected plan’s term, currency, equipment price, and estimated monthly payment.
4. The user enters their name, company (optional), email, and phone (optional).
5. On form submission, the page calls submitFinancingInquiry with the selected plan ID, equipment price, currency, selected term, estimated monthly payment, and contact details.
6. If the API returns success, the page shows a confirmation state and may display the financing_inquiry_id and status returned in the response.

Workflow 4: Getting additional help via contact page
1. User scrolls to the FAQ section retrieved via getFinancingOverview.
2. User clicks a 'Contact financing team' link associated with the FAQ content.
3. The page navigates to contact.html (without additional parameters, or with page-specific parameters if later defined by the site) as defined in the outgoing navigation rules.
interactions: On page load: read URL parameters (price, currency, selected_plan_id, selected_term_months) and initialize the calculator and selection state accordingly.
On page load: call getFinancingOverview and bind intro_text, highlights, and faq to the overview and FAQ components.
On page load or when currency changes: call getFinancingPlanOptions with the selected currency and update the available plan options in the calculator and comparison components.

On equipment price change: update the internal state and URL parameter price; when the user triggers calculation, call calculateFinancingPayments with the current price, currency, and term months derived from the active plan options.

On calculation response from calculateFinancingPayments: bind the returned calculations array to the comparison view, including plan_id, term_months, interest_rate_annual_percent, and estimated_monthly_payment.

On comparison view render: determine which calculation has the lowest estimated_monthly_payment and mark that row as highlighted in the component state (no static assumptions about which plan is best).

On plan selection (e.g., clicking 'Select' on a row): update the selected plan state (plan_id and term_months), sync selected_plan_id and selected_term_months to the URL, and populate the financing inquiry form with the corresponding calculation values.
On financing inquiry form input: live-update local state for contact_name, company_name, email, phone, and keep these values independent of URL parameters to avoid exposing personal data in the URL.

On financing inquiry form submission: call submitFinancingInquiry with the current selected plan ID, equipment price, currency, selected term, estimated monthly payment, and contact details; then handle success or error states based on the returned success flag and message.
On 'Contact financing team' link click in the FAQ section: navigate to contact.html as defined in outgoing_connections.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to control and preserve non-personal state across reloads and navigation. Supported parameters include: price (numeric equipment price), currency (currency code passed to getFinancingPlanOptions and calculateFinancingPayments), selected_plan_id (identifier of the plan chosen from the comparison table), and selected_term_months (numeric term in months for the chosen plan). On initial load, the page reads these parameters and sets the calculator and selection state. If price is present, the equipment price field is prefilled; if currency is present, it determines which plans are requested from getFinancingPlanOptions; if selected_plan_id and selected_term_months are present and match a plan in the currently loaded options or calculations, that plan is preselected in the UI and the inquiry form is pre-bound to it. When the user changes the equipment price or currency, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (excluding personal data fields) and, when calculations are triggered, calls calculateFinancingPayments with the updated values. When the user selects a plan, the selected_plan_id and selected_term_months parameters are written or updated in the URL. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL preserves the calculator inputs and chosen plan, while personal contact details for the inquiry form remain only in page state and are not stored in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Loads and displays general financing overview text, key highlights, and a summary description for financing and leasing programs.
Displays a list of financing FAQs and includes a contextual link to the contact page for additional questions.
Provides input fields for equipment price and currency selection, initializes these from URL parameters when available, requests applicable financing plans, and triggers payment calculations.

Shows all financing plans returned for the selected currency along with their calculated estimated monthly payments, highlights the plan with the lowest payment, and allows the user to select a preferred plan.

Displays a concise summary of the currently selected financing plan (term, interest rate, estimated monthly payment, currency, and equipment price) to confirm the user’s choice before submitting an inquiry.
Collects contact details (name, company, email, optional phone) and submits a short financing inquiry for the currently selected plan and calculator values.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, stacked section flow as the mockup: global dark double header (info bar + nav) at the top, then the light breadcrumb strip. Below that, a wide white main canvas contains a centered page title and intro (as seen in the blog mockup). Under this intro block, the core financing content is arranged in a linear sequence: first an overview section, then a primary calculator band (calculator on the left, selection summary on the right), followed by a full-width plan comparison table, then an FAQ section, and finally a financing inquiry form as the closing call-to-action. All sections sit inside a centered max-width content container (around 1140–1200px) on a white background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, stacked section flow as the mockup: global dark double header (info bar + nav) at the top, then the light breadcrumb strip. Below that, a wide white main canvas contains a centered page title and intro (as seen in the blog mockup). Under this intro block, the core financing content is arranged in a linear sequence: first an overview section, then a primary calculator band (calculator on the left, selection summary on the right), followed by a full-width plan comparison table, then an FAQ section, and finally a financing inquiry form as the closing call-to-action. All sections sit inside a centered max-width content container (around 1140–1200px) on a white background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical spacing mirrors the mockup: the title/intro area uses generous top and bottom padding (about 64px each) to feel like a hero-lite. Each major section (overview, calculator band, comparison, FAQ, inquiry form) is separated by 48–64px of vertical space, preserving clear breaks. Within components, padding is generally 16–24px to keep content airy. Above the fold on desktop, the overview/title plus calculator band occupy most of the visible height, mirroring how the blog title and first row of posts fill the screen in the mockup. The right-hand selection summary is narrower, occupying about 40% of the main content width, while the left-hand calculator takes about 60%. All other components span the full content width within the central container.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low density similar to the blog mockup. Each section focuses on a single idea: the overview is primarily text, the calculator shows only essential controls with generous spacing between fields, the comparison table is simplified and not overly packed, and the FAQ uses an accordion/list with clear spacing between items. Forms and tables avoid tight packing: form fields are stacked with at least 16px vertical spacing; table rows have ample row height and horizontal padding. Overall, there is more whitespace than content in each viewport slice to keep the page scannable and approachable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves down in a clear vertical rhythm, just like in the mockup. From the logo and dark navigation, it flows to the light breadcrumb, then lands on the centered 'Financing & Calculator' title and intro. From there, emphasis shifts slightly left to the financing calculator controls (primary task), then to the right-hand selection summary which uses lime accents to highlight the estimated monthly payment and selected plan. The next visual pull is the full-width comparison table, where column headers and key values use the neon-lime accent to guide scanning across options. Below that, the FAQ section gives reassurance and answers, and finally the inquiry form presents a clear next step. A floating, lime-accented help/chat button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport, detached from the main flow but always visible, echoing the mockup’s floating action button and acting as a secondary focal point.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark double header and narrow breadcrumb strip, the page opens into a broad, white content area framed by a centered container about 1140–1200px wide. At the top of this canvas, the 'Financing & Calculator' title sits in the center, with a concise, one-paragraph explanation directly beneath it, surrounded by generous vertical whitespace so it feels like a calm, corporate hero area. Scrolling just slightly reveals a two-column band: on the left, a clean, card-like financing calculator with a few well-spaced controls; on the right, a narrower summary card that uses bright lime accents to emphasize the estimated monthly payment and selected plan. Beneath this band, the layout returns to a single column: a full-width, airy plan comparison table that spans the container and uses lime highlights across column headings and key numbers, followed by an FAQ section with vertically stacked, easy-to-scan questions on a light background. At the bottom, a prominent yet uncluttered financing inquiry form sits within a subtly differentiated panel, giving users a clear, final action. Throughout the page, large vertical gaps and consistent side margins echo the blog mockup’s minimal, modern rhythm, while a small floating lime chat/help button hovers in the bottom-right corner, always available but never competing with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing overview section begins directly under the breadcrumb strip, within the main white content area. It occupies the full width of the central content container (roughly 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at about 1140–1200px) and is horizontally centered on the page. The top of this section has generous padding from the breadcrumb (about 64px), so the title and intro feel visually separated from the navigation. The page title 'Financing & Calculator' is center-aligned and spans about 50–60% of the container width, visually anchored in the middle. Below it, with about 16–24px of vertical spacing, a single paragraph of intro text is also center-aligned but constrained to a slightly narrower text column (around 50% of the container width) to maintain readability and mirror the mockup’s centered blog intro. There is an additional 48–64px of whitespace below the intro paragraph before any subsequent content, creating a clear, airy block that establishes the page’s purpose before the more interactive elements appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; participates in normal document flow as the first main content block beneath the breadcrumb.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing calculator controls sit immediately below the overview section, separated by about 48px of vertical space. On desktop, they form the left half of a two-column band inside the same centered content container. Using an implied 12-column grid, the calculator occupies approximately 7 of 12 columns (around 55–60% of the container width) and is left-aligned, starting flush with the container’s left edge. The controls are placed inside a card-like panel with a white background, subtle shadow or soft border, slightly rounded corners, and internal padding of 24px to maintain the spacious feel. The section’s heading (if present) appears at the top-left of the card, followed by stacked form controls: machine price, down payment, term length, interest rate, and any option selectors. Each label-and-input pair stacks vertically with 16–24px of spacing between rows, and where appropriate (e.g., down payment vs. term) two related inputs may share a single row using a 6/6 split, with enough horizontal padding to prevent crowding. A primary action button (such as Calculate) sits at the bottom-left of the card with ample top margin (24–32px), aligned to the left to match form alignment. On tablet and mobile, the calculator card expands to span 100% of the container width and the internal layout becomes strictly single-column for all fields, preserving comfortable spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in vertical flow but part of a two-column section on larger screens; it reflows to full-width stacked above the summary on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan selection summary appears alongside the calculator as the right-hand column within the same visual band. On desktop, it uses roughly 5 of 12 columns (about 40–45% of the container width) and is aligned to the right edge of the central container, starting on the same vertical line as the top of the calculator card. A horizontal gap of about 32px separates it from the calculator, echoing the generous gutters between blog cards in the mockup. The summary sits in a compact, card-like panel with a white background, subtle border or shadow, rounded corners, and 24px internal padding. The layout inside the card is vertical: at the top, a small, muted label such as Selected plan, followed by a more prominent, lime-accented plan name or estimated monthly payment value, which acts as a focal point. Key figures (monthly payment, total financed amount, term length) are displayed as a short list with 12–16px vertical spacing, left-aligned, and may use lime or cyan accents for values. Below these, a small selector element allows switching between recommended or alternative plans (for example, pill-style toggles or a minimal dropdown), with sufficient spacing so it does not crowd the summary text. A secondary action such as Lock this quote or Proceed appears at the bottom, aligned left or full-width, but remains visually lighter than the main calculator’s action button. On tablet and mobile viewports, this card drops below the calculator, becoming a full-width block with 48px vertical separation and the same internal card padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the summary card can use a sticky behavior within its column, remaining pinned near the top of the viewport as the user scrolls through the calculator and comparison area, stopping before overlapping the next major section. On smaller screens it behaves as a normal, non-sticky block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan comparison component is positioned directly below the calculator/summary band, with about 64px of vertical space separating it from the bottom edge of those cards. It uses the full width of the central content container, stretching from the left to right padding edges, and is centered within the white background. A section heading such as Compare financing plans is left-aligned at the top of this section, with a short descriptive subtitle beneath it, both confined to approximately 60% of the container width to mirror the text widths used higher on the page. Below the heading area, the comparison table fills most of the remaining horizontal space. On desktop, the table likely consists of a first column for features and 2–4 columns for different plans; each column is given generous horizontal padding, and row heights are relatively tall (at least 48px) so the table does not feel dense. Column headers use bold text with lime accents on plan names, echoing the bright blog titles in the mockup, guiding the eye horizontally across the options. Light horizontal dividers separate rows; vertical lines are minimal or very subtle to keep the table airy.
