On smaller screens, the table becomes horizontally scrollable within the container or transforms into stacked, card-based plan comparisons: each plan is presented as a block spanning the container width, with key features listed below, and vertical spacing between plan cards of 24–32px. The bottom of this section has about 48–64px margin before the FAQ section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; spans the full content width within the container. On small screens, the table area allows horizontal scrolling or switches to stacked cards, but remains within normal page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ section sits below the comparison table, separated by a clear vertical gap of roughly 48–64px. It continues within the same central content container but feels slightly more compact to reflect its supporting role. At the top, an FAQ heading (for example, Financing FAQs) is left-aligned and may be slightly smaller than the main page title but still prominent. Directly below, an optional brief line of intro text reassures users (for example, quick answers about payments and approval) and is limited to around 60% of the container width to maintain a clean rag. The FAQ list itself is centered within the container but constrained to approximately 70–80% of its width on large screens, leaving wider side margins to enhance readability and echo the mockup’s spacious composition. Each FAQ item is presented as a full-width row within that narrower column, typically as an accordion: the question text is bold and left-aligned, with an expand/collapse icon on the right. Each row has ample vertical padding (16–20px), and there is 8–12px of spacing between rows so that even collapsed questions do not feel crowded. Expanded answers appear below their questions with a slightly indented or lighter-weight text, separated by 8–12px from the question line. The bottom of this section has a 48–64px margin before the inquiry form panel, ensuring the FAQ and form feel like distinct steps in the user journey.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; the FAQ items expand and collapse in place but the section itself remains in the normal vertical document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing inquiry form anchors the bottom of the main content area and acts as the final, focused call-to-action. It appears below the FAQ section with a generous 64px of vertical separation. To differentiate it subtly from the rest of the white canvas while staying aligned with the minimal aesthetic, the form sits within a full-width, very light off-white or soft grey panel that spans edge-to-edge of the viewport, while the form content itself remains centered inside the same 1140–1200px container. This panel has substantial vertical padding (64–80px top and bottom) to echo the openness of the initial title area. Inside the container, the layout on desktop is a two-column structure: the left column (about 5 of 12 grid columns) holds a short, reassuring heading and supporting text explaining what happens after submission; the right column (about 7 of 12 columns) contains the actual form. The heading on the left is left-aligned and confined to around 70% of that column’s width, with 16–24px spacing to its descriptive paragraph. The form on the right is arranged in a vertical stack: name, contact details, basic business info, and one or two key financing questions (for example, preferred term or budget range). Fields are stacked with at least 16px vertical spacing; related, short fields (such as first/last name) can share a row on desktop using a 6/6 split. The primary submit button sits at the bottom-right of the form column, separated by 24–32px of top margin from the last field, and visually echoes the calculator’s primary button style. On tablet and mobile, the two columns stack: the explanatory text appears first, spanning the full container width, followed by the full-width form, with 32–40px of vertical separation between them and generous side padding to maintain airflow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains at the bottom of the main content flow within a full-width background band that visually differentiates it from preceding white sections.

name: Newsletter Signup
title: Newsletter Signup
description: Page for subscribing to the company newsletter, selecting market segments and email frequency, and submitting consent to the privacy policy.

core_features: Display a newsletter signup form with fields for full name, email address, market segment checkboxes, email frequency dropdown, optional comments, and a required privacy policy consent checkbox.
Load available market segment options and email frequency options dynamically using the getNewsletterOptions interface, including the default email frequency.
Prefill the email input when an email URL parameter is present (e.g., when linked from quotes.html or contact.html).

Allow users to select one or more market segments via checkboxes rendered from getNewsletterOptions.market_segments, including segments whose values correspond to family entertainment centers and bowling centers when provided by the API.

Provide an email frequency dropdown populated from getNewsletterOptions.email_frequencies and preselect the option whose value matches getNewsletterOptions.default_frequency (which can be configured to "monthly" to meet the requirement).
Submit the completed form to submitNewsletterSubscription with the entered name, email, selected market segments, chosen email frequency, comments, and privacy consent state.
Show inline success or error feedback based on the response from submitNewsletterSubscription, and optionally persist submission status in URL parameters so the state can be restored on page reload.
Allow navigation to privacy.html via a clickable link next to the privacy policy consent checkbox.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: New visitor from main navigation
1. User opens newsletter.html without URL parameters (via header/footer or internal call-to-action link).
2. On page load, the newsletter options loader calls getNewsletterOptions.
3. When the response arrives, the form is initialized: market segment checkboxes and email frequency dropdown are populated from the response; the default frequency option is preselected based on default_frequency.
4. User enters their full name and email address.
5. User selects one or more market segments by checking the relevant boxes (including, if available, those representing family entertainment centers and bowling centers).
6. User optionally enters comments or preferences.
7. User checks the privacy policy consent checkbox; they may click the nearby link to open privacy.html for more information.
8. User clicks the submit button; the form validates required fields and that privacy consent is checked.
9. On valid submission, the form calls submitNewsletterSubscription with the current form values.
10. If the API returns success, the page displays a success confirmation message and may update the URL (e.g., adding a success status parameter) so the confirmation can be restored if the page is reloaded.
11. If the API indicates failure, the page displays an error message using the returned message property and keeps the form values so the user can correct and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Visitor referred from quotes.html or contact.html with prefilled email
1. User clicks a "Sign up for our newsletter" link from quotes.html or contact.html that navigates to newsletter.html?email=<encoded-email>.
2. On page load, the page parses the email URL parameter and stores it as the initial email field value.
3. The newsletter options loader calls getNewsletterOptions and initializes the market segments and email frequency controls as in Workflow 1.
4. The email input is prefilled with the value from the URL parameter; the user can edit it if needed.
5. The user completes remaining fields (full name, segments, comments, privacy consent) and submits.
6. Submission and success/error handling proceed identically to Workflow 1.

Workflow 3: Page reload after submission
1. After a successful submission, the page may navigate or push a URL like newsletter.html?status=success&email=<encoded-email> (the exact parameter names and values are controlled by the implementation, not hardcoded in the UI).
2. When the page loads and detects a status parameter representing a successful subscription, it displays the confirmation panel and may optionally hide or minimize the form.
3. If the user navigates back to the form state (or if status indicates an error), the page uses the URL parameters and/or in-memory state to restore the appropriate view while still using getNewsletterOptions to populate option lists.
interactions: Form initialization: On initial page render, an initialization handler triggers a call to getNewsletterOptions. While loading, the form can show a loading state and keep the submit button disabled until options are available.

Email prefill from URL: The page parses the email URL parameter (if present) on load and sets the initial value of the email input field. This value remains editable and is not written back to the URL unless the implementation explicitly does so via navigation.

Market segment selection: The list of market segment checkboxes is generated from getNewsletterOptions.market_segments. Each checkbox uses the option.value as its internal value and option.label for user display. Users can select or deselect any combination; the selected values form the marketSegments array submitted to submitNewsletterSubscription.

Email frequency selection: The email frequency dropdown is populated from getNewsletterOptions.email_frequencies, using each item's value and label. The option whose value matches getNewsletterOptions.default_frequency is preselected on load. Users may change the selection; this chosen value becomes emailFrequency in the submission. If the backend defines an option with value "monthly" and sets default_frequency to that value, the dropdown will default to monthly and allow users to select it.
Comments entry: The comments/notes textarea captures free-form text. Any value entered is passed directly as the comments parameter to submitNewsletterSubscription.

Privacy consent and link: A required checkbox represents acceptedPrivacyPolicy. The submit button is disabled until this checkbox is checked. A nearby text link navigates to privacy.html when clicked; it does not affect the checked state by itself.

Form validation: On submit, the form validates that full name, email, email frequency, and acceptedPrivacyPolicy are present, and that at least the minimal constraints defined by the implementation are satisfied (e.g., non-empty strings). Validation errors are shown inline next to the corresponding fields. No validation options or messages are hardcoded beyond using the values present in the UI; the error display can use generic or API-supplied messages.

Submission: When validations pass, the form calls submitNewsletterSubscription with the mapped parameters (fullName, email, marketSegments, emailFrequency, comments, acceptedPrivacyPolicy). During the request, the submit button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

Submission success: If submitNewsletterSubscription returns success=true, the confirmation panel shows a success message using the returned message property. The newsletter_subscription_id and created_at values can be displayed or stored in-memory if needed. The form may reset or be hidden based on implementation choice.

Submission error: If success=false or the request fails, an error message is displayed. When available, the message property from the response is shown so no static error text is required. The form remains editable for correction and resubmission.

Privacy navigation: Clicking the privacy policy link triggers navigation to privacy.html in the same tab or a new tab, depending on implementation. No additional parameters are required for this navigation, but they may be added if needed by the overall site architecture.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and live API responses. On every load of newsletter.html, the page checks for an email parameter and, if present, uses its decoded value to prefill the email field in the signup form. The page also may interpret additional URL parameters such as a generic status indicator or other keys (e.g., representing a prior successful submission) to decide whether to show the form, a confirmation state, or an error state. However, no specific status values are hardcoded; any such logic should be implemented in a configurable way. The list of market segment options, email frequencies, and the default frequency are not stored in URL parameters but are always fetched via getNewsletterOptions at runtime so they remain in sync with server-side configuration. The email frequency dropdown's selected value is initialized from getNewsletterOptions.default_frequency and then maintained as in-memory state while the user interacts with the page. On successful submission, the implementation may update the URL using navigation (e.g., via query parameters) to reflect the submission outcome; on reload, this status is read and used to restore the confirmation or error view. No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage mechanisms are used; the only persistence across reloads comes from URL parameters and any server-side behavior associated with the interfaces.

components.functionality: On page initialization, call getNewsletterOptions to load the available market segment and email frequency options, then expose these options and the default frequency to child components (primarily the newsletter signup form). Also manage loading and error states during this fetch.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when newsletter.html is loaded. Parses the email URL parameter and then calls getNewsletterOptions with no parameters.
onLoadSuccess: Invoked when getNewsletterOptions returns successfully; stores the market_segments, email_frequencies, and default_frequency in in-memory state available to the signup form.
onLoadError: Invoked when getNewsletterOptions fails; sets an error state that can be rendered as a message preventing form use until the issue is resolved.
onRetryClick: Optional handler bound to a 'retry loading options' control, which re-invokes getNewsletterOptions if the initial call failed.

components.functionality: Render and manage the newsletter signup form, including full name, email, market segment selection, email frequency dropdown, comments field, privacy consent checkbox, and submit button. Ensure all required fields are validated and that submission uses submitNewsletterSubscription with the correct parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize form fields with default empty values, except for email which is prefilled from the email URL parameter when present, and emailFrequency which is initialized from getNewsletterOptions.default_frequency once options are loaded.
onChangeFullName: Update the in-memory fullName value whenever the full name input changes.
onChangeEmail: Update the in-memory email value whenever the email input changes, overriding any initial value from the URL parameter.
onToggleMarketSegment: When a market segment checkbox is clicked, add or remove the option.value from the marketSegments array representing selected segments.
onChangeEmailFrequency: When the user selects a different email frequency option from the dropdown, update the emailFrequency state to the chosen option's value.
onChangeComments: Update the comments state as the user types in the comments/notes field.
onTogglePrivacyConsent: When the privacy consent checkbox is toggled, update acceptedPrivacyPolicy and enable or disable the submit button based on its value.
onClickPrivacyPolicyLink: Navigate to privacy.html using standard link navigation when the user clicks the privacy policy link next to the consent checkbox.
onValidate: Before submission, validate that fullName, email, emailFrequency, and acceptedPrivacyPolicy are present; return a validation result that controls whether submission proceeds.

onSubmit: When the user clicks the submit button and validation passes, call submitNewsletterSubscription with parameters mapped from the current form state (fullName, email, marketSegments, emailFrequency, comments, acceptedPrivacyPolicy). Set a submitting state to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSubmitSuccess: Handle a successful response from submitNewsletterSubscription by clearing or locking the form as desired, updating the confirmation panel state with values like newsletter_subscription_id, status, created_at, and message, and optionally updating the URL to reflect a success status.

onSubmitError: Handle an error response by displaying the message property returned by submitNewsletterSubscription (if available), keeping the current form values for user correction, and clearing the submitting state so resubmission is allowed.

components.functionality: Display dynamic feedback to the user about the status of their newsletter subscription attempt, including success messages, error messages, and any relevant metadata returned from submitNewsletterSubscription. Control the visibility of this panel based on in-memory state or URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Inspect URL parameters (such as a generic status indicator or subscription identifier, if set during a previous submission) to determine whether a success or error state should be displayed on page load.

onSubmissionStateChange: When the result of submitNewsletterSubscription is received, update the panel's state to show either a success or error message using the response fields, and determine whether the form component should be hidden, reset, or remain visible.
onDismissClick: Optional handler when the user closes or dismisses the confirmation panel, which can clear the status state and optionally update the URL to remove the status-related parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same stacked, full-width section pattern as the mockup: global double-height dark header (info bar + primary nav) at the top, a light, shallow breadcrumb strip directly beneath it, then a wide white main content area with a centered page title ('Newsletter Signup') and one-paragraph intro. Below this title block sits a single, centered content column that houses the newsletter signup experience: first the loader (when needed) occupying the same region as the form, then the actual signup form as the primary interactive block, and finally an optional confirmation panel in the same vertical flow. All components live inside a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) on the white background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same stacked, full-width section pattern as the mockup: global double-height dark header (info bar + primary nav) at the top, a light, shallow breadcrumb strip directly beneath it, then a wide white main content area with a centered page title ('Newsletter Signup') and one-paragraph intro. Below this title block sits a single, centered content column that houses the newsletter signup experience: first the loader (when needed) occupying the same region as the form, then the actual signup form as the primary interactive block, and finally an optional confirmation panel in the same vertical flow. All components live inside a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px) on the white background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header block still consumes roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport height, with the breadcrumb as a slim strip beneath it. The main content starts with generous vertical padding above and below the title/intro (approximately 48–64px top and bottom). The newsletter signup form then occupies the central portion of the remaining viewport, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container width (about 7–8 columns in a 12-column grid), centered. Vertical spacing between the title block, the form, and the confirmation panel maintains the ~48–64px separation to keep each step visually distinct. The loader uses the same footprint as the form and appears centered in that area, without changing the overall layout proportions.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low and airy. Large paddings (16–24px) are used inside the form and the confirmation panel, with clear separation between field groups and call-to-action areas. Only the essential fields and options are visible at once, with ample whitespace around them to avoid crowding. The loader presents minimal visual elements (spinner/progress indicator and 1–2 lines of helper text) in a spacious, centered block, maintaining the clean, uncluttered look. The confirmation panel is similarly light, with a short message, maybe one supporting line, and plenty of whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a strong vertical path: logo and navigation in the dark header → breadcrumb → large, center-aligned 'Newsletter Signup' title → short intro paragraph explaining the value → the signup form as the main focus, where market segment choices and email frequency options are presented. The primary submit button at the bottom of the form uses the neon-lime accent, pulling focus as the main action. When active, the loader is centered in the same region, clearly indicating progress without stealing attention from the overall layout. After submission, the confirmation panel appears in the same central area, becoming the new focal point. The global floating lime chat button remains in the bottom-right corner of the viewport as an always-available secondary action, consistent with the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a familiar corporate shell: a full-width, dark double header at the top with subdued typography, neon-lime and cyan accents, and a horizontal navigation, followed by a slim, light breadcrumb strip spanning the full width. Below, the background switches to a bright white canvas, where a centered content container (around 1140–1200px wide) holds the Newsletter Signup experience. At the top of this container, the 'Newsletter Signup' title is center-aligned and given ample breathing room, with a concise introductory paragraph directly beneath it, both surrounded by generous vertical padding so they occupy most of the upper third of the visible white area. Below this title block, the central column narrows slightly, with the signup form positioned at the visual center of the page, spanning roughly two-thirds of the container and surrounded by whitespace on both sides. When the page is preparing options, a loader appears in this exact region, vertically and horizontally centered within the white content area, without shifting the title or the overall layout. After loading, the form fields, segment selection, and frequency controls appear, stacked in a linear flow with clear grouping and spacing, ending with a wide neon-lime primary button. On successful submission, a confirmation panel slides into this same central position—either replacing or appearing directly above/below the form—with a clean message and subtle lime accents. Throughout, the sense of space and clarity is maintained: generous gaps (around 48–64px) separate the title block, the form region, and the confirmation state, while the global floating lime chat button sits detached at the bottom-right of the viewport, hovering over the white background as a small yet visually strong circular action that does not interfere with the main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter options loader is positioned within the main white content area, directly below the title and intro block and occupying the same footprint reserved for the signup form. It sits inside the centered content container, aligned to the 12-column grid, and spans roughly 60–70% of the container width (around 7–8 columns), with equal whitespace on the left and right. Vertically, it is placed about 48–64px below the intro paragraph, so there is a clear separation from the title area while still feeling connected as part of the same flow. The loader content is centered both horizontally and vertically within its own block: a subtle card-like region with 24px internal padding, a very light border or faint shadow on white, and plenty of empty space around a spinner/progress indicator and one short line of text such as 'Loading newsletter options…'. The entire loader block is relatively shallow in height but surrounded by generous whitespace above and below (24–32px) to maintain the airy layout. When active, it temporarily hides or covers the form area; when the data is ready, this block disappears and the signup form occupies its place without changing the overall page structure or the relationship to the title block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader behaves as an inline content block within the main column, occupying the same grid area as the signup form. It is not fixed or floating; instead, it is centered within its allocated section. Optionally, it may appear as a semi-opaque overlay on top of the form area, but constrained to the width and height of that central column, dimming only that region while keeping the header, breadcrumb, and surrounding whitespace unaffected. It scrolls naturally with the page and disappears once loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter signup form is the primary content block in the main white section, located directly beneath the title and intro text within the centered content container. Its top edge begins approximately 48–64px below the intro paragraph, respecting the same vertical rhythm as in the mockup between title and content. The form aligns to the 12-column grid, spanning roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–70% of the container width) and is centered horizontally, leaving generous side margins on both left and right to echo the spacious feel of the original three-column blog row. The form itself is presented as a clean panel with a white background (matching the page) and subtle structure defined by spacing rather than heavy borders: internal horizontal padding is around 24px, with vertical padding of 24–32px. Fields are arranged in a straightforward top-to-bottom flow: first basic contact fields, then a clearly separated block for selecting market segments (which may visually echo a three-column layout internally, but remains within the form’s width), followed by email frequency options, and finally consent to the privacy policy. Each grouping is separated by 24–32px of vertical space, with section labels or subtle headings aligned left within the form width. The main submit button sits at the bottom of the form, aligned left within the form column (or centered, depending on the global pattern), spanning roughly 40–50% of the form width but not edge-to-edge, and uses the neon-lime accent as a strong visual anchor. There is additional whitespace (24–32px) below the form before the next content (e.g., confirmation panel) to preserve the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form behaves as a standard scrollable block within the main content column. It is not sticky or fixed; it participates in the natural top-to-bottom scroll. Its width and position remain responsive but centered on larger screens, collapsing towards full width (--approximately 100% of the container) on smaller viewports while maintaining consistent internal padding. If the loader is active, the form’s area may be temporarily hidden or covered; if the confirmation panel appears and is designed to replace the form, the form smoothly collapses out of view in the same space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter confirmation panel appears in the same primary content region as the signup form, inside the centered max-width container and aligned to the same 12-column grid. It uses the same horizontal span as the form—roughly 7–8 columns centered—so that it visually aligns with where the user just interacted. Vertically, it either replaces the form in-place (ideal for a clean transition) or appears immediately below it with about 32–48px of separation; in both cases it remains below the title and intro, maintaining the linear top-to-bottom hierarchy. The panel presents as a slightly more pronounced card on the white background: a white surface with a subtle shadow or a 1px border, and a neon-lime accent, such as a left border strip or an icon, to signal success while staying on brand. Internal padding is generous (24–32px on all sides), with a short, bold headline like 'Subscription confirmed' at the top, left-aligned within the panel, and a brief supporting sentence or two beneath it. If any secondary actions are present (e.g., 'Manage preferences'), they appear as small, understated links or buttons aligned to the left or right at the bottom, separated from the body text by 16–24px. The panel is relatively compact in height but sits within ample vertical whitespace: at least 32–48px above and below, ensuring it feels like a clear, discrete state in the flow rather than crowded against other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The confirmation panel is an inline block that appears in the same central column as the form and scrolls normally with the page. It does not float or stick; instead, it occupies the form’s previous space or sits directly beneath it, depending on the interaction pattern. If implemented as a replacement, it inherits the form’s position, making the transition feel natural and preserving the visual rhythm from the title downward. On smaller screens, it expands to use the full container width while retaining consistent internal padding and accent details.

name: About
title: About
description: Page that retrieves and displays structured company profile information (overview, mission, history, leadership, capabilities) and provides navigation to distributors, events, and contact pages.

core_features: Load and display company overview and mission using getAboutPageContent.
Display a structured history timeline from getAboutPageContent, with optional URL-parameter-based focusing on a specific section.
List leadership profiles with expandable bios, sourced from getAboutPageContent.
List manufacturing and service capabilities using the capabilities data from getAboutPageContent.
Provide in-page navigation to jump to sections (overview, mission, history, leadership, capabilities, global presence, events, contact).
Provide navigation links from the About page to the distributor locator, events listing (pre-filtered to upcoming), and contact page.
user_workflows:
View overall company profile:
1. User opens about.html.
2. On initial load, the page calls getAboutPageContent.
3. The page displays the overview and mission content once the data is returned.
4. User scrolls the page or uses in-page navigation to move between sections.

Inspect company history timeline:
1. User opens about.html, optionally with a URL parameter like about.html?section=history.
2. Page loads data via getAboutPageContent and renders all sections.
3. If URL parameter section=history is present, the history section is scrolled into view and highlighted.
4. User can click pagination or filtering controls in the timeline component to focus on parts of the timeline (handled entirely client-side without new API calls).

Explore leadership team:
1. User scrolls or jumps (via in-page navigation) to the leadership section.
2. Leadership component receives the leadership array from getAboutPageContent and renders a list of entries.
3. User clicks a leadership item to expand or collapse the detailed bio.
4. The expanded/collapsed state is updated in the component without altering the URL.

Review capabilities in manufacturing and services:
1. User navigates to the capabilities section via scrolling or in-page navigation.
2. Capabilities component renders items from the capabilities array returned by getAboutPageContent.
3. User optionally uses simple client-side controls (e.g., category filter, show more/less) to adjust what is visible in the list, without additional API calls.

Move from About page to distributor locator:
1. User reaches the global presence / distribution area of the About page.
2. User clicks the link control for the distributor locator.
3. Browser navigates to distributors.html without additional parameters.

Move from About page to upcoming events:
1. User reaches the events / trade shows section of the About page.
2. User clicks the link control for events.
3. Browser navigates to events.html with a URL parameter status=upcoming (events.html?status=upcoming).

Move from About page to contact page:
1. User reaches the final call-to-action section of the About page.
2. User clicks the contact link control.
3. Browser navigates to contact.html without additional parameters.
interactions: On page load, trigger a single call to getAboutPageContent and populate all bound components with the returned object.
Clicking items in the in-page navigation menu updates the URL parameter (e.g., ?section=mission) using pushState/replaceState and scrolls to the corresponding section.

Clicking timeline controls (e.g., next/previous range, expand/collapse details) changes local component state and, optionally, updates a URL parameter such as ?section=history&historyView=<viewMode> for deep-linking.
Clicking a leadership entry toggles the detailed bio visibility for that specific entry.
Clicking the distributor locator link navigates to distributors.html.
Clicking the events link navigates to events.html?status=upcoming.
Clicking the contact link navigates to contact.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page reads and manages state exclusively via URL parameters and in-memory component state. Supported URL parameters include: section: identifies which section of the About page should be focused when the page loads (e.g., about.html?section=overview, about.html?section=history, about.html?section=leadership, about.html?section=capabilities, about.html?section=global-presence, about.html?section=events, about.html?section=contact). When section is present, after getAboutPageContent resolves and all sections are rendered, the page scrolls to the corresponding section and highlights it. historyView (optional): controls a purely client-side display mode of the history timeline (e.g., about.html?section=history&historyView=compact). The timeline component reads this parameter on load and chooses the appropriate internal view mode; when users change the view mode, the component updates historyView in the URL using pushState/replaceState. leadershipFocus (optional): allows deep-linking to a specific leadership profile (e.g., about.html?section=leadership&leadershipFocus=<index-or-id-from-array>). The leadership component, after bind to getAboutPageContent.leadership, checks this parameter and expands the matching item by default. None of these parameters change the data loaded from getAboutPageContent; they only affect which parts of the already-loaded data are visible or emphasized. Outgoing navigation relies on fixed URL patterns provided by the site architecture: distributors.html, events.html?status=upcoming, and contact.html.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page by calling getAboutPageContent when about.html is loaded, then distributes the returned data object (company_overview, mission, history_timeline, leadership, capabilities) to child components via a shared state or props mechanism.

Reads and interprets URL parameters relevant to the About page (section, historyView, leadershipFocus) and notifies relevant content sections to adjust their initial state (e.g., scroll targets, view modes, focused items). It also updates the URL when users navigate between sections using the in-page navigation.

Provides a list of anchors to jump to major sections of the About page (overview, mission, history, leadership, capabilities, global presence, events, contact). Clicking an item scrolls to the associated section container and updates the section URL parameter.

Displays the company overview text retrieved from getAboutPageContent. This section acts as the general description of the company and is the default scroll target when no section parameter is present.
Displays the mission statement text from getAboutPageContent. If the section parameter equals mission, this section is highlighted and scrolled into view after data load.

Renders the company history as a chronological sequence of items using the history_timeline array from getAboutPageContent. Supports client-side controls to switch views (e.g., compact vs. detailed) and optionally focuses on a specific range based on the historyView URL parameter.

Displays a list of leadership profiles using the leadership array from getAboutPageContent. Each item shows name and role, with the ability to expand to show the full bio. If leadershipFocus is set, the matching profile is automatically expanded.

Lists the company capabilities (such as manufacturing, design, support-related capabilities) using the capabilities array from getAboutPageContent. Offers client-side filters or show-more controls without further API calls.

Describes the existence of a global distribution network (textual content managed outside this specification) and contains a link control that routes users to distributors.html to find local distributors.
Provides a short explanatory text about company participation in events or trade shows (content not hardcoded here) and a link that navigates to events.html with the status=upcoming parameter.
Provides a closing call to action encouraging users to get in touch, with a link to contact.html.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit within the main content area that begins directly under the site-wide dark double header and light breadcrumb strip. Inside the main canvas, content is stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence within a centered, max-width container (~1140–1200px). The sequence is: page title and intro → in-page navigation → the structured About sections in a logical story order (overview → mission → history → leadership → capabilities) → outward-facing link sections (global presence/distributors → events/tradeshows → contact CTA). Non-visual infrastructure components (data loader and URL state controller) wrap or overlay this stack without visibly breaking the vertical rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit within the main content area that begins directly under the site-wide dark double header and light breadcrumb strip. Inside the main canvas, content is stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence within a centered, max-width container (~1140–1200px). The sequence is: page title and intro → in-page navigation → the structured About sections in a logical story order (overview → mission → history → leadership → capabilities) → outward-facing link sections (global presence/distributors → events/tradeshows → contact CTA). Non-visual infrastructure components (data loader and URL state controller) wrap or overlay this stack without visibly breaking the vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The title/intro + in-page navigation area together occupy roughly the upper half of the first viewport of the main content, echoing the mockup’s generous hero-like treatment for the blog title and first row of items. Each major section below gets a clear vertical separation of about 48–64px, maintaining a calm, segmented reading experience. Most sections are constrained to the central content column (~70–80% of viewport width up to 1200px) with left and right margins, while visually heavier areas like the history timeline and leadership grid are allowed to span the full container width. The bottom of the page maintains ample white space below the final contact CTA, similar to the open area beneath the blog cards in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low density throughout: short paragraphs, clearly separated headings, and lists broken into 2–3 columns with generous gutters. No area is overloaded; each section is allowed to breathe with 24–32px padding internally and 48–64px gaps between sections. Visual clusters such as leadership profiles or capability lists respect the implied 3-column grid, using 2–3 items per row with wide horizontal spacing. The link sections at the bottom are intentionally minimal, mirroring the blog cards: a compact visual plus a clear lime-accented title and concise descriptor, surrounded by whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves vertically from the dark header and breadcrumb into the bright, centered About title and intro, which act as a focal anchor. Immediately beneath, a horizontal in-page navigation band provides a left-to-right scanning moment similar to the three blog items, guiding users toward key sections. As users scroll, the narrative unfolds in a logical story: overview → mission → history timeline → leadership → capabilities, each section clearly delineated by spacing. The lower link sections then gently redirect attention outward to global presence/distributors and events, culminating in a strong, lime-accented contact CTA. A site-level floating chat button (outside this component list) remains docked in the bottom-right, providing an ever-present secondary action endpoint in the user’s peripheral vision.

layout.overall_description: Under the dark, double-height corporate header and the slim light breadcrumb, the main About page opens onto a wide, white canvas with a centered content column. At the top of this column, a large, left-aligned or center-aligned “About” heading and a brief, single-paragraph introduction sit with generous vertical padding, occupying roughly the upper third to half of the visible canvas and establishing a calm, modern tone. Just below, a horizontal in-page navigation band stretches across the content width, with labeled tabs for Overview, Mission, History, Leadership, Capabilities, and more, echoing the horizontal pull of the three-column blog row from the mockup. Scrolling down, the content flows in a simple, stacked progression: a clear overview block, followed by a focused mission section, a taller, more structured history timeline, then a light grid of leadership profiles and a structured capabilities list—all aligned to the same central grid with ample vertical breathing room. Near the bottom, focused link sections for global presence/distributors and events appear as minimal, card-like blocks with lime-accented headings, leading into a more visually assertive contact CTA band that feels like a natural conclusion to the story. Throughout, whitespace and consistent spacing preserve a low-density, scannable feel, while the faint tech-playful accent colors and the ever-present floating chat button in the bottom-right subtly remind users that immediate interaction is always available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader sits as an invisible layer covering the entire main content column from just below the breadcrumb strip down to the bottom edge of the About page content. When loading, it displays a semi-transparent white overlay over the central container, dimming all sections beneath while keeping the dark global header visible. A simple, compact loading indicator (such as a spinner or progress bar) is centered both vertically and horizontally within the content container, occupying only a small circle or bar area but clearly visible against the muted overlay. Horizontal margins of the overlay match the content container (roughly 10–15% of viewport width on each side), maintaining alignment with all other sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width overlay within the main content area, positioned above all other About-page components but below the global header; it is fixed relative to the scrolling content (it scrolls away with the page) and appears only during loading states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section router functions as a non-visual structural wrapper around the entire stack of About sections, spanning the full width of the centered content container from just below the in-page navigation to the bottom of the contact CTA section. It does not add any visible spacing or boxes; instead, it ensures that each named section aligns precisely with the grid so that scrolling to a hash or URL state positions that section neatly near the top of the viewport below the global header. In practice, it organizes the vertical order of Overview, Mission, History, Leadership, Capabilities, Global Presence, Events, and Contact CTA as distinct anchor blocks with their own top offsets respecting the 48–64px inter-section gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, structural; controls scroll positioning and URL-based navigation within the vertical stack of sections. It ensures that when a section is targeted, the page scrolls so that the section header sits just below the sticky in-page navigation and global header, without overlapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation is positioned directly beneath the page title and introductory paragraph, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the intro text. It spans the full width of the central content container (~70–80% of the viewport width, up to 1140–1200px) and is horizontally centered, forming a shallow band across the page. Within this band, section labels (Overview, Mission, History, Leadership, Capabilities, etc.) appear as evenly spaced text tabs or pill-like buttons arranged left-to-right in a single row on larger screens, wrapping to a second row only on smaller breakpoints. Each nav item has generous horizontal padding (12–16px) and 8–12px vertical padding, with lime or cyan accent on hover and a lime underline or pill background indicating the active section, subtly echoing the bright blog titles in the mockup. There is 16–24px of white space below this band before the first content section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured as a sticky band that adheres to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past the title/intro area, sitting just below the global header. It maintains its width and alignment with the central content container while staying visible as users scroll through lower sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview section is the first major content block under the in-page navigation, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space to maintain a clear break. It is anchored to the same centered content container, occupying roughly 70–80% of the viewport width up to the 1140–1200px max, with generous internal padding of 24–32px top and bottom. The section title sits near the top of the block, aligned left, with one to two short paragraphs of text below; the total visible height is modest, so the entire overview feels contained within roughly one-third of the viewport height on a typical desktop. There is ample whitespace to the left and right of the text, and the bottom of the section ends with about 48–64px of vertical gap before the mission section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow; scrolls normally with the page and acts as the first anchor target for the in-page navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission section follows directly below the overview, with a pronounced vertical gap of about 48px that visually separates the two blocks. It uses the same centered container width and internal padding, maintaining consistent left alignment with the overview title for a clean, columnar feel. The section heading is prominent but slightly smaller than the main About page title, followed by a succinct mission statement and, optionally, a single highlighted quote or key phrase that is given breathing room above and below. The section height is slightly taller than the overview but still within a comfortable, single-viewport scroll, keeping the page feeling light. A 48–64px gap below this content leads into the taller history timeline section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupies a fixed position in the vertical stack and serves as another scroll target linked from the sticky in-page navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history timeline section occupies a more visually substantial band in the middle of the page, starting about 48–64px below the mission section. It still respects the central content container width, but the internal layout stretches closer to the container edges, making fuller use of the horizontal space for timeline markers and dates. The section header is aligned to the left with generous top padding (32px), while the timeline itself runs vertically down the center or slightly off-center of the container, with key milestones branching out left and right, each given clear vertical spacing (24–32px) for easy scanning. This section typically spans at least one full viewport height, and possibly more, creating a sense of depth in the middle of the page. Ample whitespace at the bottom transitions softly into the leadership section, with about 64px of vertical space before the next heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, with anchor positioning tuned so that when navigated to, the section heading and the top of the first timeline items are fully visible just below the sticky navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The leadership section begins below the history timeline with a clear 48–64px gap, returning to a lighter, card-based feel that subtly references the three-column blog row from the mockup. Within the central content container, the section title spans the full width at the top, followed by a grid of leadership profiles arranged in 2–3 columns depending on screen width, each card occupying roughly one-third of the container width on desktop with wide gutters between them. Each profile card has internal padding of 16–24px and significant vertical spacing between rows (24–32px), ensuring the grid feels airy rather than crowded. The total height of the section may span roughly one to two viewports, but whitespace between rows and around the grid preserves the low-density aesthetic. The bottom of the section ends with another 48–64px vertical gap before the capabilities section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, grid-based behavior that reflows responsively (from three columns on large screens down to two or one column on smaller screens) while maintaining alignment with the central container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly after the leadership section with approximately 48px of vertical spacing, the capabilities section acts as a structured list of what the company can do, laid out in a balanced multi-column format. The heading is left-aligned at the top of the container, followed by capability groupings arranged in two or three vertical columns across the central width, each column taking roughly one-third of the container on larger screens. List items have comfortable vertical spacing (8–16px) and column gutters are wide (24–32px), so the content remains skimmable and visually light. This section’s height is moderate, typically fitting within a single viewport, reinforcing a tidy, categorized snapshot before users move on to outward-looking links. A clear 48–64px whitespace band below indicates the transition into the global presence link section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, with responsive column behavior; columns collapse gracefully to fewer columns on smaller screens while preserving vertical spacing between capability items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global presence section appears in the lower third of the page, starting roughly 48–64px below the capabilities section, and introduces a slightly more card-like, link-driven treatment. Within the central container, a concise heading such as “Global Presence & Distributors” is left-aligned at the top with ample breathing room, followed by one or two horizontally aligned link blocks that each occupy around half the container width on desktop with a wide gutter between. Each link block is shallow in height, featuring a small icon or map-style graphic on the left and a lime-accented link title plus short descriptive text on the right, echoing the simplicity of the blog item rows. The overall section remains relatively compact, consuming less than a full viewport height and leaving generous white space below before the events/tradeshows section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static link section; behaves like a mini card-row that adapts to single-column stacking on smaller screens while staying aligned with the main content grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events and tradeshows section follows the global presence section with a similar 48px vertical separation, visually pairing the two as outward-facing engagement opportunities. It uses the same centered container width with a section heading at the top and a small number of event link items arranged either as a vertical list or a light two- to three-column row, each item taking up roughly one-third or one-half of the container width depending on the number of upcoming events. Each event link item is minimal—title in lime, date/location in subdued text, and perhaps a small icon—surrounded by ample whitespace above and below (16–24px) to maintain scannability. The section’s vertical footprint remains moderate, typically fitting within the lower half of a viewport, leaving a comfortable margin before the final contact CTA block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; items reflow from multi-column to single-column on smaller viewports while maintaining generous vertical spacing between event cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact CTA section sits at the bottom of the About page content, separated from the events/tradeshows section by a noticeable 64px gap to emphasize its role as a concluding call to action. It spans the full width of the central content container and may feel slightly more band-like than other sections, with a stronger use of lime accent on the primary button or highlighted text. The main CTA message is centered horizontally within the block (or left-aligned with a large button immediately to the right), with generous vertical padding (32–48px) above and below, drawing the eye while still leaving white space around it. The entire section is relatively shallow in height but visually strong, standing out as the final focal point before the page’s natural white-space fade-out at the bottom. Below this block, additional whitespace of about 64–96px continues the clean, minimal feel, with the bottom-right floating chat button (from the broader design system) hovering above this space as an always-available alternate contact method.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, but designed to visually pair with the site-wide floating chat button that is fixed at the bottom-right of the viewport, creating a dual-contact affordance at the end of the scroll.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: General contact page where visitors can view corporate contact details and submit enquiries (general, media, partnership, or other topics) via a simple form. Topics are loaded dynamically and enquiries are submitted through the provided interfaces, with a confirmation state and optional navigation to the newsletter signup page.

core_features: Load available enquiry topics dynamically using getContactTopics and present them as selectable options in the contact form.
Display corporate contact details (address, phone numbers, general email) as non-interactive reference information.
Provide a general contact form capturing topic, message, contact name, email, company name, and phone.
Validate required fields client-side before submitting an enquiry.
Submit enquiries using submitContactInquiry and display server feedback including a reference identifier when available.
Expose an optional action from the confirmation state to navigate to newsletter.html, passing the contact email as a URL parameter for newsletter signup.
Support deep-linking and pre-filling of the form using URL parameters (e.g., selected topic, contact details, or message).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic enquiry submission
1. User navigates to contact.html from the site header, footer, or an external link.
2. The page controller component initializes and calls getContactTopics to retrieve available enquiry topics.
3. Once topics are loaded, the topic selector is populated; if a valid ?topic= URL parameter is present, that topic is preselected.
4. User reviews the corporate contact details block for reference.
5. User selects a topic (if not already preselected), fills in their name, email, optional company and phone, and writes their message.
6. User clicks the form submit button.
7. The form component validates required fields (topic, contactName, email, message). If validation fails, inline errors are shown and no API call is made.
8. If validation passes, the form component calls submitContactInquiry with the current form values.
9. While waiting for the response, the form shows a submitting state (e.g., disabling the submit button).
10. On a successful response (success=true), the page switches to a confirmation state that shows the response message and contact_reference (if provided) and stores the submitted email in page state.
11. From the confirmation state, the user may click an "Also sign me up for the newsletter" action, which navigates to newsletter.html with the email passed as a URL parameter (e.g., newsletter.html?email=<submitted_email>).
12. Alternatively, the user can return to the form (if supported) to submit another inquiry.

Workflow 2: Pre-filtered contact topic via URL
1. User clicks a link from elsewhere on the site or an external resource that points to contact.html with a topic parameter (e.g., contact.html?topic=<topic_value_from_getContactTopics>). Additional parameters such as name, email, company, phone, or message may also be included.
2. The contact page loads and calls getContactTopics.
3. After topics are retrieved, the controller checks whether the URL topic parameter matches one of the returned topic values. If it matches, that topic is preselected in the topic selector; otherwise, no preselection is performed.
4. The controller pre-fills other form fields from URL parameters when present (e.g., ?name=, ?email=, ?company=, ?phone=, ?message=), decoding them appropriately.
5. User can modify any pre-filled data, then completes and submits the enquiry as in Workflow 1.
6. On success, the page behaves as in Workflow 1, but the updated form values (including any changes to the email) are used for the newsletter navigation.

Workflow 3: Handling submission errors and retrying
1. User fills out the form and submits it.
2. The form validates successfully and calls submitContactInquiry.
3. The API responds with success=false or an error condition.
4. The submission feedback component displays an error message based on the response.message and/or a generic error, while preserving the user’s form input values.
5. The user corrects any issues (e.g., edits message or email) and clicks submit again.
6. The form re-validates and, if valid, calls submitContactInquiry again.
7. On a subsequent success, the confirmation state is shown and the retry workflow ends.

Workflow 4: Newsletter navigation after contact confirmation
1. After a successful enquiry submission, the confirmation component has access to the submitted email (the same email used in submitContactInquiry).
2. The confirmation state shows an action (link or checkbox plus button) labeled to indicate newsletter signup.
3. When the user triggers this action, the page navigates to newsletter.html, appending the email as a URL parameter derived from the stored submitted email (e.g., newsletter.html?email=<submitted_email>). No additional interface calls are made from the contact page in this step.
4. The newsletter page can then use that URL parameter to pre-fill its own forms (outside the scope of this page).
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Event: onLoad (window or page-controller level).
- Behavior: Parse URL parameters (topic, name, email, company, phone, message, and any others defined by implementation). Call getContactTopics. Store the resulting topic list and initial form state in page-level state.
- Behavior: Once topics are loaded, validate any topic specified via URL parameter against the list; if valid, set it as selected; otherwise, leave selection unset or use a default strategy defined by implementation.

Topic selection:
- Element: Topic selector component (e.g., dropdown, radio group, or similar).
- Event: onChange when user selects a different topic.
- Behavior: Update the form’s topic field in state.
- Behavior: Optionally update the URL’s topic parameter using pushState/replaceState without reloading the page so the state is shareable (e.g., contact.html?topic=<selected_value> plus any other existing parameters).

Form field editing:
- Elements: Input fields for contactName, email, companyName, phone, and message.
- Events: onChange and/or onBlur for each field.
- Behavior: Update corresponding properties in the form state object.
- Behavior: Clear any field-specific validation errors when the user edits a field that previously failed validation.

Form submission:
- Element: Submit button inside the contact form.
- Event: onClick (or form onSubmit event).
- Behavior: Prevent default browser submission; run client-side validation ensuring at least topic, contactName, email, and message are present and structurally valid (e.g., email format check as defined by implementation).
- Behavior: If validation fails, set validation error messages in state and do not call submitContactInquiry.
- Behavior: If validation succeeds, set submissionState to "submitting", disable the submit button, and call submitContactInquiry with the current state values: topic, message, contactName, email, companyName, phone.

Handling submitContactInquiry response:
- Event: onSubmitContactInquirySuccess (internal, triggered when API responds).
- Behavior (success=true):
  - Set submissionState to "success".
  - Store the returned contact_reference and response.message in state.
  - Store the submitted email in state for use by the newsletter navigation.
  - Optionally update the URL with a submitted flag and reference (e.g., contact.html?submitted=1&ref=<contact_reference>), using the actual contact_reference from the response rather than any hardcoded value.
- Behavior (success=false or error):
  - Set submissionState to "error".
  - Display an error message from response.message when provided, or a generic message defined by implementation.
  - Re-enable the submit button and keep the existing form values.

Confirmation view and newsletter navigation:
- Element: Confirmation/status panel visible when submissionState is "success".
- Display: The API’s response.message and contact_reference (if present) as reference information.
- Element: Newsletter action (e.g., link or checkbox plus a separate button) labeled to indicate signup for the newsletter.
- Event: onClickNewsletterLink (or onChange + onClick if using a checkbox followed by a button).
- Behavior: When triggered, read the stored email from the last successful submission state. Construct a URL to newsletter.html including the email as the value of an email parameter (e.g., newsletter.html?email=<stored_email>, URL-encoded) and navigate the browser to that URL. Do not call any additional page interfaces.

Error dismissal and retry:
- Element: Error message block or banner on the form when submissionState is "error".
- Event: onClickCloseError (if a close icon/button is provided) or implicit when user edits fields.
- Behavior: Clear the error display while keeping the user’s inputs.
- Behavior: Allow the user to submit the form again, repeating the normal submission interaction.

Direct navigation from header/footer:
- Element: Header navigation item labeled to represent this page.
- Event: onClickNavContact (header) or onClickNavContact (footer).
- Behavior: Navigate to contact.html (with no or minimal query parameters). Any existing state is reset according to the standard page initialization process.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is maintained primarily in memory for the duration of the page session and partially mirrored in URL parameters so that links can represent pre-filled or pre-filtered contact forms. No localStorage or similar persistent client-side storage is used.

URL parameters and their effects (all parameter names are indicative and can be adapted, but must be handled generically):
- topic: When present on load, after getContactTopics returns, the controller checks if its value matches any value field from getContactTopics. If it matches, the topic selector is pre-populated with that option; otherwise, the topic remains unselected or defaults according to implementation. When the user changes the topic in the selector, the controller may update the topic parameter in the URL (using history.pushState or history.replaceState) without reloading the page.
- name, email, company, phone, message: When present, these parameters are decoded and used to pre-fill the corresponding fields in the form’s initial state. Subsequent user changes are not automatically written back to the URL except where explicitly implemented. The controller should avoid overwriting user edits using URL values after initial load.
- submitted: This parameter can be set by the page after a successful submission (for instance, submitted=1). Its presence on load can be interpreted to restore a confirmation-focused state, but because the underlying data for a past submission is not loaded via any interface on this page, it should only affect whether the page attempts to show a generic "thank you" state versus the empty form. The implementation must not assume any specific contact_reference based solely on this parameter.
- ref: This parameter can be used to reflect the contact_reference returned by submitContactInquiry. It can be appended or updated in the URL when a submission succeeds. On initial load, if both submitted and ref are present, the page may choose to show a confirmation summary containing ref as plain text; however, since there is no interface on this page to refetch the underlying submission, the page should not attempt to look up or validate ref and must treat it as display-only.

In-memory state (illustrative):
- topics: The array returned by getContactTopics, used to populate the topic selector.
- formState: An object containing topic, contactName, email, companyName, phone, message, and any validation error flags or messages.
- submissionState: A string representing overall form status (e.g., "idle", "submitting", "success", "error").
- submissionResult: An object storing the most recent submitContactInquiry response (including success, contact_reference, and message).
- submittedEmail: The email address used in the most recent successful submission, used to construct the navigation URL to newsletter.html.

All UI components derive their rendering solely from these state objects and the URL parameters as interpreted above. The only external data dependencies are the results from getContactTopics and submitContactInquiry, as required by this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, state management, and interaction between the topic loader, form, and confirmation components. On load, parses URL parameters, calls getContactTopics, initializes form state, and exposes handlers to update URL parameters when relevant state changes. Manages the submissionState and submissionResult based on submitContactInquiry responses.

Displays corporate contact information such as mailing address, main phone numbers, and general contact email. This content is static or server-rendered and not modified by page interactions. It serves as a reference section alongside the contact form.

Presents the list of enquiry topics retrieved via getContactTopics as selectable options (e.g., dropdown or list of radio buttons). Reflects and updates the current topic value in formState managed by the page controller.

Provides input fields for contactName, email, optional companyName and phone, and a message text area. Includes a mechanism for selecting or confirming the topic (via the contact-topic-selector component or a hidden field bound to its state). Handles client-side validation and invokes submitContactInquiry with the current form values when submitted.

Displays feedback about the submission state: loading/submitting status, validation errors, API error messages, and success confirmation. On success, shows response.message and contact_reference from submitContactInquiry and exposes an entry point for newsletter signup navigation.

When triggered from the confirmation state, reads the submittedEmail value managed by the page controller and navigates to newsletter.html, appending an email URL parameter derived from that value. No additional interfaces are called; it only performs URL construction and navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the stacked, full-width structure from the mockup: dark double header (info bar + primary nav) at the very top → shallow light breadcrumb strip → main white content canvas with a centered max-width container. Inside this container, the Contact page starts with a centered page title and short intro paragraph, followed by a primary content row that splits into two columns: corporate contact details on the left and the interactive enquiry area (topic selector, form, and feedback) on the right. The newsletter opt-in navigation sits near the bottom of this right column, aligned with the form flow. A global floating action/chat button remains at the bottom-right of the viewport, visually detached from the content, consistent with the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the stacked, full-width structure from the mockup: dark double header (info bar + primary nav) at the very top → shallow light breadcrumb strip → main white content canvas with a centered max-width container. Inside this container, the Contact page starts with a centered page title and short intro paragraph, followed by a primary content row that splits into two columns: corporate contact details on the left and the interactive enquiry area (topic selector, form, and feedback) on the right. The newsletter opt-in navigation sits near the bottom of this right column, aligned with the form flow. A global floating action/chat button remains at the bottom-right of the viewport, visually detached from the content, consistent with the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The double header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height, matching the mockup, with the breadcrumb as a shallow strip below. The main content container is centered at ~1140–1200px width, with generous vertical padding above and below (48–64px) around the title/intro block. There is ~48px separation between the title/intro and the two-column contact content row. Inside that row, the left corporate-contact-details block spans about 4 of 12 grid columns (~33% of container width), while the right column (topic selector, form, feedback, newsletter link) spans about 8 columns (~67%). A gutter of ~32px separates the two columns. Each card-like block or panel uses 16–24px internal padding to maintain the airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and spacious. Text blocks (title, intro, contact details) are given ample whitespace above and below (24–32px) and are not crowded by other elements. The form fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between controls, avoiding multi-column forms. Only necessary inputs are visible, with conditional fields appearing without collapsing the overall airiness. Feedback and newsletter elements are kept visually light and compact, with plenty of white space around them so the page never feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the same vertical narrative as the mockup: logo and navigation in the dark header → breadcrumb → large, center-aligned 'Contact' title → supporting intro paragraph. From there, the eye is led into a lateral flow: first to the left-hand corporate contact details (which ground the corporate identity), then across to the more vivid, interactive right-hand column where the topic selector and primary form live, emphasized with neon-lime accents on buttons and active states. After submission, attention is pulled slightly downward to the status/feedback panel and then to the newsletter opt-in link. The global floating chat button at the bottom-right acts as an always-available secondary action and a visual endpoint, echoing the mockup’s bottom-right focal point.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark, double-height corporate header and the thin light breadcrumb bar, the Contact page opens into a wide white content area framed by a centered max-width container. At the top of this container, a bold, center-aligned 'Contact' headline sits with generous breathing room above and below, followed by a single, concise intro paragraph that explains how visitors can reach out. After a comfortable vertical gap, the layout shifts into a two-column row: on the left, a compact but legible corporate contact details block occupies roughly one-third of the width, reading like a clean info card; on the right, a wider column spanning about two-thirds of the width holds the interactive experience. At its top, a clear topic selector lets users choose the type of enquiry, followed directly by a vertically stacked contact form with modern, roomy fields and a neon-lime accented submit button. When a submission occurs, an inline feedback panel appears directly beneath the form, staying within the same column. Just below that, a subtle newsletter navigation link invites visitors to continue engagement. Throughout, vertical spacing between these elements stays generous—typically 24–48px—so each block feels distinct yet part of a single, coherent column. Outside this centered canvas, the full-width white background continues downward with ample empty space, and in the lower-right corner of the viewport, the floating lime-accented chat/action button hovers above the content, echoing the original mockup’s playful tech character while not interfering with the calm, corporate main layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller acts as the structural wrapper for all Contact-specific content within the main white canvas, starting immediately below the breadcrumb strip. It spans the full width of the centered content container (approximately 1140–1200px) and is horizontally centered within the browser window. Vertically, it introduces a top padding of about 64px from the bottom of the breadcrumb to the page title and a bottom padding of about 64px before the content fades into empty white space. Inside it, content is arranged in a linear top-to-bottom order: first the title and intro block centered in the container, then a two-column row with the corporate contact details on the left and the topic selector + form + feedback + newsletter navigation stacked on the right. It ensures the two-column row stays aligned to a 12-column grid, with a left column of ~4 columns and a right column of ~8 columns, separated by a generous gutter (~32px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that follows the natural scroll; it defines the main content region beneath the global header and breadcrumb, constraining child components to the centered max-width grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The corporate-contact-details block sits in the main content row, anchored to the left side of the centered content container. It begins roughly 48px below the intro paragraph, aligned horizontally so its left edge lines up with the container’s left margin. It occupies about 4 of 12 grid columns (roughly one-third of the container width), giving it a tall, card-like vertical rectangle. Within this block, there is 24px of internal padding on all sides, allowing the company name, address, phone numbers, email, and any office hours to be stacked clearly with 8–16px spacing between lines. The top of this block is aligned with the top of the topic selector in the right column, creating a clean horizontal baseline. There is a clear gap of about 32px between this block and the right-hand column. The bottom of the contact details block does not need to align perfectly with the form; it can end higher or lower, but there should be at least 48px of breathing room to the next major element below (if any), preserving the minimal, airy character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-column card fixed in the page flow; it does not scroll independently or float, but maintains its position relative to the title/intro and the right-hand form column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-topic-selector is positioned at the top of the right-hand column within the main content row, horizontally aligned so its left edge starts after the central gutter (about 32px from the corporate-contact-details block). It spans the full width of this right column, which is approximately 8 of 12 grid columns (~67% of the container). Vertically, its top edge aligns with the top edge of the corporate-contact-details block, creating a clean cross-page alignment. It appears as a low-profile yet visually clear control (for example, a dropdown, segmented control, or tab-like selector) with about 16–24px of internal vertical padding and 24px side padding, giving it a modern, tappable feel. Neon-lime or cyan accents highlight the active state or focus state to echo the mockup’s accent character. Below it, there is a vertical gap of approximately 24px before the contact-details-form begins, keeping the hierarchy obvious without crowding controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline control that anchors the top of the interactive right-hand column; it may dynamically update the form below but remains fixed in place within the scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-form sits directly beneath the contact-topic-selector in the right-hand column, sharing the same horizontal width (around 8 of 12 grid columns) and left alignment. There is a vertical gap of about 24px between the bottom of the selector and the top of the form. The form is vertically oriented: fields are stacked one per row, with 16–24px vertical spacing between each input or textarea, maintaining the low-density, scannable layout. Internally, the form uses 24px padding on all sides, framing the form content like a subtle card against the white background.
