The primary submit button is placed at the bottom-right of the form content area, with a neon-lime filled style that echoes the mockup’s accent and draws the eye, and with about 24px of space above it from the last field and 16–24px below it to the card edge. The overall height of the form is flexible depending on the chosen topic, but it should maintain at least 48px of blank space below before the feedback panel appears, so that the page still feels breathable when the feedback is hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form block within the right column; it expands vertically as needed with topic-specific fields but does not overlay other content. It pushes subsequent elements (feedback panel and newsletter link) downward as it grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-submission-feedback panel is positioned directly beneath the contact-details-form in the right-hand column, aligned to the same left and right edges, and spanning the full width of that column (about 8 of 12 grid columns). When visible, it appears after a vertical gap of roughly 32px below the bottom of the form, preserving a clear separation between the act of submitting and the confirmation state. Visually, it behaves like a compact status card: 16–24px internal padding, subtle border or soft background tint, and concise message lines with 8–16px spacing. Any success icon or status indicator aligns to the left, with text wrapping to the right, maintaining the clean corporate look. The panel’s overall height remains modest to keep it light, with at least 24px of space below it before the newsletter-optin-navigator, so the post-submission area doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual, inline panel that appears and disappears in-place within the scroll flow. It does not overlap other content; instead, it smoothly expands the vertical space between the form and the newsletter navigation when active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-optin-navigator sits near the bottom of the right-hand column, below the contact-submission-feedback panel when that panel is shown, or directly below the form when the feedback is not visible. It is separated from whichever element is above by roughly 24–32px of vertical space, keeping it visually distinct as a follow-up action. Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the right column’s content, but the text or button may be right-aligned within its own width to subtly echo the page’s bottom-right endpoint emphasis. It is relatively small—either a single line of text with an inline link (e.g., 'Also, sign up for our newsletter') or a slender button—using neon-lime or cyan for the interactive part to stay coherent with accent usage. The navigator does not extend beyond the right column’s width and leaves at least 48–64px of white space below it before the end of the main content area, maintaining the minimal and airy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element that sits at the tail end of the main interaction flow. It does not float or stick; instead, it naturally scrolls with the page and remains a subtle, end-of-journey call-to-action within the right column.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the company’s privacy policy, including data usage, cookies, user rights, and privacy contact information.

core_features: Fetch and display the full privacy policy content (sections and last updated date) using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Render a structured list of policy sections to allow quick navigation within the page.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the privacy policy via URL parameters (e.g., privacy.html?section={sectionId}).
Indicate the last updated date of the privacy policy as provided by the backend.
Provide a functional link from any relevant policy text to the contact.html page for privacy-related inquiries (e.g., contacting the data protection officer).
Keep the currently selected section in sync with the URL parameter so browser navigation (back/forward) and shared links behave predictably.
user_workflows:
Viewing the privacy policy from the site footer:
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the global footer.
2. Browser navigates to privacy.html without additional URL parameters.
3. On page load, the privacy-policy-content-fetcher component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. Once data is returned, the page displays the last updated date and all sections via the privacy-last-updated-label and privacy-sections-renderer components.
5. The privacy-section-toc component lists the sections for quick navigation within the same page.

Navigating between sections using the table of contents:
1. User scrolls to the table of contents rendered by privacy-section-toc.
2. User clicks a section item in the table of contents.
3. The click handler reads the clicked section’s id and updates the URL to privacy.html?section={id} (replacing or adding the section parameter without reloading the page, if using SPA behavior).
4. The privacy-section-anchor-handler interprets the updated URL parameter and sets the activeSectionId in in-memory state.
5. The privacy-sections-renderer scrolls or jumps to the corresponding section and applies active styling based on activeSectionId.

Opening the page on a specific section via a direct link:
1. User follows a link from another page or an external source to privacy.html?section={id}.
2. On page load, privacy-policy-content-fetcher requests the policy data via getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. In parallel, privacy-section-anchor-handler reads the section URL parameter and stores it as pendingSectionId.
4. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, privacy-sections-renderer matches pendingSectionId against the loaded sections.
5. If a match exists, the page scrolls or jumps directly to that section and marks it as active; if there is no match, the page displays all sections with no specific active section highlighted.

Contacting the data protection officer (or equivalent privacy contact):
1. User reads the policy section describing privacy contacts or data subject rights.
2. Within that section’s body text, the dpo-contact-link component renders an actionable element that is mapped to contact.html.
3. User clicks the contact link.
4. Browser navigates to contact.html (no additional URL parameters are required by this page’s specification).
5. User can then use the contact page’s functionality (outside the scope of this page) to submit a privacy-related request.

Refreshing or revisiting the privacy policy:
1. User refreshes the browser while on privacy.html (with or without a section URL parameter).
2. On each load, privacy-policy-content-fetcher calls getPrivacyPolicyContent to ensure the user sees the latest policy version.
3. If a section parameter exists in the URL, privacy-section-anchor-handler again applies deep-linking logic so the correct section is focused.

Using browser back/forward with section navigation:
1. User navigates between multiple sections via the table of contents, which updates the section URL parameter.
2. User clicks the browser back button.
3. The browser restores the previous URL (with its previous section parameter value).
4. privacy-section-anchor-handler detects the URL change (via popstate or equivalent) and updates activeSectionId.
5. privacy-sections-renderer adjusts the visible active section and scroll position to match the restored URL state.
interactions: On page load, the system automatically calls getPrivacyPolicyContent (no user input required), then binds the returned last_updated value and sections array to the respective components.

When the user clicks a section item in the table of contents, the click handler updates the URL parameter section to the id of the chosen section and instructs the content viewer to scroll to that section.
When the URL parameter section changes (either via clicks or browser navigation), the active section in the content viewer is updated, and the table of contents highlights the corresponding entry.
When the user clicks the privacy contact link within the policy text, the browser navigates to contact.html according to the outgoing navigation rule.

If getPrivacyPolicyContent fails (e.g., network or server error), an error state is displayed by the privacy-error-state component, and the user can trigger a retry action (e.g., clicking a retry button) that calls getPrivacyPolicyContent again.

If the returned sections array is empty, the privacy-empty-state component displays a fallback informational message indicating that no policy content is currently available, instead of showing an empty page.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page uses URL query parameters to manage state, specifically the `section` parameter for intra-page navigation. When the page loads, the `privacy-section-anchor-handler` reads `section` from `window.location.search` (e.g., `privacy.html?section={id}`). This value is stored as `activeSectionId` in in-memory page state. The `privacy-policy-content-fetcher` then calls `getPrivacyPolicyContent` and stores the result (e.g., `policyData.last_updated` and `policyData.sections`). Once both the URL parameter and content are available, `privacy-sections-renderer` compares `activeSectionId` to the list of `sections[].id` from the API. If a corresponding section exists, that section is treated as active, and the page scrolls or jumps to it; if not, `activeSectionId` is set to a neutral value (such as null) and no special section is highlighted. When the user clicks a section in the table of contents, the click handler updates `window.history` with a new URL that has `?section={clickedId}` (replacing any existing `section` value). This URL change updates `activeSectionId` via the same handler used on initial load, keeping the view and the URL in sync. Browser back/forward actions are handled by listening to history events and re-calculating `activeSectionId` from the URL. No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies are used for state management on this page; all relevant state is derived from the current URL and the response from `getPrivacyPolicyContent`.

components.functionality: Requests the structured privacy policy data from the backend using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface and exposes it to other components as in-memory state.
Displays the last updated date of the privacy policy using the last_updated value returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Synchronizes the `section` URL parameter with an internal activeSectionId state that other components use for highlighting and scrolling to the correct section.

Renders an interactive list of policy sections based on the sections array and allows the user to click to navigate to a specific section; updates the URL parameter to reflect the selection and highlights the active section.

Iterates over the sections array returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent and renders each section’s title and body; uses activeSectionId to scroll to and visually distinguish the currently active section.

Provides a functional navigation link to contact.html wherever the policy content designates a privacy-contact or data-protection-officer link (for example, by attaching this component to a specific anchor or placeholder within the section body).
Shows an error message and retry control if loading the privacy policy via getPrivacyPolicyContent fails; allows the user to attempt the request again.
Handles the situation where getPrivacyPolicyContent returns no sections by displaying a neutral informational state instead of an empty page.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s stacked, full-width section pattern: the existing dark double header and light breadcrumb remain unchanged at the top, followed by a wide white main content area. Inside this main area, a centered content container holds the page title and intro (provided by the global layout), then the privacy-last-updated-label, followed by a visually light but clearly segmented table of contents block, and then the long-form privacy sections. Error and empty states occupy the same main content region, replacing the ToC and sections when triggered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s stacked, full-width section pattern: the existing dark double header and light breadcrumb remain unchanged at the top, followed by a wide white main content area. Inside this main area, a centered content container holds the page title and intro (provided by the global layout), then the privacy-last-updated-label, followed by a visually light but clearly segmented table of contents block, and then the long-form privacy sections. Error and empty states occupy the same main content region, replacing the ToC and sections when triggered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered max-width container of about 1140–1200px with 24–32px horizontal padding. Use 48–64px vertical spacing between major content bands: from breadcrumb to title block, from title/intro to the ToC, and from the ToC to the main policy text. Within components, apply 16–24px internal padding to cards and callouts. The ToC block occupies roughly the upper third of the main content area above the fold, while the sections renderer fills the remaining vertical space with comfortable margins to keep the layout airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the page low-density despite the text-heavy nature of a privacy policy. Use a relatively narrow text column (around 60–70% of the container width) with generous line height and 32–48px spacing between sections. The ToC items mimic the light blog-card feel from the mockup: small, tappable blocks with lime-accented titles and plenty of white space around them rather than a dense navigation list. Loader, error, and empty states are centered with minimal but clear messaging, preserving visual calm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the mockup’s vertical flow: the eye moves from header and breadcrumb down to the centered page title and intro, then immediately to the subtle privacy-last-updated-label. From there, the lime-accented ToC items act like the three blog cards in the mockup, pulling attention horizontally across the row (and additional rows if needed). Once a section is selected or the user scrolls, attention flows down into the main text column of the privacy-sections-renderer. The DPO contact link provides a late-stage accent focal point near the bottom of the content, while error/empty states temporarily become the primary visual focus when active.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark double header and the light breadcrumb strip, the Privacy Policy page opens onto a wide white canvas with a centered content container. At the top of this container, the global layout presents a large, left-aligned “Privacy Policy” title with a short introductory sentence beneath it, framed by generous top and bottom padding so the area feels open and important. Directly below the intro, a small, subtle “Last updated” label sits left-aligned, close to the title but with enough breathing room to feel secondary. After a comfortable vertical gap, a table-of-contents block spans most of the container width: a set of lime-accented, card-like links arranged in a responsive grid reminiscent of the mockup’s three blog items, encouraging quick scanning of key sections like Data Usage, Cookies, and User Rights. Beneath this, the main body of the policy flows in a narrower, centered text column, with each section clearly separated by generous spacing and strong headings for easy scanning. In error or empty states, the ToC and content are replaced by a centered message card in the same column, maintaining overall alignment and spacing. Throughout, the layout stays clean, minimal, and corporate, with neon-lime accents picked up in ToC items and the DPO contact link, and with the site-wide floating action button still visible at the bottom-right, floating over the main white canvas without interfering with the reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content fetcher operates within the main white content area, conceptually wrapping both the table of contents and the policy sections. It occupies the full width of the centered content container (about 80–90% of the page width on desktop, capped at 1140–1200px) and extends vertically from just below the page intro down to the end of the privacy content. While data is loading, a single loading indicator is centered horizontally within this column and positioned roughly in the middle of the viewport height, with at least 64px of white space above and below it. This keeps the loading state visually calm and aligned with the overall spacious design, without introducing additional framing or heavy containers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays in the normal document flow, expanding vertically as needed. The loading overlay does not cover the header or breadcrumb; it only occupies the main content column beneath the title and above the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated label is positioned directly beneath the main “Privacy Policy” heading inside the centered content container. It is left-aligned with the title, occupying only as much horizontal space as its text requires (typically less than one third of the container width), and sits about 8–16px below the title to feel related but clearly subordinate. Below the label, leave roughly 24–32px of vertical space before the start of the table-of-contents block or any intro paragraph, so the page’s top area still feels airy. The text is small and discreet, with no heavy borders or backgrounds, ensuring it provides context without competing for attention with the title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in normal flow, shifting above the ToC and main content on all breakpoints. On narrower screens, it simply wraps to multiple lines if needed but maintains its left alignment and modest spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The anchor handler is an invisible functional layer associated with the top of the main content column and with each section heading rendered by the sections renderer. It has no visible dimensions or styling, but logically occupies the same horizontal position as the text column and respects the vertical offsets of the fixed header and breadcrumb. When a user selects a ToC item or an anchored link, the scroll target is adjusted so the corresponding section heading appears just below the bottom edge of the dark header and breadcrumb band, with about 24–32px of breathing room above the heading, preserving the spacious look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive in terms of layout; it influences scroll positioning only. It behaves as a set of invisible anchors embedded just above section headings, taking into account fixed-header offset but not altering the visible flow of content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents sits in the main content container beneath the title/intro and the last-updated label, separated from them by about 48px of vertical space to give it its own visual band, similar in weight to the blog row in the mockup. On desktop, it spans the full inner width of the container and arranges section links as card-like items in a responsive grid: typically three items per row (each card occupying roughly one third of the container width with 24–32px horizontal gutters), wrapping to additional rows as needed for more sections. Each card is relatively shallow, with 16–24px internal padding, a light outline or soft shadow, and a lime-accented title that echoes the mockup’s blog titles; any supporting text is minimal to retain a light, scannable feel. Vertically, the ToC block has 24–32px of padding above and below its cards, and there is a clear 48–64px gap between the bottom of the ToC and the first policy section, so it reads as a distinct, navigational zone. On tablet and mobile, the ToC collapses to two columns and then a single column, but maintains the generous vertical spacing so the page never feels crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the document as a block-level section. It scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page; no sticky behavior is applied. On smaller screens, its grid reflows responsively, but its position relative to the title above and the sections below remains the same.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sections renderer defines the main reading column for the privacy policy beneath the ToC. It is positioned within the same centered content container but uses a narrower text width for comfort: on desktop, the text column occupies about 60–70% of the container width (roughly 7–8 of 12 columns), centered horizontally so there are equal white margins on either side. Each top-level section starts with a clear heading aligned to the left edge of this column, followed by paragraphs of body text with generous line height and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs. Separate sections are divided by 48–64px of vertical space, visually grouping content without visible separators. The top of the sections block begins about 48–64px below the ToC, mirroring the spacing between major sections elsewhere on the page. On smaller screens, the text column grows to nearly the full width of the container while preserving internal paddings and vertical spacing, maintaining the overall airy, minimal feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the remainder of the main content area below the ToC in normal flow, expanding vertically as needed. It cooperates with the anchor handler for smooth scrolling to headings but does not fix itself in place; the user scrolls naturally through the sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The DPO contact link is placed within the relevant section rendered by the sections renderer, typically near the bottom of a “Contact” or “Your rights” section. It is left-aligned with the main text column and appears after a short explanatory paragraph, with an additional 16–24px of top margin so it reads as a distinct call-to-action. Visually, it is more prominent than a standard inline link: styled either as a lime-accented text link with a subtle underline or as a light pill-shaped button with a thin lime border and 8–12px of horizontal padding, but without heavy fills that would overpower the calm layout. It occupies only as much width as its label requires, leaving plenty of white space to the right, and typically sits about 48px above the final section’s bottom margin, acting as a visual endpoint within the text column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal document flow as part of the relevant section’s content. On narrower screens, it simply wraps or scales with text size but stays anchored to the left edge of the text column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in the same position as the ToC and sections when a load failure occurs, effectively replacing them within the main content container. It is centered within the text area that normally holds the sections: on desktop, the message card aligns with the narrower text column (about 60–70% of container width) and is centered horizontally, with 48–64px of blank space above and below to keep it feeling light. The card itself can have 24–32px of internal padding and a simple icon plus short message stacked vertically, all center-aligned, without heavy borders. This placement ensures the user’s eye flows from the header, breadcrumb, title, and last-updated label straight down into the error message in a clean, uninterrupted column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in the normal flow directly beneath the last-updated label (and any intro text), pushing the ToC and sections out of view or hiding them while the error is active. It scrolls like regular content if additional debug or retry controls are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state follows the same spatial logic as the error state, occupying the main content column where the ToC and sections would normally appear when there is no policy content to display. It is positioned within the centered text column, aligned horizontally with the title above, and begins about 48px beneath the last-updated label to maintain consistent section spacing. The empty-state message is concise and center-aligned within a lightly styled container or simply within the white background, with 24–32px of vertical padding and ample whitespace around it so it feels intentional and aligned with the minimal aesthetic. If a call-to-action (such as a reload link or contact suggestion) is present, it appears directly below the message with 16–24px of spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the ToC and sections in the normal flow while active, sitting underneath the title and last-updated label. It scrolls normally and does not overlay other sections; when content becomes available, it is removed and the standard ToC and sections layout reappears.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays dynamically loaded website terms and conditions, with section navigation, deep-linking, and cross-reference to the Privacy Policy.

core_features: Fetch and render the latest Terms of Use content (last updated date and sections) using getTermsOfUseContent.
Display a structured list of sections with in-page navigation (table of contents) generated from the fetched sections.
Support deep-linking to specific sections via URL parameters and synchronize section navigation state with the URL.
Provide client-side search/filtering within the Terms of Use content (no additional APIs) to help users quickly locate clauses.
Expose a clearly visible cross-reference link to the Privacy Policy page (privacy.html).
Handle loading and error states when retrieving Terms of Use content, with options to retry.
user_workflows:
View full Terms of Use:
1) User navigates to terms.html from the footer or a direct link.
2) On page load, the page calls getTermsOfUseContent.
3) While loading, a loading state is shown.
4) After successful response, the last updated date and all sections are rendered in order.
5) User scrolls through the content to read the terms.

Navigate directly to a specific section via URL:
1) User opens a link such as terms.html?sectionId={section-id}.
2) On page load, the page calls getTermsOfUseContent.
3) After content loads, the page looks up the section with id matching the sectionId parameter.
4) If found, the view scrolls to that section and visually emphasizes it.
5) The corresponding entry in the table of contents is also highlighted as active.

Navigate between sections using the table of contents:
1) User views the section list generated from getTermsOfUseContent.sections.
2) User clicks a section entry.
3) The click updates the URL to terms.html?sectionId={clicked-section-id} (using history.pushState or similar without page reload).
4) The page scrolls to the selected section and updates the active state in the table of contents.
5) If the user refreshes the page, the same section is scrolled to again based on the sectionId parameter.

Search within terms content:
1) User enters a query into the in-page search field.
2) On input change or form submit, the page runs a client-side filter across the in-memory sections returned by getTermsOfUseContent.
3) Sections whose title or body match the query are either highlighted, or non-matching sections are de-emphasized/hidden according to the functional design.
4) The URL is updated to include search={encoded-query} so that the state can be restored on reload.
5) If the user clears the search field, the URL search parameter is removed and the full content view is restored.

Follow the Privacy Policy cross-reference:
1) While reading the terms, the user sees a cross-reference link labeled according to the site’s content strategy that points to the Privacy Policy.
2) On click, the browser navigates to privacy.html.
3) No additional data is fetched on the Terms of Use page for this action.
interactions: On page load of terms.html, trigger a call to getTermsOfUseContent and render loading or error states based on the response.
On successful getTermsOfUseContent response, populate the last updated display and render each section in order with stable anchors based on section.id.
On detection of a URL parameter sectionId, after content is rendered, scroll the viewport to the element bound to that section.id and mark it as active in the table of contents.
On detection of a URL parameter search, pre-fill the in-page search field with its value and apply client-side search filtering/highlighting to the sections.
On click of an entry in the table of contents, update the URL’s sectionId parameter to the clicked section’s id (without reloading), scroll to the section, and update active styling.
On input change or submit in the in-page search form, update the URL’s search parameter and re-run client-side filtering/highlighting of sections using the in-memory content.
On click of the Privacy Policy link within the terms content or footer area of this page, navigate to privacy.html.
On error from getTermsOfUseContent, display a non-blocking error message and a retry control; on click of retry, invoke getTermsOfUseContent again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Terms of Use page relies on URL query parameters to manage view state without localStorage. Supported parameters include: 1) sectionId: terms.html?sectionId={section-id}. When present, after getTermsOfUseContent has loaded and all sections are rendered, the page attempts to find a section whose id matches sectionId. If found, it programmatically scrolls to that section and sets it as active in navigation; if not found, it leaves the default top-of-page view. Clicking a section in the table of contents updates this parameter via history API, so the selected section is encoded in the URL and survives reload or sharing. 2) search: terms.html?search={encoded-query}. When present, the page initializes the search input with this value and runs a client-side filter or highlight routine over the fetched sections. Any change in the search input updates the search parameter in the URL, again via the history API, enabling restore-on-reload and shareable filtered views. Removing the search (clearing the field) removes the parameter from the URL and restores the unfiltered view. The page does not modify server-side data or rely on any stored state other than what is derived from the current URL and the latest response from getTermsOfUseContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getTermsOfUseContent, manages loading and error states, and provides the fetched last_updated and sections data to child components.
Displays the static page title 'Terms of Use' and the dynamic 'last updated' information from getTermsOfUseContent.

Renders a table of contents using the list of sections, allows users to navigate to a specific section, and visually indicates the active section based on the URL sectionId parameter and current scroll position.

Renders the full terms content by iterating over sections from getTermsOfUseContent and creating anchorable section blocks using section.id and section.title, with section.body displayed as legal text.

Watches for the sectionId parameter in the URL. After sections are rendered, it locates the corresponding section element and scrolls it into view, updating internal active-section state used by the table of contents and any section highlighting.
Provides an in-page text input for searching within the Terms of Use. It updates the search query parameter in the URL and triggers client-side filtering/highlighting logic in the content viewer.
Reads the search parameter from the URL and applies client-side filter or highlight rules to the in-memory sections, affecting which sections or fragments are emphasized in the terms-section-viewer.
Provides a navigational link from the Terms of Use page to the Privacy Policy at privacy.html, typically placed near clauses that reference privacy or data protection.
Displays an error message and a retry control when getTermsOfUseContent fails. The retry control triggers a new invocation of getTermsOfUseContent via the terms-content-loader.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1140–1200px) within a full-width background.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same stacked, full-width section structure as the mockup: dark double header (info bar + main nav) at the top, a light breadcrumb strip below, then a wide white main content area with a centered max-width container. Inside this container: first the terms page header (title + intro, centered), then a search area, then a two-column layout where a left sidebar lists sections (TOC) and the right main column shows the active terms section. Global behaviors like deeplink handling and loading states sit on top of or inside this main content column without breaking the linear top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same stacked, full-width section structure as the mockup: dark double header (info bar + main nav) at the top, a light breadcrumb strip below, then a wide white main content area with a centered max-width container. Inside this container: first the terms page header (title + intro, centered), then a search area, then a two-column layout where a left sidebar lists sections (TOC) and the right main column shows the active terms section. Global behaviors like deeplink handling and loading states sit on top of or inside this main content column without breaking the linear top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the double header occupying roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport, with a shallow breadcrumb strip beneath it. In the main content, give the terms header block generous vertical padding (around 48–64px top and bottom). Place the search form with ample spacing above and below (about 32–48px). Below that, let the two-column content zone consume the remaining height. Within that zone, allocate roughly 25–30% of the inner container width to the TOC sidebar and 70–75% to the section viewer, with a clear gutter between them. Maintain generous internal padding (16–24px) in all interactive/text blocks, and preserve 32–48px vertical spacing between major subsections in the long-form terms text.
layout_strategies.content_density: Match the mockup’s low-density, airy feel: limit the number of elements competing in any horizontal band, use comfortable line lengths for text, and maintain generous whitespace between blocks. The terms header and search form each sit in their own vertical bands. The TOC presents a simple vertical list with plenty of breathing room between items and no cramped dividers. The section viewer uses clear headings with sizeable spacing above, and paragraphs that are easy to scan. Error notices and system states are concise bars with enough padding to feel deliberate, not crammed in.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top to bottom, then left to right within the main content. The user first processes the global header and navigation, then the breadcrumb, then lands on the centered 'Terms of Use' header. Their attention then moves down to the search form, which offers an immediate way to find information. Below that, the gaze moves to the left TOC column to understand the structure, then flows across to the right into the detailed terms content. Within the content, highlighted links like the Privacy Policy use the neon-lime accent to create subtle focal points. A loader or error notice temporarily pulls attention to the middle of the content area without disrupting the overall vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark, neon-accented corporate header and the slim light breadcrumb bar, the page opens into a spacious white canvas with a centered content column. At the top of this column, a large, center-aligned 'Terms of Use' title and a short explanatory paragraph sit with generous vertical padding, echoing the clean, minimal hero block from the blog mockup. Immediately beneath, a wide but not full-width search bar is aligned within the same central column, surrounded by ample whitespace so it feels prominent yet uncluttered. Further down, the layout resolves into a calm two-column composition: on the left, a slim, sticky sidebar lists the terms sections; on the right, a broader content panel presents the active section’s text with generous margins and clear section breaks. The left sidebar and right content area share a consistent vertical rhythm, with headings, paragraphs, and spacing tailored for long-form reading. Error messages, loading states, and deeplink behaviors all play out inside this main content band without disturbing the overall vertical flow. The entire structure sits on the same centered grid and breathing-space system as the original mockup, preserving the modern, corporate-yet-playful aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader occupies the main terms content zone within the white background area, directly overlaying both the TOC sidebar and the section viewer when active. It is anchored to the inner max-width container, spanning 100% of that container’s width and extending vertically from just below the search form down to the bottom edge of the visible section viewer. A semi-transparent light overlay subtly dims the underlying TOC and content, while a centered loading indicator (spinner or animated icon) sits approximately in the middle of the right content column, slightly above the vertical center of the viewport to align with the main reading area. Generous empty space surrounds the loader indicator, in keeping with the low-density design, so the state feels calm and deliberate rather than busy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the main content container: it sits on top of the TOC sidebar and section viewer when active, blocking interaction with them but not affecting the layout flow when hidden. It scrolls with the page, always covering the relevant content area rather than being fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms header occupies the top portion of the white main content area, directly beneath the light breadcrumb strip. It sits inside the centered max-width container, spanning roughly 70–80% of the container width and centered horizontally, leaving comfortable left and right margins. The main title 'Terms of Use' is center-aligned, large, and spaced away from the text above (breadcrumb) by around 48–64px of vertical padding. Beneath the title, a short, center-aligned introductory paragraph spans a slightly narrower text width, with about 16–24px of space between title and paragraph. The entire header block has generous top and bottom padding (around 48–64px total) to visually separate it from both the breadcrumb above and the search form below, mirroring the airy hero area in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section in the normal document flow; it scrolls away as the user moves down, with no sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The TOC sidebar is placed within the main content container below the search form, forming the left column of a two-column layout. On desktop, it occupies approximately 25–30% of the container width, aligned flush with the container’s left edge, with a clear vertical alignment to the top of the section viewer on the right. Its inner content area has 16–24px padding on all sides. The list of section titles is arranged in a simple vertical stack, each item taking the full sidebar width with about 12–16px vertical spacing between items. Active or hovered items use the neon-lime accent (for text or a vertical indicator bar) to visually tie back to the brand accents from the dark header. The sidebar extends down the page as needed for long lists, but its content is visually light and not crammed, maintaining ample whitespace below the last item. On narrower screens (tablet and below), the TOC collapses to a full-width block placed above the section viewer, still with generous top and bottom margins to keep the layout breathable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the TOC sidebar is sticky relative to the viewport: once scrolled into view, it remains fixed along the left side of the main content column, with its top edge offset slightly below the bottom of the dark header (about 24–32px) so it never overlaps global navigation. It scrolls normally on smaller screens where sticky behavior might be disabled for usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section viewer forms the main right-hand column of the two-column layout, starting at the same vertical line as the TOC sidebar, just below the search form. On desktop, it occupies roughly 70–75% of the inner container width and sits to the right of the TOC sidebar with a clear gutter of about 32px between them, ensuring the layout feels open rather than cramped. The section viewer’s content is left-aligned within this column, with 24–32px padding on its left and right to keep line lengths comfortable. Each top-level terms section starts with a bold heading, separated from the previous section by 32–48px of vertical space, followed by well-spaced paragraphs (about 16–24px between paragraphs). Subheadings and bullet lists are spaced slightly tighter but still maintain the airy character. When a TOC item is clicked or a deeplink is used, the corresponding section heading within this viewer aligns just below the bottom of the sticky global header and TOC offset, ensuring the heading is fully visible and not hidden behind any fixed elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard, scrollable content area that flows in the normal document layout. It scrolls vertically with the page; it does not have its own internal scrollbars on desktop. Its anchor positions are coordinated with the deeplink handler so that in-page links scroll precisely to appropriate spots below the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deeplink handler is non-visual and occupies no explicit space. Conceptually, it is attached to the section viewer area, monitoring URL hash changes and mapping them to specific section headings or anchors within the terms text. Its presence is felt in the way sections smoothly scroll into view and are correctly aligned relative to the sticky global header and TOC when users arrive via external links or refresh with a hash in the URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-rendering; it does not affect layout or spacing. Functionally, it controls scroll positioning within the section viewer and may apply a brief highlight effect to the targeted section, but this does not change the structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits within the main content container, directly beneath the terms header block and above the two-column layout. It is horizontally centered within the container but slightly biased visually toward the right by aligning its left edge roughly with the left edge of the section viewer column. On desktop, the search bar spans about 50–60% of the container’s width, leaving wide margins on either side for a clean, minimal feel. The input field is a single-line control with 16–24px internal padding, and a search icon is aligned on the left or right, echoing the subtle neon accent from the header (for the icon or focus state). There is roughly 32–48px of vertical space above the search form (separating it from the header) and about 32px below it (separating it from the TOC/section viewer zone), reinforcing the low-density layout. On smaller screens, the search form expands to nearly full width of the container while keeping the same generous vertical margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the normal flow, scrolling away as the user moves down the page. It does not stick to the top, but its filter effects are managed behind the scenes by the search filter controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search filter controller is non-visual and conceptually lives alongside the search form and section viewer. It listens to input in the search form and updates which sections or text ranges are emphasized within the section viewer. Its influence is seen in the way matching terms may be highlighted or non-matching sections subtly de-emphasized, but it does not manifest as any standalone UI element or take up physical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-rendering; it does not alter the spatial layout. It only changes visual states (e.g., highlights, filters) inside existing components such as the TOC and section viewer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Privacy Policy link appears within the main terms content, near the top of the section viewer. It is embedded in a short, left-aligned informational line or paragraph that explains the relationship between the Terms of Use and the Privacy Policy. This line is placed close to the beginning of the first section—typically just after the introductory text within the section viewer—with about 16–24px of vertical space above and below, so it is easy to spot without disrupting the reading flow. The link itself is styled in the brand’s neon-lime or cyan accent color, possibly underlined or slightly bolder than surrounding text, making it a clear but not overpowering focal point. It remains within the standard text width of the section viewer, respecting the same margins and line lengths as the rest of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the section viewer’s text content; it scrolls with the rest of the document and does not have any sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notice appears in the main content area, just below the terms header and above the search form, or directly above the section viewer when more context is needed. It spans nearly the full width of the inner content container, aligned with the header and section viewer edges, and presents as a horizontally oriented bar with 16–24px padding on all sides. The message text is left-aligned and concise, with a small icon on the left to indicate an error or warning. The background color is a soft alert tone that stands out against the white canvas but does not clash with the neon accents; the lime or cyan accent may be used for key words or an inline action link. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below the notice so it looks deliberate and not squeezed between components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element that appears in the normal flow, pushing the search form and content area downward when visible. It does not overlay content and scrolls away with the page as the user moves down.
